Source: evaluation technology from speaker identification to affective analysis : a multi-step system for analyzing childrens storie we propose a multi-step system for the analysis of childrens stories that is intended to be part of a larger text-to-speechbased storytelling system . a hybrid approach is adopted , where pattern-based and statistical methods are used along with utilization of external knowledge sources . this system performs the following story analysis tasks : identification of characters in each story ; attribution of quotes to specific story characters ; identification of character age , gender and other salient personality attributes ; and finally , affective analysis of the quoted material . the different types of analyses were evaluated using several datasets . for the quote attribution , as well as for the gender and age estimation , substantial improvement over baseline was realized , whereas results for personality attribute estimation and valence estimation are more modest .
human1: We’re building a multi-step system to analyze kids’ stories so it can plug into a bigger text to speech storytelling thing. The idea is pretty simple: we mix rule-based patterns with stats stuff, and we also use some outside knowlege. First, we figure out who the characters are in each storie. Then we try to match each quote to the right character (who said what). After that, we estimate basics like age and gender, plus a few personality bits. Finally, we look at how the quoted lines “feel” the affect/valance, like positive or negative vibes.We tested it on several datasets and saw solid improvment over the basic baselines for quote attribtution and for guessing gender/age. The personality traits and the sentiment part are harder, so the gains there are more modest, but still noticable. Overall, it kinda works pretty well, tho there’s alot left to polish.

acquisition of semantic classes for adjectives from distributional evidence
in this paper , we present a clustering experiment directed at the acquisition of semantic classes for adjectives in catalan , using only shallow distributional features . we define a broad-coverage classification for adjectives based on ontological semantics . we classify along two parameters ( number of arguments and ontological kind of denotation ) , achieving reliable agreement results among human judges . the clustering procedure achieves a comparable agreement score for one of the parameters , and a little lower for the other .
human1:
We’re trying to group adjectivs into meaningful buckets for Catalan, but doing it the easy way: just using shallow distro features (basically, what words show up around them) instead of heavy, deep parsing stuff. First we made a broad label set based on ontologial semantics so it actually covers lots of cases, not just toy examples.
Then we sort each adjective along two knobs: (1) how many arguements it tends to take, and (2) what kind of thing it denoates (its ontologial kind). Humans could agree on these labels pretty reliably not perfect, but good enuf. Our clustering method gets about the same agreeemnt for one knob, and a bit lower for the other, so it’s kinda close to people, just not fully there yet. Overall, simple signals go a long way, even if the second part still needs some polishng.

user-driven development of the sonar corpus interfaces
opensonar is an online system that allows for analyzing and searching the large scale dutch reference corpus sonar . due to the size of the corpus , accessing the information contained in the dataset has proven to be difficult for less technically inclined researchers . the opensonar project aims to facilitate the use of the sonar corpus by providing a user-friendly online interface . to make sure that the resulting system is practically useful , several user groups have been identified , who drive the interface development process by providing practical use cases . the current system is already used in educational and research settings .
human1:
We built OpenSonar, an online tool for analysing and searching the big Dutch reference corpus (SoNaR). The corpus is massive, which made it kinda hard for less techy reseachers to get at the good stuff. So the goal here is a friendlier intreface that lets people browse, search, and explore without needing command-line foo.
To make sure it’s actually useful (not just fancy buttons), we lined up a few user groups teachers, students, corpus folks, and other lang-people and asked them for practial use-cases. Their feedback basically drives what we build next: what views matter, which filters are needed, and how results should be shown.
The current system is alreadly in use in eductional settings and reseach projects. It’s not perfect yet, but the user-driven bits keep nudging the UI in the right direction, so more people can work with the SoNaR data without jumping thru tech hoops.

the benefit of stochastic pp attachment to a rule-based parser
to study pp attachment disambiguation as a benchmark for empirical methods in natural language processing it has often been reduced to a binary decision problem ( between verb or noun attachment ) in a particular syntactic configuration . a parser , however , must solve the more general task of deciding between more than two alternatives in many different contexts . we combine the attachment predictions made by a simple model of lexical attraction with a full-fledged parser of german to determine the actual benefit of the subtask to parsing . we show that the combination of data-driven and rule-based components can reduce the number of all parsing errors by 14 % and raise the attachment accuracy for dependency parsing of german to an unprecedented 92 % .
human1:
We looked at the old headache of PP attachment you know, deciding whether a prepositional phrase sticks to the verb or the noun. Lots of benchmarks make it a neat little binary thing (verb vs. noun) in one tidy setup, but a real praser has to juggle more than two choices across tons of messy contexts. So we mixed a super simple lexical attraaction model (it guesses where a PP “likes” to go) with a full-on Greman parser to see if this subtask actually helps the whole pipeline, not just the toy case.
Turns out the combo of data-driven bits + rule-based logic plays nice together: we cut overall parsing errors by about 14% and pushed PP-attachment accuracy for German dep parsing up to around 92%, which is kinda unprecedented for this setup. Not gonna lie, some edges are still rough (and a few decisions feel a bit hand-wavy), but the hybrid setup does real work instead of just looking clever on paper. Pretty solid win, even if it’s not perfct.

a cohesion graph based approach for unsupervised recognition of literal and non-literal use of multiword expressions
we present a graph-based model for representing the lexical cohesion of a discourse . in the graph structure , vertices correspond to the content words of a text and edges connecting pairs of words encode how closely the words are related semantically . we show that such a structure can be used to distinguish literal and non-literal usages of multi-word expressions .
human1: We use a simple graph-y trick to model how words in a text stick together aka lexical coehsion. Each important word becomes a vertex, and we draw edeges between pairs that are sematic-ally close or show up together alot; stronger links mean tighter relatedness. With this unsupervized setup, the graph helps us tell when a multi-word expression is being used literal vs non-literal. So if a phrase like “spill the beans” is surrounded by talk about secrets/gossip, the graph nudges it toward figurative; if it’s near pots, kitchens, and actual beans, it leans literal. It’s pretty lightweight (no fancy labels or hand-coded rules), kinda robust across different texts, and while not perfct, it does a decent job spliting the two uses without much fuss.

alternative phrases and natural language information division of informatics
this paper presents a formal analysis for a large class of words called alternative markers , which includes other ( than ) , such ( as ) , and besides . these words appear frequently enough in dialog to warrant serious attention , yet present natural language search engines perform poorly on queries containing them . i show that the performance of a search engine can be improved dramatically by incorporating an approximation of the formal analysis that is compatible with the search engines operational semantics . the value of this approach is that as the operational semantics of natural language applications improve , even larger improvements are possible .
human1: We look at a big family of words I call “alternative markers” things like “other (than)”, “such (as)”, and “besides.” These show up freqently in everyday talk and search queries, but most natural-language search engins kinda stumble when those words are present. In the paper, I give a formal analaysis of how these markers work basically how they divide up info in a sentence and why they confuse search. The trick is: you don’t need the full heavy math to get wins. If you plug a light-weight aproximation of that analysis into a search engine in a way that matches the engine’s operatoinal semantcs, results get way better on those tricky queries. Even nicer, as the application’s semantics improve over time, this same approach scales up—yielding even bigger improvments without tons of extra hackery. Bottom line: handle “other than,” “such as,” and “besides” a bit smarter → fewer dumb misses and more on-point hits. Not perfct, but def a solid step.

a markov logic approach to bio-molecular event extraction
in this paper we describe our entry to the bionlp 2009 shared task regarding biomolecular event extraction . our work can be described by three design decisions : ( 1 ) instead of building a pipeline using local classifier technology , we design and learn a joint probabilistic model over events in a sentence ; ( 2 ) instead of developing specific inference and learning algorithms for our joint model , we apply markov logic , a general purpose statistical relation learning language , for this task ; ( 3 ) we represent events as relational structures over the tokens of a sentence , as opposed to structures that explicitly mention abstract event entities . our results are competitive : we achieve the 4th best scores for task 1 ( in close range to the 3rd place ) and the best results for task 2 with a 13 percent point margin .
human1: We joined the BioNLP’09 shared task on bio-molecular event extraction and went with a pretty simple-but-bold idea: instead of a pipeline of local clasifiers, we train one joint probablistic model that looks at all the events in a sentense together. Rather than cooking up special infernce or learining code, we just used Markov Logic a general-purpose statistical relational thing so we could model constraints + probabilities in one place. Also, we represent events as relations over the tokns (who’s connected to who in the sentence) instead of inventing extra “event” objects floating around. How’d it do? Pretty decent tbh: we got 4th on Task 1 (basically neck-and-neck with 3rd), and best on Task 2 with about a 13 percentage-point margin. Not perfct, but the joint + Markov-logic combo clearly helped, and we didn’t need funky custom algos to get there.

Our approach is based on three design choices: rather than building a pipeline of local classifiers, we learn a joint probabilistic model that captures interactions among all events in a sentence; instead of developing task-specific inference and learning procedures, we adopt Markov Logic as a general statistical relational framework for modeling these dependencies; and we encode events as relational structures over sentence tokens, without introducing additional abstract event nodes. The system ranked 4th in the shared task evaluation, closest to the 3rdranked system, for Task 1, and it achieved the highest score for Task 2 with a margin of about 13 percentage points. confidence measure for word alignment
in this paper we present a confidence measure for word alignment based on the posterior probability of alignment links . we introduce sentence alignment confidence measure and alignment link confidence measure . based on these measures , we improve the alignment quality by selecting high confidence sentence alignments and alignment links from multiple word alignments of the same sentence pair . additionally , we remove low confidence alignment links from the word alignment of a bilingual training corpus , which increases the alignment f-score , improves chinese-english and arabic-english translation quality and significantly reduces the phrase translation table size .
human1: We propose a simple confidence score for word alignment that looks at the posterior probablity of each link between words. There are two pieces: a sentence-level confidence (how sure we are the whole sentence pair is aligned well) and a link-level confidence (how sure we are about each word↔word link). Using these, we take multiple alignments for the same sentense pair and keep the high-confidence bits, tossing the sketchy ones. We also prune low-confidence links from the big bilingal training data. Net result: higher alignment F-score, better Chinese↔English and Arabic↔English tranlsation quality, and a way smaller phrase table (less bloat, faster lookups). It’s not perfct and some edges are a lil fuzzy, but picking the solid links and ditching the weak ones gave us a pretty nice improvment overall.

It defines a sentence-level score that indicates how reliable an alignment is for a particular sentence pair, as well as a link-level score that measures the likelihood of each word-to-word connection. Based on these measures, we combine multiple alignments for the same sentence pair by filtering for highconfidence sentence alignments and high-confidence links. We also apply link-level pruning to bilingual training data to remove low-confidence connections. These result in higher alignment F-scores, improved Chinese–English and Arabic–English translation performance under standard evaluation conditions, and significantly smaller phrase translation tables. These are limited by the quality of posterior estimates and may be sensitive to alignment sparsity.

an optimal-time binarization algorithm for linear context-free rewriting systems with fan-out two
linear context-free rewriting systems ( lcfrss ) are grammar formalisms with the capability of modeling discontinuous constituents . many applications use lcfrss where the fan-out ( a measure of the discontinuity of phrases ) is not allowed to be greater than 2. we present an efficient algorithm for transforming lcfrs with fan-out at most 2 into a binary form , whenever this is possible . this results in asymptotical run-time improvement for known parsing algorithms for this class .
human1: We look at LCFRS a grammer formalism that can handle discontinous bits in a sentence. In lots of apps people keep the fan-out (how many separate pieces a phrase can split into) to no more than 2. We give an optmal-time algorithim for turning any LCFRS with fan-out ≤ 2 into a binary form (rules combine things two-at-a-time), whenever that’s actually possible.Why bother? Binarizing shrinks the branching, so known parsing methods for this class run faster asymptoticaly. In plain words: same language power, fewer wide rules, better run-time. The approach is pretty efficent, and doesn’t require fancy extra tricks just the right transformations. Bottom line: if your LCFRS sticks to fan-out two, we can binari-ize it in optimal time and your parser gets a speed bump, in theory and usually in pratice too.

Human 2: LCFRSs can describe discontinuous constituents and many practical grammars limit their fan-out-the number of separate spans a constituent may occupy-to at most two. We present an optimal-time algorithm which converts any LCFRS with fan-out ≤ 2 into binary form, if that is possible. Binarization reduces rule arity, which in turn improves the asymptotic running time of known parsing algorithms for this grammar class. Our analysis settles the theoretical conditions under which binarization is possible and makes clear that the approach cannot be generalized to grammars with higher fan-out.

hierarchical discriminative classification for text-based geolocation
text-based document geolocation is commonly rooted in language-based information retrieval techniques over geodesic grids . these methods ignore the natural hierarchy of cells in such grids and fall afoul of independence assumptions . we demonstrate the effectiveness of using logistic regression models on a hierarchy of nodes in the grid , which improves upon the state of the art accuracy by several percent and reduces mean error distances by hundreds of kilometers on data from twitter , wikipedia , and flickr . we also show that logistic regression performs feature selection effectively , assigning high weights to geocentric terms .
human1:
We’re trying to guess where a piece of text comes from on the map (text-based geo-location). A lot of older methods chop the world into a geodesic grid and do IR-style matching, but they ignore the natural heirarchy of cells (big areas → smaller ones) and kinda assume cells are independent (they’re not, lol).
Our fix: use a hierarchial, coarse-to-fine stack of logisitc regression models over the grid’s nodes pick a broad region first, then zoom into finer cells. This simple discrimnative setup bumps accurracy by a few percent and cuts mean error distences by hundereds of kilometers on Twitter, Wikipedia, and Flickr. Bonus: logistic regression basically does feature selection for free, pushing high weights on geo-centric words (city names, local lingo, etc). Not magic, but a clean win with a pretty sane model, even if a few edges are still a bit rough.

Human 2: Text-based document geolocation predicts a document's location from its content. Prior work has often relied on information-retrieval methods over geodesic grids but treats grid cells as independent, without accounting for their inherent hierarchical structure. Instead, we use a discriminative, hierarchical model where logistic regression classifiers operate at successive levels of the grid: a document is first assigned to a coarse region and then routed to increasingly fine cells. This structure reduces independence errors and leverages features more effectively. Across datasets such as Twitter, Wikipedia, and Flickr, the hierarchical approach improves overall accuracy and lowers mean distance error, while the learned weights highlight strongly geocentric terms. We also note limitations, including reduced reliability for short texts and ambiguity involving overlapping place names.

a system to solve language tests for second grade students
this paper describes a system which solves language tests for second grade students ( 7 years old ) . in japan , there are materials for students to measure understanding of what they studied , just like sat for high school students in us . we use textbooks for the students as the target material of this study . questions in the materials are classified into four types : questions about chinese character ( kanji ) , about word knowledge , reading comprehension , and composition . this program doesnt resolve the composition and some other questions which are not easy to be implemented in text forms . we built a subsystem for each finer type of questions . as a result , we achieved 55 % - 83 % accuracy in answering questions in unseen materials .
human1: We built a simple system to help solve langauge tests for 2nd graders (about 7 y/o). In Japan kids get practice booklets to check what they learned kinda like tiny SATs for US highschoolers. We point our program at the same textboks they study from. The questions fall into four buckets: Kanji (Chinese charcters), word knowlege, reading comprehension, and composition. Our program doesnt really handle composition (and a few other super free-form ones), so we skip those for now. For the rest we made a small subsytem for each type so it can focus better. The standard textbook materials and assessment booklets feature four main types of questions: the recognition of Kanji, items testing word knowledge, reading-comprehension questions, and composition. classifying japanese polysemous verbs based on fuzzy c-means
this paper presents a method for classifying japanese polysemous verbs using an algorithm to identify overlapping nodes with more than one cluster . the algorithm is a graph-based unsupervised clustering algorithm , which combines a generalized modularity function , spectral mapping , and fuzzy clustering technique . the modularity function for measuring cluster structure is calculated based on the frequency distributions over verb frames with selectional preferences . evaluations are made on two sets of verbs including polysemies .
human1: We’re grouping Japanese verbs with many meanings (polysemous verbs), but we don’t force a verb into just one bucket some meanings overlap, so a word can live in more than one clustr. Our algorithim is graph-based and unsupervized: we build a graph from how verbs show up in verb frames (incl. their selectional preferneces), then use a mix of spectral mapping  a generalized modulairty score to see where the structure is, and finally fuzzy c-means  so memberships can be soft instead of all-or-nothing. The modulairty is computed from the frequncy patterns over those frames, which tells us how tightly verbs hang together. We ran evals on two verb sets (with lots of polyesmies) and the method sorts them pretty well showing overlapping senses are real and the fuzzy approach catches them, even if a few edges are still a lil messy.

Source:
challenges for extracting biomedical knowledge from full text
at present , most biomedical information retrieval and extraction tools process abstracts rather than full-text articles . the increasing availability of full text will allow more knowledge to be extracted with greater reliability . to investigate the challenges of full-text processing , we manually annotated a corpus of cited articles from a molecular interaction map ( kohn , 1999 ) . our analysis demonstrates the necessity of full-text processing ; identifies the article sections where interactions are most commonly stated ; and quantifies both the amount of external knowledge required and the proportion of interactions requiring multiple or deeper inference steps . further , it identifies a range of nlp tools required , including : identifying synonyms , and resolving coreference and negated expressions . this is important guidance for researchers engineering biomedical text processing systems .
human1: Right now most bio-medical text tools read abstracts, not the whole paper. As more full-text becomes availablity, we can pull out more facts with better reliablity. To see what’s tricky, we hand-annotated a bunch of papers cited by a molecular interaction map (Kohn, 1999) and analysed what’s really going on. Turns out full text is kinda necessary: we identifed which secitons most often state the actual interactions, how much outside/backgroud knowledge you need, and how often you must do multi-step or deeper inferencing (not just one hop).Also, you need a decent NLP toolbox: spot synonmys, resolve co-reference (who “it/they” refers to), and handle negated lines so you don’t record things that aren’t true. Bottom line: if you’re building biomed text processing, don’t stop at abstracts go full text, or you’ll miss alot, tbh.

"challenges for extracting biomedical knowledge from full text,
While currently most biomedical information extraction and text mining systems focus on abstracts, more comprehensive knowledge extraction will become possible in view of the increasing availability of full-text content. In order to investigate the challenges introduced by the processing of full text, we prepared a corpus through manual annotation of cited articles involved in a molecular interaction map (Kohn, 1999). Our analysis underlines the importance of text processing, shows which sections contain the largest number of molecular interactions, quantifies both external knowledge required and the proportion of interactions requiring multi-step or deeper inference, and outlines the basic NLP tools needed for effective extraction: synonym detection, coreference resolution, and handling negated expressions. These findings provide the necessary guidance for researchers developing biomedical text processing systems.

a flexemic tagset for polish
the article notes certain weaknesses of current efforts aiming at the standardization of pos tagsets for morphologically rich languages and argues that , in order to achieve clear mappings between tagsets , it is necessary to have clear and formal rules of delimiting poss and grammatical categories within any given tagset . an attempt at constructing such a tagset for polish is presented .
human1: We point out some problems with the current standarization of POS tagsets for morphologically rich langauges. A lot of tags kinda mush together part-of-speech and grammer features (case, gender, etc.), so mapping one tagset to another becomes messy. Our take: if you want clean mappings, you need clear, formal rules that say where POS ends and where grammatical cateogries begin otherwise it’s apples vs. oranges. So we sketch a flexemic tagset for Polsih that tries to draw those lines crisply. The idea is to keep POS labels lean and push inflectional stuff into well-defined feature slots, with rules that are explicit (not hand-wavy). With that, cross-walking between tagsets gets a lot less painful, even if a few edges still feel a bit fuzzy and will need more polish (no pun intennded).

Support vector machines for paraphrase identification and corpus construction
The lack of large aligned monolingual sentence corpora restricts the development of paraphrase models based on statistical machine translation. In this paper, we show how annotated datasets and Support Vector Machines can be used for generating extended monolingual paraphrase corpora from comparable news clusters available on the web. Our approach relies on features such as morphological variants, WordNet synonyms and hypernyms, log-likelihood word pairings derived from baseline sentence alignments, and formal string metrics like word-based edit distance. This approach significantly reduces the alignment error rate in the corpora extracted compared to heuristic methods that rely on sentence position within the text.

dale : a word sense disambiguation system for biomedical documents
automatic interpretation of documents is hampered by the fact that language contains terms which have multiple meanings . these ambiguities can still be found when language is restricted to a particular domain , such as biomedicine . word sense disambiguation ( wsd ) systems attempt to resolve these ambiguities but are often only able to identify the meanings for a small set of ambiguous terms . dale ( disambiguation using automatically labeled examples ) is a supervised wsd system that can disambiguate a wide range of ambiguities found in biomedical documents . dale uses the umls metathesaurus as both a sense inventory and as a source of information for automatically generating labeled training examples . dale is able to disambiguate biomedical documents with the coverage of unsupervised approaches and accuracy of supervised methods .
human1: The DALE system for word sense disambiguation in biomedical texts is the subject of this research. Many words in papers can have many meanings, and the biomedical field is no exception. DALE can handle more terms than the typical WSD systems. It is supervised and automatically generates training examples and the sense list using the UMLS metathesaurus. The system's coverage is similar to that of unsupervised systems, but its accuracy is closer to that of supervised ones.

Human 2:
"DALE : A Word Sense Disambiguation System for Biomedical Documents
Terms with several meanings make automatic interpretation of biomedical documents difficult. This problem persists even for specialized domains such as biomedicine. The goal of a Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) system is to remove such ambiguities, but most WSD systems work only with a very limited number of ambiguous terms. DALE is a supervised WSD system that has been designed and developed to handle a wide range of ambiguities in biomedical texts. It takes the UMLS Metathesaurus both as a sense inventory and as a source for generating automatically labeled training instances. DALE combines the wide coverage typically seen in unsupervised methods with the high accuracy characteristic of supervised approaches and thus disambiguates biomedical documents effectively.

evaluating a crosslinguistic grammar resource : a case study of wambaya
this paper evaluates the lingo grammar matrix , a cross-linguistic resource for the development of precision broad coverage grammars , by applying it to the australian language wambaya . despite large typological differences between wambaya and the languages on which the development of the resource was based , the grammar matrix is found to provide a significant jump-start in the creation of the grammar for wambaya : with less than 5.5 person-weeks of development , the wambaya grammar was able to assign correct semantic representations to 76 % of the sentences in a naturally occurring text . while the work on wambaya identified some areas of refinement for the grammar matrix , 59 % of the matrix-provided types were invoked in the final wambaya grammar , and only 4 % of the matrix-provided types required modification

Despite significant typological divergence between Wambaya and the languages that informed the development of the resource, the Grammar Matrix provides a substantial head start on creating the Wambaya grammar: in less than 5.5 person-weeks of development, the grammar assigns correct semantic representations to 76% of sentences of a naturally occurring text. the benefit of stochastic pp attachment to a rule-based parser
to study pp attachment disambiguation as a benchmark for empirical methods in natural language processing it has often been reduced to a binary decision problem ( between verb or noun attachment ) in a particular syntactic configuration . a parser , however , must solve the more general task of deciding between more than two alternatives in many different contexts . we combine the attachment predictions made by a simple model of lexical attraction with a full-fledged parser of german to determine the actual benefit of the subtask to parsing . we show that the combination of data-driven and rule-based components can reduce the number of all parsing errors by 14 % and raise the attachment accuracy for dependency parsing of german to an unprecedented 92 % .

combining prediction by partial matching and logistic regression for thai word segmentation
word segmentation is an important part of many applications , including information retrieval , information filtering , document analysis , and text summarization . in thai language , the process is complicated since words are written continuously , and their structures are not well-defined . a recognized effective approach to word segmentation is longest matching , a method based on dictionary . nevertheless , this method suffers from character-level and syllable-level ambiguities in determining word boundaries . this paper proposes a technique to thai word segmentation using a two-step approach . first , text is segmented , using an application of prediction by partial matching , into syllables whose structures are more well-defined . this reduces the earlier type of ambiguity . then , the syllables are combined into words by an application of a syllable-level longest matching method together with a logistic regression model which takes into account contextual information . the experimental results show the syllable segmentation accuracy of more than 96.65 % and the overall word segmentation accuracy of 97 % .
human1: The intricacies of Thai word segmentation a critical task for numerous NLP applications are discussed in this paper. This task is hampered by continuous writing and vague word structures. Despite being widely used, dictionary-based longest matching has trouble with ambiguities at the character and syllable levels. The authors suggest a two-step method. In order to reduce initial ambiguities, it first uses Prediction by Partial Matching (PPM) to segment text into more distinct syllables. Second, a logistic regression model that takes contextual information into account is combined with a syllable-level longest matching technique to combine these syllables into words. The experimental results show high accuracy, with an overall word segmentation accuracy of 97% and a syllable segmentation accuracy of over 96.65%.

Human 2: Word segmentation plays a crucial role in tasks such as information retrieval, filtering, document processing, and text summarization. In Thai, this task is challenging because words are written without spaces and lack clear structural boundaries. The commonly used dictionary-based longest-matching approach is effective but often fails when dealing with character- and syllable-level ambiguities. This paper introduces a two-stage method for Thai word segmentation. In the first stage, Prediction by Partial Matching (PPM) is applied to divide the text into syllables, which have more consistent structural properties and therefore reduce initial ambiguity. In the second stage, these syllables are grouped into full words using a combination of syllable-level longest matching and a logistic regression model that incorporates contextual cues. Experiments show that the system achieves over 96.65% accuracy at the syllable level and around 97% accuracy for complete word segmentation.

exploiting structured ontology to organize scattered online opinions
we study the problem of integrating scattered online opinions . for this purpose , we propose to exploit structured ontology to obtain well-formed relevant aspects to a topic and use them to organize scattered opinions to generate a structured summary . particularly , we focus on two main challenges in implementing this idea , ( 1 ) how to select the most useful aspects from a large number of aspects in the ontology and ( 2 ) how to order the selected aspects to optimize the readability of the structured summary . we propose and explore several methods for solving these challenges . experimental results on two different data sets ( us presidents and digital cameras ) show that the proposed methods are effective for selecting aspects that can represent the major opinions and for generating coherent ordering of aspects .

The authors explore and suggest a number of approaches to address these problems. Results from experiments on datasets related to "US Presidents" and "Digital Cameras" show how well their techniques work at choosing elements that reflect key viewpoints and creating a logical hierarchy for them.

extracting opinions , opinion holders , and topics expressed in online news media text
this paper presents a method for identifying an opinion with its holder and topic , given a sentence from online news media texts . we introduce an approach of exploiting the semantic structure of a sentence , anchored to an opinion bearing verb or adjective . this method uses semantic role labeling as an intermediate step to label an opinion holder and topic using data from framenet . we decompose our task into three phases : identifying an opinion-bearing word , labeling semantic roles related to the word in the sentence , and then finding the holder and the topic of the opinion word among the labeled semantic roles . for a broader coverage , we also employ a clustering technique to predict the most probable frame for a word which is not defined in framenet . our experimental results show that our system performs significantly better than the baseline .
human1: In this paper, we propose a technique to extract opinions, their holders, and their topics from online news media sentences. The method makes use of a sentence's semantic structure, particularly when it comes to verbs or adjectives that express opinion. Using information from FrameNet, the technique employs Semantic Role Labelling (SRL) as a transitional step to determine the topic and opinion holder. There are three phases to the task: recognising the word that expresses an opinion; identifying the word's semantic functions in the sentence; identifying the topic and the person holding the opinion among these designated semantic roles. The system also uses a clustering technique to predict the most likely semantic frame for words that aren't explicitly defined in FrameNet in order to guarantee wider coverage. Results from experiments show that this system performs noticeably better than baseline techniques.

cluster stopping rules for word sense discrimination
as text data becomes plentiful , unsupervised methods for word sense disambiguation ( wsd ) become more viable . a problem encountered in applying wsd methods is finding the exact number of senses an ambiguity has in a training corpus collected in an automated manner . that number is not known a priori ; rather it needs to be determined based on the data itself . we address that problem using cluster stopping methods . such techniques have not previously applied to wsd . we implement the methods of calinski and harabasz ( 1975 ) and hartigan ( 1975 ) and our adaptation of the gap statistic ( tibshirani , walter and hastie , 2001 ) . for evaluation , we use the wsd test set from the national library of medicine , whose sense inventory is the unified medical language system . the best accuracy for selecting the correct number of clusters is 0.60 with the c & h method . our error analysis shows that the cluster stopping methods make finergrained sense distinctions by creating additional clusters . the highest f-scores ( 82.89 ) , indicative of the quality of cluster membership assignment , are comparable to the baseline majority sense ( 82.63 ) and point to a path towards accuracy improvement via additional cluster pruning . the importance and significance of the current work is in applying cluster stopping rules to wsd .
human1: The automatic determination of the correct number of senses for an ambiguous word in a given corpus is a major challenge in unsupervised Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) that is addressed in this paper. In order to address this, the authors employ a novel technique that has not been employed in WSD before: cluster stopping methods. They apply techniques by Hartigan, Calinski and Harabasz (C&H), and a modified version of the gap statistic. The C&H approach selected the right number of clusters with the best accuracy of 60% when tested on the National Library of Medicine's WSD test set (using UMLS as the sense inventory). Error analysis showed that by producing more clusters, these techniques typically produce finer-grained sense distinctions. Additional cluster pruning may be necessary to improve accuracy further, as the highest F-scores for cluster membership assignment (82.89%) were similar to the majority sense baseline (82.63%). The main contribution is the investigation and innovative use of cluster stopping rules for WSD.

A major challenge for these methods is determining how many senses a word actually exhibits in an automatically gathered training corpus, since this number is not known beforehand and must be inferred from the data. In this work, we explore the use of cluster-stopping criteria to address this issue an approach not previously applied to WSD. We experiment with the methods of Calinski & Harabasz (1975), Hartigan (1975), and a modified version of the Gap Statistic (Tibshirani, Walther & Hastie, 2001). For evaluation, we use the National Library of Medicine’s WSD dataset, which is based on the Unified Medical Language System. The highest accuracy for selecting the correct number of clusters is 0.60 using the C&H measure. Error analysis suggests that these techniques tend to split senses more finely by generating extra clusters. The best F-score (82.89), which reflects cluster assignment quality, is similar to the majority-sense baseline (82.63) and indicates that further improvements may be achieved through more aggressive cluster pruning. The main contribution of this study is demonstrating the usefulness of cluster-stopping rules for WSD.

combining optimal clustering and hidden markov models for extractive summarization
we propose hidden markov models with unsupervised training for extractive summarization . extractive summarization selects salient sentences from documents to be included in a summary . unsupervised clustering combined with heuristics is a popular approach because no annotated data is required . however , conventional clustering methods such as k-means do not take text cohesion into consideration . probabilistic methods are more rigorous and robust , but they usually require supervised training with annotated data . our method incorporates unsupervised training with clustering , into a probabilistic framework . clustering is done by modified k-means ( mkm ) -- a method that yields more optimal clusters than the conventional k-means method . text cohesion is modeled by the transition probabilities of an hmm , and term distribution is modeled by the emission probabilities . the final decoding process tags sentences in a text with theme class labels . parameter training is carried out by the segmental k-means ( skm ) algorithm . the output of our system can be used to extract salient sentences for summaries, or used for topic detection . content - based evaluation shows that our method outperforms an existing extractive summarizer by 22.8 % in terms of relative similarity, and outperforms a baseline summarizer that selects the top n sentences as salient sentences by 46.3 % .

Extractive summarization involves selecting the most important sentences from documents to form a summary. While unsupervised clustering with heuristics is widely used because it requires no labeled data, standard approaches like k-means fail to model textual cohesion. monads as a solution for generalized opacity
in this paper we discuss a conservative extension of the simply-typed lambda calculus in order to model a class of expressions that generalize the notion of opaque contexts . our extension is based on previous work in the semantics of programming languages aimed at providing a mathematical characterization of computations that produce some kind of side effect ( moggi , 1989 ) , and is based on the notion of monads , a construction in category theory that , intuitively , maps a collection of simple values and simple functions into a more complex value space , in a canonical way . the main advantages of our approach with respect to traditional analyses of opacity are the fact that we are able to explain in a uniform way a set of different but related phenomena , and that we do so in a principled way that has been shown to also explain other linguistic phenomena ( shan , 2001 ) .

we propose a spectral approach for unsupervised constituent parsing that comes with theoretical guarantees on latent structure recovery . our approach is grammarless we directly learn the bracketing structure of a given sentence without using a grammar model . the main algorithm is based on lifting the concept of additive tree metrics for structure learning of latent trees in the phylogenetic and machine learning communities to the case where the tree structure varies across examples . although finding the minimal latent tree is np-hard in general , for the case of projective trees we find that it can be found using bilexical parsing algorithms . empirically , our algorithm performs favorably compared to the constituent context model of klein and manning ( 2002 ) without the need for careful initialization .

Although determining the minimal latent tree is typically NP-hard, the authors demonstrate that bilexical parsing algorithms can be used to accomplish this for projective trees. Their suggested algorithm performs well empirically when compared to the Constituent Context Model (Klein and Manning, 2002), especially without the need for meticulous initialisation.

this paper presents a dependency language model ( dlm ) that captures linguistic constraints via a dependency structure , i.e. , a set of probabilistic dependencies that express the relations between headwords of each phrase in a sentence by an acyclic , planar , undirected graph . our contributions are three-fold . first , we incorporate the dependency structure into an n-gram language model to capture long distance word dependency . second , we present an unsupervised learning method that discovers the dependency structure of a sentence using a bootstrapping procedure . finally , we evaluate the proposed models on a realistic application ( japanese kana-kanji conversion ) . experiments show that the best dlm achieves an 11.3 % error rate reduction over the word trigram model .

proper name machine translation from japanese to japanese sign language
this paper describes machine translation of proper names from japanese to japanese sign language ( jsl ) . proper name transliteration is a kind of machine translation of proper names between spoken languages and involves character-tocharacter conversion based on pronunciation . however , transliteration methods can not be applied to japanese-jsl machine translation because proper names in jsl are composed of words rather than characters . our method involves not only pronunciation-based translation , but also sense-based translation , because kanji , which are ideograms that compose most japanese proper names , are closely related to jsl words . these translation methods are trained from parallel corpora . the sense-based translation part is trained via phrase alignment in sentence pairs in a japanese and jsl corpus . the pronunciation-based translation part is trained from a japanese proper name corpus and then post-processed with transformation rules . we conducted a series of evaluation experiments and obtained 75.3 % of accuracy rate , increasing from baseline method by 19.7 points . we also developed a japanese-jsl proper name translation system , in which the translated proper names are visualized with cg animations .
human1: A technique for automatically translating Japanese proper names into Japanese Sign Language (JSL) is described in this paper. Because JSL proper names are made up of words rather than characters, this task is more difficult than transliteration between spoken languages, which is based on pronunciation and is character-to-character. The suggested approach blends sense-based and pronunciation-based translation. This is important because JSL words are closely related to kanji, the ideograms that make up the majority of Japanese proper names. Both translation components are trained using parallel corpora: a Japanese proper name corpus for the pronunciation-based component, which is then post-processed using transformation rules, and a Japanese-JSL sentence pair for the sense-based component, which is trained through phrase alignment. With an accuracy rate of 75.3%, evaluation experiments significantly outperformed the baseline by 19.7 points. A Japanese-JSL proper name translation system that uses computer-generated imagery (CG) animations to visualise the translated names was also developed as a result of the research.

easily identifiable discourse relations
we present a corpus study of local discourse relations based on the penn discourse tree bank , a large manually annotated corpus of explicitly or implicitly realized relations . we show that while there is a large degree of ambiguity in temporal explicit discourse connectives , overall connectives are mostly unambiguous and allow high-accuracy prediction of discourse relation type . we achieve 93.09 % accuracy in classifying the explicit relations and 74.74 % accuracy overall . in addition , we show that some pairs of relations occur together in text more often than expected by chance . this finding suggests that global sequence classification of the relations in text can lead to better results , especially for implicit relations .

The researchers' overall classification accuracy was 74.74% and their explicit relation classification accuracy was 93.09%. Additionally, they discovered that some pairs of relations occur more frequently in text than would be predicted by chance. This latter result suggests that, especially for implicitly realised relations, a global sequence classification approach for relations within a text may produce better results.

geographic reference analysis for geographic document querying
the work presented in this paper concerns information retrieval from geographical documents , i.e . documents with a major geographic component . the final aim , in response to an informational query of the user , is to return a ranked list of relevant passages in selected documents , allowing text browsing within them . we consider in this paper the spatial component of the texts and the queries . the idea is to perform an off-line linguistic analysis of the document , extracting spatial expressions ( i.e . expressions denoting geographical localisations ) . the point is that such expressions are ( in general ) much more complex than simple place names . we present a linguistic analyser which recognises them , performing a semantic analysis and computing symbolic representations of their `` content '' . these representations , stored in the text thanks to xml annotation , will act as indexes of passages with which queries are compared . the matching of queries with text expressions is a complex process , needing several kinds of numeric and symbolic computations .

dependency-driven anaphoricity determination for coreference
this paper proposes a dependency-driven scheme to dynamically determine the syntactic parse tree structure for tree kernel-based anaphoricity determination in coreference resolution . given a full syntactic parse tree , it keeps the nodes and the paths related with current mention based on constituent dependencies from both syntactic and semantic perspectives , while removing the noisy information , eventually leading to a dependency-driven dynamic syntactic parse tree ( d-dspt ) . evaluation on the ace 2003 corpus shows that the d-dspt outperforms all previous parse tree structures on anaphoricity determination , and that applying our anaphoricity determination module in coreference resolution achieves the so far best performance .

Based on syntactic and semantic constituent dependencies, this method selectively keeps only the nodes and paths pertinent to the current mention out of a complete syntactic parse tree. The outcome of this procedure is a dependency-driven dynamic syntactic parse tree (D-DSPT), which successfully eliminates noisy information. The D-DSPT performs better for determining anaphoricity than any previously suggested parse tree structure, according to evaluations on the ACE 2003 corpus. Additionally, the best performance to date is obtained when this anaphoricity determination module is integrated into coreference resolution.

the effects of lexical resource quality on preference violation detection
lexical resources such as wordnet and verbnet are widely used in a multitude of nlp tasks , as are annotated corpora such as treebanks . often , the resources are used as-is , without question or examination . this practice risks missing significant performance gains and even entire techniques . this paper addresses the importance of resource quality through the lens of a challenging nlp task : detecting selectional preference violations . we present david , a simple , lexical resource-based preference violation detector . with asis lexical resources , david achieves an f1-measure of just 28.27 % . when the resource entries and parser outputs for a small sample are corrected , however , the f1-measure on that sample jumps from 40 % to 61.54 % , and performance on other examples rises , suggesting that the algorithm becomes practical given refined resources . more broadly , this paper shows that resource quality matters tremendously , sometimes even more than algorithmic improvements .

joint inference for knowledge extraction from biomedical literature
knowledge extraction from online repositories such as pubmed holds the promise of dramatically speeding up biomedical research and drug design . after initially focusing on recognizing proteins and binary interactions , the community has recently shifted their attention to the more ambitious task of recognizing complex , nested event structures . state-ofthe-art systems use a pipeline architecture in which the candidate events are identified first , and subsequently the arguments . this fails to leverage joint inference among events and arguments for mutual disambiguation . some joint approaches have been proposed , but they still lag much behind in accuracy . in this paper , we present the first joint approach for bioevent extraction that obtains state-of-the-art results . our system is based on markov logic and adopts a novel formulation by jointly predicting events and arguments , as well as individual dependency edges that compose the argument paths . on the bionlp09 shared task dataset , it reduced f1 errors by more than 10 % compared to the previous best joint approach .

Based on Markov Logic, the suggested system has a novel formulation that predicts events and their arguments together with the individual dependency edges that make up the argument paths. When tested on the BioNLP09 shared task dataset, this system outperformed the previous best joint approach by reducing F1 errors by more than 10%.

a machine learning based approach to evaluating retrieval systems

this paper presents a rule-based approach for finding out the stems from text in bengali , a resource-poor language . it starts by introducing the concept of orthographic syllable , the basic orthographic unit of bengali . then it discusses the morphological structure of the tokens for different parts of speech , formalizes the inflection rule constructs and formulates a quantitative ranking measure for potential candidate stems of a token . these concepts are applied in the design and implementation of an extensible architecture of a stemmer system for bengali text . the accuracy of the system is calculated to be ~89 % and above .

improved inference for unlexicalized parsing
we present several improvements to unlexicalized parsing with hierarchically state-split pcfgs . first , we present a novel coarse-to-fine method in which a grammars own hierarchical projections are used for incremental pruning , including a method for efficiently computing projections of a grammar without a treebank . in our experiments , hierarchical pruning greatly accelerates parsing with no loss in empirical accuracy . second , we compare various inference procedures for state-split pcfgs from the standpoint of risk minimization , paying particular attention to their practical tradeoffs . finally , we present multilingual experiments which show that parsing with hierarchical state-splitting is fast and accurate in multiple languages and domains , even without any language-specific tuning .
human1: Several developments in unlexicalized parsing with hierarchically state-split PCFGs are described in this paper. It first presents a brand-new coarse-to-fine parsing technique. This method employs a grammar's own hierarchical projections for incremental pruning, along with a quick and easy way to calculate these projections without the need for a treebank. This hierarchical pruning greatly accelerates parsing without compromising empirical accuracy, according to experiments. Second, the study highlights the practical trade-offs of different inference processes for state-split PCFGs by comparing them from the standpoint of risk minimisation. Lastly, multilingual experiments are presented, showing that, even without any language-specific tuning, parsing with hierarchical state-splitting is accurate and fast across a variety of languages and domains.

automatic identification of sentiment vocabulary : exploiting low association with known sentiment terms
we describe an extension to the technique for the automatic identification and labeling of sentiment terms described in turney ( 2002 ) and turney and littman ( 2002 ) . their basic assumption is that sentiment terms of similar orientation tend to co-occur at the document level . we add a second assumption , namely that sentiment terms of opposite orientation tend not to co-occur at the sentence level . this additional assumption allows us to identify sentiment-bearing terms very reliably . we then use these newly identified terms in various scenarios for the sentiment classification of sentences . we show that our approach outperforms turneys original approach . combining our approach with a naive bayes bootstrapping method yields a further small improvement of classifier performance . we finally compare our results to precision and recall figures that can be obtained on the same data set with labeled data .
human1: Building on the work of Turney (2002) and Turney and Littman (2002), this paper presents an extension to the automatic identification and labelling of sentiment terms. At the document level, their initial approach presumes that sentiment terms with comparable orientations co-occur. A second important premise is added by this expanded method: sentiment terms with opposing orientations typically do not co-occur at the sentence level. This new presumption greatly improves the accuracy of sentiment-bearing term identification. These recently discovered terms are then used in a variety of contexts to classify sentiment at the sentence level. Turney's original method is outperformed by the authors' approach. Additionally, their method produces a slight improvement in classifier performance when combined with a Naive Bayes bootstrapping method. Lastly, they use labelled data to compare their findings with precision and recall numbers that can be obtained from the same dataset.

Human 2: This work extends earlier methods for automatically identifying and labeling sentiment vocabulary. The previous approach assumed that words with similar sentiment tend to appear together across documents. We introduce an additional assumption that words with opposite sentiment rarely appear in the same sentence. This extra constraint enables highly reliable detection of sentiment terms. We then apply the newly discovered terms to several sentence-level sentiment classification tasks. Our experiments show that this method achieves better performance than the original approach by Turney, and that adding a simple naive Bayes bootstrapping step yields a small further improvement. We also compare our results with the precision and recall scores obtainable from supervised data on the same corpus.

a better -best list : practical determinization of weighted finite tree
ranked lists of output trees from syntactic statistical nlp applications frequently contain multiple repeated entries . this redundancy leads to misrepresentation of tree weight and reduced information for debugging and tuning purposes . it is chiefly due to nondeterminism in the weighted automata that produce the results . we introduce an algorithm that determinizes such automata while preserving proper weights , returning the sum of the weight of all multiply derived trees . we also demonstrate our algorithms effectiveness on two large-scale tasks .
human1: The problem of redundancy in ranked lists of output trees from syntactic statistical natural language processing applications is discussed in this paper. Redundant entries distort tree weights and decrease debugging information. The non-determinism of the weighted automata producing these results is the root cause of this issue. In order to guarantee that the returned value accurately reflects the sum of weights for all multiply-derived trees, the authors suggest an algorithm that determinizes such automata while precisely maintaining weights. They use two large-scale tasks to show how effective their algorithm is.

Human 2: Many ranked outputs produced by statistical tree based language processing systems contain several identical trees. This duplication can distort the true weights of the trees and reduces the usefulness of the ranked list for analysis and system tuning. The main cause of this repetition is the nondeterministic behavior of the weighted automata that generate the trees. In this work we present an algorithm that transforms these automata into deterministic ones while correctly preserving the total weight of all trees that were generated multiple times. The algorithm collapses repeated trees and assigns them the combined weight they should have. We also show the effectiveness of our approach on two large real world tasks.

Source: this study investigates on building a better chinese word segmentation model for statistical machine translation . it aims at leveraging word boundary information , automatically learned by bilingual character-based alignments , to induce a preferable segmentation model . we propose dealing with the induced word boundaries as soft constraints to bias the continuous learning of a supervised crfs model , trained by the treebank data ( labeled ) , on the bilingual data ( unlabeled ) . the induced word boundary information is encoded as a graph propagation constraint . the constrained model induction is accomplished by using posterior regularization algorithm . the experiments on a chinese-to-english machine translation task reveal that the proposed model can bring positive segmentation effects to translation quality .
human1: The goal of this research is to create a better statistical machine translation (SMT) word segmentation model for Chinese. The main concept is to use bilingual character-based alignments' automatically learnt word boundary information to improve segmentation. The suggested approach uses these induced word boundaries as soft constraints to bias a supervised Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) model's continuous learning. Labelled treebank data is used to train this CRFs model, and then unlabelled bilingual data is used to adapt it. The posterior regularisation algorithm is used to induct the constrained model after the induced word boundary information is encoded as a graph propagation constraint. Through advantageous segmentation effects, experiments on a Chinese-to-English machine translation task show that this suggested model improves translation quality.

Human 2: This paper investigates how to build a stronger Chinese word segmentation model for statistical machine translation. The main goal is to take advantage of word boundary signals that are automatically learned through bilingual character alignments, and use them to guide the development of a better segmentation model. We treat these learned boundaries as soft constraints that influence the continuous training of a supervised CRF model, which is originally trained on labeled treebank data but further adjusted using unlabeled bilingual data. The boundary information is represented as a graph propagation constraint, and the improved model is learned through a posterior regularization approach. Experiments on a Chinese to English translation task show that the proposed method enhances segmentation and leads to better translation performance.

Source:
bilingual random walk models for automated grammar correction of esl author-produced text
we present a novel noisy channel model for correcting text produced by english as a second language ( esl ) authors . we model the english word choices made by esl authors as a random walk across an undirected bipartite dictionary graph composed of edges between english words and associated words in an authors native language . we present two such models , using cascades of weighted finitestate transducers ( wfsts ) to model language model priors , random walk-induced noise , and observed sentences , and expectation maximization ( em ) to learn model parameters after park and levy ( 2011 ) . we show that such models can make intelligent word substitutions to improve grammaticality in an unsupervised setting .
human1: In order to automatically correct the grammar of texts written by authors of English as a Second Language (ESL), this paper presents a novel noisy channel model. The model's central component is a representation of the English word choices made by ESL authors as a random walk across an undirected bipartite dictionary graph. The author's native language and English words are connected in this graph. The study introduces two such models that model observed sentences, random walk-induced noise, and language model priors using cascades of weighted finite-state transducers (WFSTs). In accordance with Park and Levy's (2011) methodology, Expectation Maximisation (EM) is used to learn model parameters. The authors show that these models can improve grammaticality in an unsupervised environment by intelligently substituting words.

5. What metrics were used to evaluate grammaticality improvements — e.g., GLEU, ERRANT, human judgments?
6. What is the central takeaway: that unsupervised models can rival supervised ones in certain ESL contexts, that native-language-informed graphs are effective for lexical errors, or that graph-based WFST modeling is a promising paradigm shift?
irrelevant_question: 4. What datasets were used to test the model — were they standardized ESL corpora (e.g., NUCLE, Lang-8), and were gold grammatical corrections available for evaluation?
predicted_question: How can the abstract describe the model's central component?
Human 2: This paper introduces a new noisy channel approach for correcting text written by authors who use English as a second language. The model treats the vocabulary choices made by these writers as a random walk over an undirected bipartite dictionary graph that links English words with words from the writer’s native language. We describe two versions of this model that use cascaded weighted finite state transducers to represent the language model, the noise introduced by the random walk, and the observed sentences. The parameters of the models are learned through an expectation maximization procedure similar to the method of Park and Levy. Our experiments show that the models can make effective word substitutions and improve grammaticality without relying on supervised data.

cross-lingual alignment and completion of wikipedia templates
for many languages , the size of wikipedia is an order of magnitude smaller than the english wikipedia . we present a method for cross-lingual alignment of template and infobox attributes in wikipedia . the alignment is used to add and complete templates and infoboxes in one language with information derived from wikipedia in another language . we show that alignment between english and dutch wikipedia is accurate and that the result can be used to expand the number of template attribute-value pairs in dutch wikipedia by 50 % . furthermore , the alignment provides valuable information for normalization of template and attribute names and can be used to detect potential inconsistenc
human1: The difference in Wikipedia size between languages many non-English Wikipedias are much smaller is the subject of this paper. It presents a technique for aligning template and infobox attributes across languages in Wikipedia. Using data from another Wikipedia, this alignment is then used to add and finish templates and infoboxes in one language. The accuracy of the authors' alignment technique between English and Dutch Wikipedia is demonstrated, revealing that it can increase the quantity of template attribute-value pairs in Dutch Wikipedia by a remarkable 50%. In addition to expansion, the alignment can be used to identify possible linguistic inconsistencies and offers useful information for standardising template and attribute names.

3. Have previous methods for expanding Wikipedia content focused on article translation, manual editing, or structural reuse — and how does your method differ?
4. How is ambiguity handled — for example, if an English attribute has multiple possible Dutch equivalents?
5. What is the most important practical outcome — reducing manual editing, boosting under-resourced Wikipedias, or setting the stage for future multilingual knowledge alignment?
irrelevant_question: 3. Have previous methods for expanding Wikipedia content focused on article translation, manual editing, or structural reuse — and how does your method differ?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the importance of cross-lingual alignment?
Human 2: In many languages, Wikipedia is significantly smaller than the English version. We introduce a method for aligning template and infobox attributes across languages in Wikipedia. This alignment enables us to fill in and extend templates and infoboxes in one language using information from another. Our experiments show that alignment between English and Dutch Wikipedia is highly accurate, and that it can increase the number of attribute-value pairs in Dutch infoboxes by about fifty percent. The alignment process also offers useful insights for standardizing template and attribute names, and it helps identify possible inconsistencies.

Source: naively collecting translations by crowdsourcing the task to non-professional translators yields disfluent , low-quality results if no quality control is exercised . we demonstrate a variety of mechanisms that increase the translation quality to near professional levels . specifically , we solicit redundant translations and edits to them , and automatically select the best output among them . we propose a set of features that model both the translations and the translators , such as country of residence , lm perplexity of the translation , edit rate from the other translations , and ( optionally ) calibration against professional translators . using these features to score the collected translations , we are able to discriminate between acceptable and unacceptable translations . we recreate the nist 2009 urdu-toenglish evaluation set with mechanical turk , and quantitatively show that our models are able to select translations within the range of quality that we expect from professional translators . the total cost is more than an order of magnitude lower than professional translation .
human1: In order to overcome the generally poor quality and sloppy outcomes of naive crowdsourcing, this paper examines crowdsourcing translation from non-professional translators and shows how quality control procedures can raise output to almost professional levels. The authors suggest automatically choosing the best output from a pool of redundant translations and subsequent edits. In order to score these translations, they present a set of features that model the translators (e.g., country of residence, and optionally, calibration against professional translators) as well as the translations themselves (e.g., language model perplexity, edit rate from other translations). These characteristics make it possible to distinguish between translations that are acceptable and those that are not. They used Mechanical Turk to recreate the NIST 2009 Urdu-to-English evaluation set in order to validate their methodology. Compared to professional translation services, their models reduced the overall cost by more than an order of magnitude while quantitatively selecting translations that were within the quality range expected from professional translators.

1. What are the main shortcomings of naive crowdsourcing in translation — is it fluency, adequacy, inconsistency, or something else?

3. What makes this problem particularly challenging in a translation task involving a language like Urdu?
4. What are the practical applications of this method — low-resource MT development, rapid dataset creation, or humanitarian/multilingual web content?
5. What is the main takeaway: that crowd translation can be viable with minimal supervision, that crowd models can match pros at 1/10th the cost, or something else?
irrelevant_question: 4. What are the practical applications of this method — low-resource MT development, rapid dataset creation, or humanitarian/multilingual web content?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain crowdsourcing's limitations?
Human 2: Collecting translations from crowd workers who are not trained professionals often leads to text that is awkward or poor in quality unless some form of quality control is applied. In this work, we show several ways to substantially raise translation quality so that it approaches the level produced by professionals. Our method gathers multiple independent translations and edits, then automatically chooses the best option among them. We introduce a set of features that assess both the translations and the translators, including the translator’s country of residence, the language model perplexity of each translation, the degree of similarity or difference compared with other translations, and when available, calibration using translations created by experts. With these feature based scores, we can reliably separate good translations from poor ones. By recreating the NIST 2009 Urdu to English evaluation set through Mechanical Turk, we demonstrate that our system can select outputs that fall within the quality range expected from expert translators, while keeping the total cost dramatically lower than professional translation.

Source: this paper introduces a maximum entropy dependency parser based on an efficient kbest maximum spanning tree ( mst ) algorithm . although recent work suggests that the edge-factored constraints of the mst algorithm significantly inhibit parsing accuracy , we show that generating the 50-best parses according to an edge-factored model has an oracle performance well above the 1-best performance of the best dependency parsers . this motivates our parsing approach , which is based on reranking the kbest parses generated by an edge-factored model . oracle parse accuracy results are presented for the edge-factored model and 1-best results for the reranker on eight languages ( seven from conll-x and english ) .
human1: An effective k-best Maximum Spanning Tree (MST) algorithm is used in this paper to propose a Maximum Entropy dependency parser. The authors show that generating the 50-best parses from an edge-factored model yields an oracle performance significantly higher than the 1-best performance of leading dependency parsers, despite earlier suggestions that the edge-factored constraints of the MST algorithm might limit parsing accuracy. Their parsing strategy, which entails reranking the k-best parses generated by an edge-factored model, is motivated by this observation. The paper shows 1-best results for the reranker across eight languages, including seven from CoNLL-X and English, and oracle parse accuracy results for the edge-factored model.

4. What is the main takeaway for parsing researchers — that MST parsers are far more capable when used with reranking, or that edge-factored models shouldn’t be underestimated?
5. How can this framework be extended — could it be integrated with neural scoring, used in semi-supervised parsing, or adapted to constituency parsing?
6. Did some languages benefit more than others from reranking — and why? Is this related to sentence length, language typology, or training data size?
irrelevant_question: 5. How can this framework be extended — could it be integrated with neural scoring, used in semi-supervised parsing, or adapted to constituency parsing?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain reranking the k-best parses?
Human 2: This paper presents a dependency parser that uses a maximum entropy model together with an efficient k best maximum spanning tree algorithm. Although earlier studies claim that the edge factored limits of the mst approach restrict parsing accuracy, we show that producing the fifty best parses from an edge factored model achieves oracle performance far beyond the single best output of leading dependency parsers. This observation motivates our method, which reranks the k best parses generated by the edge factored model. We report oracle accuracy for the base model and single best results for the reranker across eight languages, including seven from the conll x collection and English.

Source: the goal of the presented project is to assign a structure of clauses to czech sentences from the prague dependency treebank ( pdt ) as a new layer of syntactic annotation , a layer of clause structure . the annotation is based on the concept of segments , linguistically motivated and easily automatically detectable units . the task of the annotators is to identify relations among segments , especially relations of super/subordination , coordination , apposition and parenthesis . then they identify individual clauses forming complex sentences . in the pilot phase of the annotation , 2,699 sentences from pdt were annotated with respect to their sentence structure .
human1: The project described in this paper aims to give Czech sentences in the Prague Dependency Treebank (PDT) a new layer of syntactic annotation called a clause structure. The idea of segments linguistically motivated and readily identifiable units is the foundation of the annotation process. Finding connections between these segments is the responsibility of annotators, who must pay particular attention to super/subordination, coordination, apposition, and parenthesis. The individual clauses that make up complex sentences are then identified. 2,699 PDT sentences were annotated to show their sentence structure during the pilot phase.

Human 2: The aim of this project is to assign clause structure to Czech sentences in the Prague Dependency Treebank as an additional layer of syntactic annotation. The annotation approach is based on the idea of segments, which are linguistically motivated units that can be detected automatically.
