Source: paraphrases are useful for statistical machine translation ( smt ) and natural language processing tasks . distributional paraphrase generation is independent of parallel texts and syntactic parses , and hence is suitable also for resource-poor languages , but tends to erroneously rank antonyms , trend-contrasting , and polarity-dissimilar candidates as good paraphrases . we present here a novel method for improving distributional paraphrasing by filtering out such candidates . we evaluate it in simulated low and mid-resourced smt tasks , translating from english to two quite different languages . we show statistically significant gains in english-to-chinese translation quality , up to 1 bleu from nonfiltered paraphrase-augmented models ( 1.6 bleu from baseline ) . we also show that yielding gains in
human1: Due to its independence from parallel texts and syntactic parses, this paper presents a novel approach to enhance distributional paraphrase generation, which is advantageous for Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) and other NLP tasks, particularly for resource-poor languages. Its propensity to incorrectly rank antonyms, trend-contrasting, and polarity-dissimilar terms as good paraphrases is the main problem with distributional paraphrasing. The goal of the suggested approach is to eliminate these incorrect candidates. Simulated low- and mid-resource SMT tasks that require translation from English into two different languages are used to assess the methodology. Up to 1 BLEU point improvement over non-filtered paraphrase-augmented models (and 1.6 BLEU points over the baseline) is demonstrated by the results, which demonstrate statistically significant improvements in English-to-Chinese translation quality. Although the full extent of those gains is not covered in the provided text, the paper also shows that this method produces gains in additional scenarios.

3. What downstream issues do these bad paraphrases cause — degraded BLEU scores, fluency errors, semantic drift in translation?
4. Is your main contribution the new filtering technique, the demonstration of its benefit to SMT, or a general framework applicable across languages and tasks?

irrelevant_question: 5. How is this filtering approach different from prior work — does it use sentiment polarity, antonym databases, supervised classifiers, or contextual embeddings?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the novel approach's advantages?
Human 2: Paraphrases play an important role in statistical machine translation and many natural language processing applications. Methods that generate paraphrases from distributional information do not rely on parallel corpora or syntactic parsing, making them suitable for languages with limited resources, but they often mistakenly consider antonyms or expressions with contrasting trends or opposite polarity as good paraphrases. This paper introduces an approach that filters out such problematic candidates to improve distributional paraphrase generation. We evaluate the method in simulated low and medium resource translation settings from English into two very different target languages. The experiments show meaningful improvements for English to Chinese translation, including gains of up to one bleu point over paraphrase enhanced systems without filtering and gains of up to one point six bleu compared to the baseline.

Source: determining the semantic intent of web queries not only involves identifying their semantic class , which is a primary focus of previous works , but also understanding their semantic structure . in this work , we formally define the semantic structure of noun phrase queries as comprised of intent heads and intent modifiers . we present methods that automatically identify these constituents as well as their semantic roles based on markov and semi-markov conditional random fields . we show that the use of semantic features and syntactic features significantly contribute to improving the understanding performance .
human1: The goal of this paper is to comprehend the semantic structure of noun phrase web queries, which goes beyond simply determining their semantic class. Intention heads and intent modifiers make up this structure, according to the authors' formal definition. They offer techniques that use both Markov and Semi-Markov Conditional Random Fields to automatically identify these constituents and their semantic roles. According to the research, the performance of comprehending query intent is greatly improved by integrating semantic and syntactic features.

predicted_question: What practical problems arise when this finer-grained structure is not modeled — poor retrieval, irrelevant results, misclassified intent?
Human 2: Understanding the meaning behind web search queries requires more than assigning a semantic category, which has been the emphasis of most earlier studies. It also involves uncovering the internal semantic structure of the query. In this paper, we define the structure of noun phrase queries as consisting of an intent head together with intent modifiers. We introduce methods that automatically detect these components and determine their semantic roles using Markov and semi Markov conditional random field models. Our experiments show that adding semantic and syntactic features greatly improves the accuracy of semantic interpretation.

estimation of conditional probabilities with decision trees and an application to fine-grained pos tagging
we present a hmm part-of-speech tagging method which is particularly suited for pos tagsets with a large number of fine-grained tags . it is based on three ideas : ( 1 ) splitting of the pos tags into attribute vectors and decomposition of the contextual pos probabilities of the hmm into a product of attribute probabilities , ( 2 ) estimation of the contextual probabilities with decision trees , and ( 3 ) use of high-order hmms . in experiments on german and czech data , our tagger outperformed stateof-the-art pos taggers .
human1: This work presents a Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging technique based on Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) that is especially intended for tagsets with a high quantity of fine-grained tags. The strategy incorporates three fundamental concepts: Contextual POS probabilities are factorised within the HMM into a product of attribute probabilities after POS tags are broken down into attribute vectors; Decision trees are used to estimate these contextual probabilities;  utilising high-order HMMs. This tagger consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art POS taggers, according to experiments done on German and Czech datasets.

5. What are the broader applications of this tagger — downstream syntactic parsing, machine translation, or NLP for low-resource/fine-grained languages?
irrelevant_question: 5. What are the broader applications of this tagger — downstream syntactic parsing, machine translation, or NLP for low-resource/fine-grained languages?
predicted_question: How can the abstract summarize key concepts concisely?
Human 2: We describe a hidden Markov model based method for part of speech tagging that is especially effective for languages with very large and fine grained tag inventories. Our approach relies on three main ideas. First, each tag is decomposed into a vector of attributes, and the contextual probabilities in the model are factorized into probabilities over these attributes. Second, these contextual attribute probabilities are learned with decision trees. Third, we make use of high order hidden Markov models to capture richer context. Experiments on German and Czech show that our system surpasses existing state of the art taggers.

Source: systems that interact with the user via natural language are in their infancy . as these systems mature and become more complex , it would be desirable for a system developer if there were an automatic method for creating natural language generation components that can produce quality output efficiently . we conduct experiments that show that this goal appears to be realizable . in particular we discuss a natural language generation system that is composed of spot , a trainable sentence planner , and fergus , a stochastic surface realizer . we show how these stochastic nlg components can be made to work together , that they can be ported to new domains with apparent ease , and that such nlg components can be integrated in a real-time dialog system .
human1: The viability of automatically producing natural language generation (NLG) components for progressively more intricate interactive systems is vestigated in this paper. It makes the case that system developers would gain a great deal from such automation since it would make it possible to produce high-quality natural language output quickly. The authors provide experimental data indicating that this objective can be accomplished. They describe an NLG system that consists of FERGUS, a stochastic surface realiser, and SPOT, a trainable sentence planner. The study shows how these stochastic NLG elements can be successfully incorporated into a real-time dialogue system, seamlessly ported to new domains, and integrated.

irrelevant_question: 4. Can your framework generalize to other types of NLG tasks — e.g., email replies, game dialogue, personalized recommendations — or is it tuned for task-oriented systems only?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the importance of natural language generation in the context of interactive systems?
Human 2: This paper presents a method for identifying sentiment vocabulary automatically by extending earlier work from Turney. Their original approach assumed that words with the same sentiment orientation tend to appear together across documents. We add a second assumption: words with opposite sentiment orientations usually do not co occur within the same sentence. This additional constraint enables us to detect sentiment bearing terms much more reliably. We then apply the newly discovered terms to several sentence level sentiment classification tasks. Our experiments show that our method outperforms Turney’s original approach, and that combining it with a simple naive Bayes bootstrapping strategy yields a small additional improvement. We also compare our results to precision and recall scores achieved with labeled data on the same dataset.

Source: we address the problem of identifying multiword expressions in a language , focusing on english phrasal verbs . our polyglot ranking approach integrates frequency statistics from translated corpora in 50 different languages . our experimental evaluation demonstrates that combining statistical evidence from many parallel corpora using a novel ranking-oriented boosting algorithm produces a comprehensive set of english phrasal verbs , achieving performance comparable to a human-curated set .

chained machine translation using morphemes as pivot language and technology of china
as the smallest meaning-bearing elements of the languages which have rich morphology information , morphemes are often integrated into state-of-the-art statistical machine translation to improve translation quality . the paper proposes an approach which novelly uses morphemes as pivot language in a chained machine translation system . a machine translation based method is used therein to find the mapping relations between morphemes and words . experiments show the effectiveness of our approach , achieving 18.6 percent increase in bleu score over the baseline phrase-based machine translation system .

Tests show that this strategy works, as evidenced by an 18.6% improvement in BLEU score compared to a baseline phrase-based SMT system.

joint learning and inference for grammatical error correction
state-of-the-art systems for grammatical error correction are based on a collection of independently-trained models for specific errors . such models ignore linguistic interactions at the sentence level and thus do poorly on mistakes that involve grammatical dependencies among several words . in this paper , we identify linguistic structures with interacting grammatical properties and propose to address such dependencies via joint inference and joint learning . we show that it is possible to identify interactions well enough to facilitate a joint approach and , consequently , that joint methods correct incoherent predictions that independentlytrained classifiers tend to produce . furthermore , because the joint learning model considers interacting phenomena during training , it is able to identify mistakes that require making multiple changes simultaneously and that standard approaches miss . overall , our model significantly outperforms the illinois system that placed first in the conll-2013 shared task on grammatical error correction .

In order to overcome these drawbacks, this paper suggests a novel method for grammatical error correction that centres on joint inference and joint learning. The authors show that it is possible to effectively model linguistic structures with interacting grammatical properties. The incoherent predictions frequently made by independently trained classifiers are demonstrated to be corrected by their combined methodology. Additionally, the joint learning model can detect intricate errors that conventional methods miss by taking into account interacting phenomena during training. In terms of grammatical error correction, the suggested model performs noticeably better overall than the Illinois system, which won the CoNLL-2013 Shared Task.

Human 2: Most current grammatical error correction systems rely on separate models that are each trained to fix a specific type of mistake. Because these models make decisions independently, they often fail to handle errors that depend on relationships between multiple words in a sentence. We show that these interactions can be captured well enough to support a unified model, which in turn resolves the inconsistent predictions produced by independently trained classifiers. near-synonym choice in an intelligent thesaurus
an intelligent thesaurus assists a writer with alternative choices of words and orders them by their suitability in the writing context . in this paper we focus on methods for automatically choosing nearsynonyms by their semantic coherence with the context . our statistical method uses the web as a corpus to compute mutual information scores . evaluation experiments show that this method performs better than a previous method on the same task . we also propose and evaluate two more methods , one that uses anticollocations , and one that uses supervised learning . to asses the difficulty of the task , we present results obtained by human judges .

gleu : automatic evaluation of sentence-level fluency
in evaluating the output of language technology applicationsmt , natural language generation , summarisationautomatic evaluation techniques generally conflate measurement of faithfulness to source content with fluency of the resulting text . in this paper we develop an automatic evaluation metric to estimate fluency alone , by examining the use of parser outputs as metrics , and show that they correlate with human judgements of generated text fluency . we then develop a machine learner based on these , and show that this performs better than the individual parser metrics , approaching a lower bound on human performance . we finally look at different language models for generating sentences , and show that while individual parser metrics can be fooled depending on generation method , the machine learner provides a consistent estimator of fluency .
human1: In order to assess the fluency of text produced by language technology applications (such as MT, NLG, and summarisation) while separating it from faithfulness to source content, this paper presents GLEU, an automatic evaluation metric. The authors investigate the use of parser outputs as metrics and show how they relate to human assessments of the fluency of generated text. Using these parser metrics as a foundation, they create a machine learner that surpasses individual parser metrics and gets close to a lower bound of human performance. The study concludes by examining different language models for sentence generation and finding that the machine learner offers a reliable estimator of fluency, even though the generation method may deceive individual parser metrics.

predicted_question: Does the abstract explain metrics?
Human 2: Evaluating the output of language technology systems such as machine translation, natural language generation, and summarization often mixes the measurement of faithfulness to the source with the fluency of the produced text. In this work we introduce an automatic evaluation method designed to assess fluency alone. We examine how parser outputs can be used as fluency indicators and show that they align well with human judgments of how natural the generated text sounds. We then train a machine learning model based on these parser indicators and demonstrate that it performs better than each parser based metric by itself, approaching the lower bound of human agreement. Finally, we analyze different language models for sentence generation and show that while individual parser metrics can be misled depending on the generation approach, the learned model consistently provides a stable estimate of fluency.

mining atomic chinese abbreviation pairs : a probabilistic model for single character word recovery
an hmm-based single character recovery ( scr ) model is proposed in this paper to extract a large set of atomic abbreviation pairsfrom a large text corpus . by an atomic abbreviation pair , it refers to an abbreviated word and its root word ( i.e. , unabbreviated form ) in which the abbreviation is a single chinese character . this task is interesting since the abbreviation process for chinese compound words seems to be compositional ; in other words , one can often decode an abbreviated word , such as ( taiwan university ) , character-by-character back to its root form . with a large atomic abbreviation dictionary , one may be able to recover multiple-character abbreviations more easily . with only a few training iterations , the acquisition accuracy of the proposed scr model achieves 62 % and 50 % precision for training set and test set , respectively , from the aswsc-2001 corpus .

self-disclosure topic model for classifying and analyzing twitter
self-disclosure , the act of revealing oneself to others , is an important social behavior that strengthens interpersonal relationships and increases social support . although there are many social science studies of self-disclosure , they are based on manual coding of small datasets and questionnaires . we conduct a computational analysis of self-disclosure with a large dataset of naturally-occurring conversations , a semi-supervised machine learning algorithm , and a computational analysis of the effects of self-disclosure on subsequent conversations . we use a longitudinal dataset of 17 million tweets , all of which occurred in conversations that consist of five or more tweets directly replying to the previous tweet , and from dyads with twenty of more conversations each . we develop self-disclosure topic model ( sdtm ) , a variant of latent dirichlet allocation ( lda ) for automatically classifying the level of self-disclosure for each tweet . we take the results of sdtm and analyze the effects of self-disclosure on subsequent conversations . our model significantly outperforms several comparable methods on classifying the level of selfdisclosure , and the analysis of the longitudinal data using sdtm uncovers significant and positive correlation between selfdisclosure and conversation frequency and length .

In terms of categorising self-disclosure levels, the suggested model performs noticeably better than similar techniques. Additionally, self-disclosure and the frequency and duration of conversations are significantly and favourably correlated, according to the SDTM analysis of this longitudinal data.

parsing word clusters
we present and discuss experiments in statistical parsing of french , where terminal forms used during training and parsing are replaced by more general symbols , particularly clusters of words obtained through unsupervised linear clustering . we build on the work of candito and crabb ( 2009 ) who proposed to use clusters built over slightly coarsened french inflected forms . we investigate the alternative method of building clusters over lemma/part-of-speech pairs , using a raw corpus automatically tagged and lemmatized . we find that both methods lead to comparable improvement over the baseline ( we obtain f1=86.20 % and f1=86.21 % respectively , compared to a baseline of f1=84.10 % ) . yet , when we replace gold lemma/pos pairs with their corresponding cluster , we obtain an upper bound ( f1=87.80 ) that suggests room for improvement for this technique , should tagging/lemmatisation performance increase for french . we also analyze the improvement in performance for both techniques with respect to word frequency . we find that replacing word forms with clusters improves attachment performance for words that are originally either unknown or low-frequency , since these words are replaced by cluster symbols that tend to have higher frequencies . furthermore , clustering also helps significantly for medium to high frequency words , suggesting that training on word clusters leads to better probability estimates for these words .

This builds on earlier work that used clusters from coarsened French inflected forms. Additionally, changing word forms with clusters improves attachment performance for unknown or low-frequency words by replacing them with higher-frequency cluster symbols, according to an analysis of performance improvement in relation to word frequency. Crucially, medium-to-high-frequency words also benefit greatly from clustering, suggesting that training on word clusters improves probability estimates for these more prevalent terms.

pruning the search space of a hand-crafted parsing system
the demand for deep linguistic analysis for huge volumes of data means that it is increasingly important that the time taken to parse such data is minimized . in the xle parsing model which is a hand-crafted , unification-based parsing system , most of the time is spent on unification , searching for valid f-structures ( dependency attributevalue matrices ) within the space of the many valid c-structures ( phrase structure trees ) . we carried out an experiment to determine whether pruning the search space at an earlier stage of the parsing process results in an improvement in the overall time taken to parse , while maintaining the quality of the f-structures produced . we retrained a stateof-the-art probabilistic parser and used it to pre-bracket input to the xle , constraining the valid c-structure space for each sentence . we evaluated against the parc 700 dependency bank and show that it is possible to decrease the time taken to parse by 18 % while maintaining accuracy .

In order to determine whether reducing the search space early in the parsing process enhances overall parsing time while preserving f-structure quality, the authors ran an experiment. This was accomplished by constraining the valid c-structure space for every sentence by retraining a cutting-edge probabilistic parser to pre-bracket input for XLE. They show an 18% reduction in parsing time while maintaining accuracy when comparing their method to the PARC 700 dependency bank.

using mechanical turk to annotate lexicons for less commonly used languages
in this work we present results from using amazons mechanical turk ( mturk ) to annotate translation lexicons between english and a large set of less commonly used languages . we generate candidate translations for 100 english words in each of 42 foreign languages using wikipedia and a lexicon induction framework . we evaluate the mturk annotations by using positive and negative control candidate translations . additionally , we evaluate the annotations by adding pairs to our seed dictionaries , providing a feedback loop into the induction system . mturk workers are more successful in annotating some languages than others and are not evenly distributed around the world or among the worlds languages . however , in general , we find that mturk is a valuable resource for gathering cheap and simple annotations for most of the languages that we explored , and these annotations provide useful feedback in building a larger , more accurate lexicon .

The study concludes that MTurk is generally a useful and affordable resource for obtaining basic annotations for the majority of the languages examined, despite acknowledging that MTurk worker success varies by language and that workers are not distributed equally globally or across language pairs. building a japanese-chinese dictionary using
bilingual dictionaries , such as japanese-english and english-chinese to build japanese- chinese dictionary . however , japanese and chinese are nearer languages than english , there should be a more direct way of doing this . since a lot of japanese words are composed of kanji , which are similar to hanzi in chinese , we attempt to build a dictionary for kanji words by simple conversion from kanji to hanzi . our survey shows that around 2/3 of the nouns and verbal nouns in japanese are kanji words , and more than 1/3 of them can be translated into chinese directly . the accuracy of conversion is 97 % . besides , we obtain translation candidates for 24 % of the japanese words using english as a pivot language with 77 % accuracy . by adding the kanji/hanzi conversion method , we increase thecandidates by 9 % , to 33 % , with better quality candidates .
human1: In contrast to conventional approaches that rely on English, this paper offers a more direct method for creating a Japanese-Chinese dictionary. The method's central idea takes advantage of the common logographic roots of Hanzi in Chinese and Kanji in Japanese. About two-thirds of Japanese nouns and verbal nouns are Kanji words, and more than one-third of these can be translated directly into Chinese with a 97% accuracy rate using a straightforward Kanji-to-Hanzi conversion. The study also investigates the use of English as a pivot language, which produces translation candidates for 24% of Japanese words with 77% accuracy. The authors improve the quality of translations and increase candidate coverage by 33% (a 9% improvement) by combining this pivot method with the new Kanji/Hanzi conversion.
llm_questions:
1. What makes using English as a pivot language less effective for Japanese-Chinese translation compared to direct Kanji-Hanzi conversion?
 → Helps understand the motivation for bypassing English.
2. How is the Kanji-to-Hanzi conversion performed—are there specific rules, a mapping table, or a character-by-character heuristic?
 → Clarifies the method's mechanism.
3. What types of errors, if any, occur during Kanji-to-Hanzi conversion despite the high 97% accuracy?
 → Explores limitations and edge cases.
4. How do you determine whether a Kanji word has a direct Chinese equivalent? Are phonetic or semantic checks used?
 → Examines validation methods
5. What are ‘verbal nouns’ in Japanese, and why do they particularly benefit from this conversion strategy?
 → Helps non-linguists understand the term and its relevance.
irrelevant_question: 5. What are ‘verbal nouns’ in Japanese, and why do they particularly benefit from this conversion strategy?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain the method's central idea?
Human 2: Researchers often use bilingual dictionaries such as Japanese to English and English to Chinese to build Japanese Chinese dictionaries, but since Japanese and Chinese are linguistically closer, a more direct method is possible. Many Japanese words are written in kanji, which closely correspond to hanzi in Chinese, so we explore constructing a dictionary for kanji-based words through straightforward character conversion. Our analysis shows that about two thirds of Japanese nouns and verbal nouns use kanji, and more than one third of them can be directly converted into Chinese. This conversion method reaches an accuracy of ninety seven percent. Additionally, by using English as an intermediate language, we obtain translation candidates for twenty four percent of Japanese words with seventy seven percent accuracy. When we add the kanji to hanzi conversion technique, the total coverage rises to thirty three percent, and the overall quality of the translation candidates improves.

Source: france telecom r & d beijing word segmenter for sighan backoff 2006 this paper presents two word segmentation ( ws ) systems and a named entity recognition (ner ) system in france telecom r & d beijing . the one system of ws is for open tracks based on ngram language model and another one is for closed tracks based on maximum entropy approach . the ner system uses a hybrid algorithm based on class-based language model and rule-based knowledge . these systems are all augmented with a set of post-processors .
human1: Three systems two for word segmentation (WS) and one for named entity recognition (NER) developed by France Telecom R&D Beijing are described in this paper. One system uses an N-gram language model for word segmentation and is intended for open tracks. The other WS system, which is based on a maximum entropy approach, is for closed tracks. The NER system uses a hybrid algorithm that blends rule-based knowledge with a class-based language model. A collection of post-processors is added to all three systems.

1.What is the main difference between the “open track” and “closed track” tasks in word segmentation?
 → Helps clarify the purpose of using two different systems.
2. Why was an N-gram language model chosen for the open track WS system? What advantages does it offer in this context?
 → Explores the rationale behind the model choice.
3. How does the maximum entropy approach work in the closed track WS system? Is it supervised, and what kind of features are used?
 → Helps understand the learning-based model and data dependency.
4. What are some key differences in performance or behavior between the open and closed track WS systems?
 → Encourages comparison between the two methods.
5. What does the hybrid Named Entity Recognition system look like—how are rule-based and statistical methods combined?
 → Understands how two different approaches are integrated.
irrelevant_question: 5. What does the hybrid Named Entity Recognition system look like—how are rule-based and statistical methods combined?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain hybrid methods?
Human 2: We introduce two systems developed at France Telecom R&D Beijing for the SIGHAN Bakeoff 2006 word segmentation task, along with a named entity recognizer. One word-segmentation system, intended for the open track, is built using an n-gram language model, while the closed-track system uses a maximum entropy framework. The named entity system combines a class-based language model with rule-driven knowledge sources. All three systems are further enhanced through a series of post-processing modules.

Source: ctps : contextual temporal profiles for time scoping facts using state change detection temporal scope adds a time dimension to facts in knowledge bases ( kbs ) . these time scopes specify the time periods when a given fact was valid in real life . without temporal scope , many facts are underspecified , reducing the usefulness of the data for upper level applications such as question answering . existing methods for temporal scope inference and extraction still suffer from low accuracy . in this paper , we present a new method that leverages temporal profiles augmented with context contextual temporal profiles ( ctps ) of entities . through change patterns in an entitys ctp , we model the entitys state change brought about by real world events that happen to the entity ( e.g , hired , fired , divorced , etc . ) . this leads to a new formulation of the temporal scoping problem as a state change detection problem . our experiments show that this formulation of the problem , and the resulting solution are highly effective for inferring temporal scope of facts .
human1: In order to determine the real-world validity periods of facts, this paper presents a novel approach for determining the temporal scope of facts in knowledge bases (KBs). This is important because many KB facts lack temporal scope and are underspecified, which reduces their usefulness for tasks like answering questions. The accuracy of current temporal scope inference techniques is frequently poor. The suggested approach makes use of entities' Contextual Temporal Profiles (CTPs), which are temporal profiles enhanced with contextual data. The system simulates how an entity's state changes in response to real-world events (such as getting hired, fired, or divorced) by examining change patterns within the entity's CTP. As a result, the temporal scoping problem is reframed as a state change detection problem. Tests show that this new formulation of the problem and its solution are very useful for determining the temporal scope of facts.
llm_questions:
1. What is "temporal scope" in a knowledge base, and why is it important for real-world applications like question answering?
 → This helps clarify why knowing "when" something was true matters (e.g., knowing someone was CEO from 2015–2020).
2. Why do most current knowledge bases lack proper temporal scoping for facts?
 → To understand the gap this research is addressing.
3. What are Contextual Temporal Profiles (CTPs), and how do they differ from regular temporal profiles?
 → To explain how adding surrounding context (e.g., job title, events) improves time estimation.
4. How does the model detect a “state change” in an entity like being hired, promoted, or fired?
 → Makes the idea more concrete with life event examples.
5. Why is temporal scoping reframed as a “state change detection” problem? How does that help?
 → To understand the shift in methodology and why it improves results.
irrelevant_question: 5. Why is temporal scoping reframed as a “state change detection” problem? How does that help?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain temporal scope inference?
Human 2: This paper introduces a new approach for assigning temporal scope to facts in a knowledge base, which adds information about the real world time periods during which a fact is valid. Without this information, many facts are incomplete and less useful for applications like question answering. Existing methods for determining temporal scope tend to have low accuracy. We propose a technique that uses contextual temporal profiles of entities, which describe how an entity changes over time in response to real world events such as being hired, dismissed, or divorced. By detecting these state changes, we reformulate temporal scoping as a state change detection task. Experiments show that this formulation and the resulting method are highly effective for determining when facts hold.

effect of word complexity on l2 vocabulary learning
research has shown that a number of factors , such as maturational constraints , previous language background , and attention , can have an effect on l2 acquisition . one related issue that remains to be explored is what factors make an individual word more easily learned . in this study we propose that word complexity , on both the phonetic and semantic levels , affect l2 vocabulary learning . two studies showed that words with simple grapheme-to- phoneme ratios were easier to learn than more phonetically complex words , and that words with two or fewer word senses were easier to learn that those with three or more .
human1: In contrast to other L2 acquisition factors like age, prior language background, and attention, this paper examines the unexplored impact of word complexity on second language (L2) vocabulary learning. According to the study's hypothesis, learnability is impacted by complexity at both the phonetic and semantic levels. Two investigations show that: It was easier to learn words with simpler grapheme-to-phoneme ratios (phonetically less complex). It was simpler to learn words with two or fewer senses (semantically less complex) than those with three or more.
llm_questions:
1. How is “word complexity” defined in this paper?
 → Does it focus only on pronunciation (phonetics) and number of meanings (semantics)?
2. What is a grapheme-to-phoneme ratio, and why does it matter for language learning?
 → Can you give an example of a word with a simple vs. complex ratio?
3. Why are words with fewer meanings (senses) easier to learn?
 → Is it because they cause less confusion for the learner?
4. What types of L2 learners were involved in the study (e.g., age group, language background)?
 → Were results consistent across all learner types?
5. Did the study focus on short-term learning or long-term retention of the words?
 → Are simpler words remembered better even after some time?
irrelevant_question: 4. What types of L2 learners were involved in the study (e.g., age group, language background)?
predicted_question: How can the abstract describe the impact of complexity on l2 vocabulary learning?
Human 2: Research has shown that many variables, including age-related factors, prior language experience, and attention, influence second language learning. A related question that has not been examined in depth is which properties make certain words easier to acquire. In this work, we argue that the complexity of a word, both in terms of its sound structure and its meaning, plays a role in learning new vocabulary in a second language. Results from two studies indicate that words with straightforward spelling to sound patterns are learned more easily than those with more complex pronunciations, and that words with only a few meanings are acquired more readily than those with several different senses.

Source: ucolorado som : extraction of drug-drug interactions from biomedical text using knowledge- rich and knowledge-poor features in this paper , we present our approach to semeval-2013 task 9.2. it is a feature rich classification using libsvm for drug-drug interactions detection in the biomedical domain . the features are extracted considering morphosyntactic , lexical and semantic concepts . tools like opendmap and tees are used to extract semantic concepts from the corpus . the best f-score that we got for drugdrug interaction ( ddi ) detection is 50 % and 61 % and the best f-score for ddi detection and classification is 34 % and 48 % for test and development data respectively .
human1:
The UColorado SOM approach to SemEval-2013 Task 9.2, which focusses on the extraction and categorisation of drug-drug interactions (DDIs) from biomedical text, is described in this paper.
Their approach uses LibSVM as part of a feature-rich classification system. Using tools such as OpenDMAP and TEES to extract these semantic concepts from the corpus, the features are extracted by taking into account morphosyntactic, lexical, and semantic concepts. For DDI detection alone, the system's best F-scores on test and development data were 50% and 61%, respectively. The best F-scores for test and development data, respectively, were 34% and 48% for the combined task of DDI detection and classification.

 → Can you give a few examples of each?
4. Why were OpenDMAP and TEES chosen to extract semantic concepts?
 → What kinds of biomedical knowledge do these tools bring into the system?
5. What role does LibSVM play in this DDI detection pipeline?
 → Is it used for both detection and classification stages?
irrelevant_question: 1. What is a drug-drug interaction (DDI), and why is it important to detect them in biomedical text?
predicted_question: Does the abstract highlight features?
Human 2: We describe our method for SemEval 2013 Task 9.2, which uses a feature rich classifier based on LibSVM to detect drug drug interactions in biomedical text. Our system collects a wide range of morphosyntactic, lexical, and semantic features, and relies on tools such as Opendmap and TEES to extract semantic information from the data. In our experiments, the system achieved F scores of 50 percent and 61 percent for identifying drug drug interactions and scores of 34 percent and 48 percent for the combined task of detection and classification on the test and development sets.

Source:
ua-zbsa : a headline emotion classification through web information this paper presents a headline emotion classification approach based on frequency and co-occurrence information collected from the world wide web . the content words of a headline ( nouns , verbs , adverbs and adjectives ) are extracted in order to form different bag of word pairs with the joy , disgust , fear , anger , sadness and surprise emotions . for each pair, we compute the mutual information score which is obtained from the web occurrences of an emotion and the content words . our approach is based on the hypothesis that group of words which co-occur together across many documents with a given emotion are highly
probable to express the same emotion .
human1: This study presents a method for classifying headline emotions that makes use of frequency and co-occurrence data collected from the Internet. In order to create different "bags of words" pairs with six distinct emotions joy, disgust, fear, anger, sadness, and surprise the method takes content words (nouns, verbs, adverbs, and adjectives) from headlines. The mutual information score for each of these pairs is calculated using the web occurrences of the emotion and the content words that go with it. This method's central premise is that word clusters that frequently appear together with a specific emotion in a large number of documents are very likely to convey that same emotion.

 → What’s the importance of excluding function words?
4. What are the six emotions considered in this study?
 → Why were these particular emotions (joy, disgust, fear, anger, sadness, and surprise) selected?
5. How is mutual information used in this method?
 → Does it measure how often a word appears with a particular emotion online?
irrelevant_question: 2. Why do the authors focus on headlines specifically?
predicted_question: In order to create different "bags of words" pairs with six distinct emotions joy, disgust, fear, anger, sadness, and surprise the method takes content words (nouns, verbs, adverbs and adjectives) from headlines.
Human 2: This paper describes a method for classifying the emotional tone of news headlines by using frequency and co-occurrence information gathered from the web. We extract the main content words from each headline, including nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs, and pair them with the six target emotions: joy, disgust, fear, anger, sadness, and surprise. For every word–emotion pair, we calculate a mutual information score based on how often the word and the emotion appear together across web documents. The core idea behind our approach is that words which frequently appear in similar contexts with a particular emotion are more likely to convey that same emotional category.

Source: generating referring expressions with reference domain theory in this paper we present a reference generation model based on reference domain theory which gives a dynamic account of reference . this reference model assumes that each referring act both relies and updates the reference context . we present a formal definition of a reference domain , a generation algorithm and its instantiation in the give challenge .
human1: In order to present a dynamic viewpoint on the process of making references, this paper presents a reference generation model based on reference domain theory. This model's fundamental premise is that each act of referencing simultaneously updates and depends on the preexisting reference context. In the paper, a reference domain is defined formally, the suggested generation algorithm is described, and its practical application in the GIVE Challenge a standard for producing human-like references in interactive environments is illustrated.

Source:
self-training for enhancement and domain adaptation of statistical parsers trained on small datasets creating large amounts of annotated data to train statistical pcfg parsers is expensive , and the performance of such parsers declines when training and test data are taken from different domains . in this paper we use selftraining in order to improve the quality of a parser and to adapt it to a different domain , using only small amounts of manually annotated seed data . we report significant improvement both when the seed and test data are in the same domain and in the outof-domain adaptation scenario . in particular , we achieve 50 % reduction in annotation cost for the in-domain case , yielding an improvement
of 66 % over previous work , and a 20-33 % reduction for the domain adaptation case . this is the first time that self-training with small labeled datasets is applied successfully to these tasks . we were also able to formulate a characterization of when selftraining is valuable .
human1: The high annotation cost for statistical PCFG parsers and their performance degradation across domains are issues that this paper investigates using self-training. With minimal amounts of manually annotated seed data, the authors hope to enhance parser quality and adapt them to new domains. They report notable gains in both out-of-domain adaptation and when seed and test data come from the same domain. In particular, they reduced the annotation cost by 50% for in-domain parsing, which was a 66% improvement over earlier work. A cost reduction of 20–33% was noted for domain adaptation. According to the paper, self-training with small labelled datasets for these tasks has never been applied successfully before. The authors also describe the situations in which self-training is most effective.
llm_questions:
1. What problem is the paper trying to solve?
 → Is it about improving parsers when there isn’t much annotated data available?
2. What is a statistical PCFG parser, and why does it need large annotated datasets?
 → Can you explain what PCFG means and why it relies heavily on training data?
3. What is self-training in this context?
 → Does it mean the parser trains itself further using unlabeled data and its own predictions?
4. What is meant by seed data in this paper?
 → Are these small sets of manually labeled examples used to kickstart the training?
5. What are the key results the authors report?
 → For example, how much annotation cost is saved and how performance improves?
irrelevant_question: → Can you explain what PCFG means and why it relies heavily on training data?
predicted_question: Does the abstract define self-training?
Human 2: This work investigates how self-training can be used to improve and adapt statistical PCFG parsers when only limited annotated data is available. Since manually creating large labeled corpora is costly and parser performance typically drops on out-of-domain text, we explore a method that uses a small amount of human-labeled data as a starting point and automatically extends it. Our experiments show substantial gains both when training and testing occur within the same domain and when adapting to a new domain. In particular, self-training cuts annotation effort by half for the in-domain setting, achieving a 66 percent improvement over earlier approaches, and reduces annotation needs by 20–33 percent for domain adaptation. To our knowledge, this is the first successful application of self-training with very small labeled datasets for these tasks, and we also identify conditions under which the method is most effective.

models for inuktitut-english word alignment
this paper presents a set of techniques for bitext word alignment , optimized for a language pair with the characteristics of inuktitut-english . the resulting systems exploit cross-lingual affinities at the sublexical level of syllables and substrings , as well as regular patterns of transliteration and the tendency towards monotonicity of alignment . our most successful systems were based on classifier combination , and we found different combination methods performed best under the target evaluation metrics of f-measure and alignment error rate .

 → Are multiple machine learning models combined to make better alignment decisions?1.
irrelevant_question: 4. How do regular patterns of transliteration help in aligning words?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain regular patterns?
Human 2: This paper introduces a set of methods for aligning words in bitexts, tailored to the specific characteristics of the Inuktitut and English language pair. The approach takes advantage of cross-lingual similarities at the sublexical level, including syllables and character substrings, as well as predictable transliteration patterns and the generally monotonic nature of alignments between the two languages. Our best-performing systems rely on combining multiple classifiers, and we observe that different ways of combining these classifiers excel depending on whether the evaluation metric is f measure or alignment error rate.

with support vector machines and systems engineering
we use svm classifiers to predict the next action of a deterministic parser that builds labeled projective dependency graphs in an incremental fashion . non-projective dependencies are captured indirectly by projectivizing the training data for the classifiers and applying an inverse transformation to the output of the parser . we present evaluation results and an error analysis focusing on swedish and turkish .
human1: In this paper, we describe a system that uses Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers to predict a deterministic parser's next action. Labelled projective dependency graphs are created incrementally by this parser. The classifiers' training data is projectivized, and the output of the parser undergoes an inverse transformation to account for non-projective dependencies. With an emphasis on the Swedish and Turkish languages, the paper presents evaluation results and an error analysis.

Human 2: We employ support vector machine classifiers to determine the next step of a deterministic parser that incrementally constructs labeled projective dependency graphs. Non projective relations are handled indirectly by converting the training data into a projective form and then reversing this transformation on the parser output. We report evaluation results for this method and provide an error analysis with special attention to Swedish and Turkish.

Source: elleipo : a module that computes coordinative ellipsis for language generators that dont many current sentence generators lack the ability to compute elliptical versions of coordinated clauses in accordance with the rules for gapping , forward and backward conjunction reduction , and sgf ( subject gap in clauses with finite/fronted verb ) . we describe a module ( implemented in java , with german and dutch as target languages ) that takes non-elliptical coordinated clauses as input and returns all reduced versions licensed by coordinative ellipsis . it is loosely based on a new psycholinguistic theory of coordinative ellipsis proposed by kempen . in this theory , coordinative ellipsis is not supposed to result from the application of declarative grammar rules for clause formation but from a procedural component that interacts with the sentence generator and may block the overt expression of certain constituents .
human1: In order to enable language generators, especially those for German and Dutch, to generate elliptical versions of coordinated clauses, this paper presents ELLIEPO, a Java-based module. Numerous current generators are unable to manage different types of coordinative ellipsis, including subject gaps in clauses with finite/fronted verbs (SGF), gapping, and the reduction of forward and backward conjunctions. All grammatically licensed reduced versions of non-elliptical coordinated clauses are produced by ELLIEPO. Kempen's new psycholinguistic theory of coordinative ellipsis, which holds that ellipsis results from a procedural element interacting with the sentence generator to prevent the overt expression of specific constituents rather than declarative grammar rules, serves as a loose basis for its design.
llm_questions:
1. What is coordinative ellipsis, and why is it useful in natural language generation?
 → Is it when we omit repeated parts of coordinated sentences to make them shorter or more natural?
2. Why do many current language generators struggle to handle gapping or conjunction reduction?
 → Are they not programmed to detect which parts can be safely omitted?
3. What are some examples of forward and backward conjunction reduction?
 → Can you show how a long sentence gets shortened using ellipsis?
4. What does SGF (Subject Gap in clauses with Fronted/Finite verbs) mean, and how is it different from regular gapping?
 → Is it a special case that appears in German or Dutch sentence structures?
5. How does the ELLIEPO module process input and produce elliptical output?
 → Does it take full sentences and generate all the grammatically correct shortened versions?
irrelevant_question: → Can you show how a long sentence gets shortened using ellipsis?
predicted_question: Does the abstract define coordinative ellipsis?
Human 2: This paper introduces Elleipo, a module that generates coordinated sentences with appropriate ellipsis for language generation systems that lack this capability. Many existing generators cannot produce shortened coordinated clauses that follow the patterns of gapping, forward and backward conjunction reduction, or subject gap constructions. Elleipo, implemented in Java and supporting German and Dutch, takes fully expressed coordinated clauses as input and outputs all reduced forms permitted by ellipsis rules. Its design is inspired by a recent psycholinguistic account proposed by Kempen, which treats ellipsis not as the direct result of standard grammatical rules but as the outcome of a procedural mechanism that works alongside the generator and can suppress the explicit realization of certain elements.

Source: handling noisy training and testing data in the eld of empirical natural language processing , researchers constantly deal with large amounts of marked-up data ; whether the markup is done by the researcher or someone else , human nature dictates that it will have errors in it . this paper will more fully characterise the problem and discuss whether and when ( and how ) to correct the errors . the discussion is illustrated with speci c examples involving function tagging in the penn treebank .

tree conditional random fields
in this paper we apply conditional random fields ( crfs ) to the semantic role labelling task . we define a random field over the structure of each sentences syntactic parse tree . for each node of the tree , the model must predict a semantic role label, which is interpreted as the labelling for the corresponding syntactic constituent . we show how modelling the task as a tree labelling problem allows for the use of efficient crf inference algorithms , while also increasing generalisation performance when compared to the equivalent maximum entropy classifier . we have participated in the conll-2005 shared task closed challenge with full syntactic information .
human1: The use of Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) for semantic role labelling is described in this paper. The authors directly define a random field over the syntactic parse tree structure of each sentence. The model's job is to predict a semantic role label for each node in the tree, which is equivalent to labelling the syntactic constituent that goes with it. The study shows that effective CRF inference algorithms can be used when the task is framed as a tree labelling problem. Additionally, it is demonstrated that this method outperforms an equivalent maximum entropy classifier in terms of generalisation. The authors use complete syntactic information to acknowledge their involvement in the CoNLL-2005 shared task closed challenge.

1. What is Semantic Role Labelling (SRL), and why is it important in NLP?
 → Does it involve identifying what role each phrase plays in a sentence, like who did what to whom?
2. What is a syntactic parse tree, and how is it used in this paper?

whats in a p-value in nlp
in nlp , we need to document that our proposed methods perform significantly better with respect to standard metrics than previous approaches , typically by reporting p-values obtained by rank- or randomization-based tests . we show that significance results following current research standards are unreliable and , in addition , very sensitive to sample size , covariates such as sentence length , as well as to the existence of multiple
metrics . we estimate that under the assumption of perfect metrics and unbiased data , we need a significance cut-off at 0.0025 to reduce the risk of false positive results to < 5 % . since in practice we often have considerable selection bias and poor metrics , this , however, will not do alone .

P-values are frequently used to show that suggested methods perform noticeably better than earlier approaches on common metrics. According to the authors, the way significance results are currently reported is unreliable and extremely sensitive to variables like sample size, covariates (like sentence length), and the existence of multiple metrics. They calculate that a more stringent significance cut-off of p < 0.0025 is required to lower the risk of false positives to less than 5%, even in the case of perfect metrics and unbiased data. The paper's conclusion points out that, considering the pervasive problems of selection bias and subpar metrics in real-world NLP research, this adjustment alone is insufficient.

We argue that, according to prevailing research practices, these significance findings are often unstable. in this paper , we describe a phrase-based unigram model for statistical machine translation that uses a much simpler set of model parameters than similar phrasebased models . the units of translation are blocks pairs of phrases . during decoding , we use a block unigram model and a word-based trigram language model . during training , the blocks are learned from source interval projections using an underlying high-precision word alignment . the system performance is significantly increased by applying a novel block extension algorithm using an additional highrecall word alignment . the blocks are further filtered using unigram-count selection criteria . the system has been successfully test on a chinese- english and an arabicenglish translation task .

we present a bayesian formulation for weakly-supervised learning of a combinatory categorial grammar ( ccg ) supertagger with an hmm . we assume supervision in the form of a tag dictionary , and our prior encourages the use of crosslinguistically common category structures as well as transitions between tags that can combine locally according to ccgs combinators . our prior is theoretically appealing since it is motivated by languageindependent , universal properties of the ccg formalism . empirically , we show that it yields substantial improvements over previous work that used similar biases to initialize an em-based learner . additional gains are obtained by further shaping the prior with corpus-specific information that is extracted automatically from raw text and a tag dictionary .

successful application of multi-view cotraining algorithms relies on the ability to factor the available features into views that are compatible and uncorrelated . this can potentially preclude their use on problems such as coreference resolution that lack an obvious feature split . to bootstrap coreference classifiers , we propose and evaluate a single-view weakly supervised algorithm that relies on two different learning algorithms in lieu of the two different views required by co-training . in addition , we investigate a method for ranking unlabeled instances to be fed back into the bootstrapping loop as labeled data , aiming to alleviate the problem of performance deterioration that is commonly observed in the course of bootstrapping .

This algorithm uses two different machine learning algorithms instead of two different views. we describe a history-based generative parsing model which uses a k-nearest neighbour ( k-nn ) technique to estimate the models parameters . taking the output of a base n-best parser we use our model to re-estimate the log probability of each parse tree in the n-best list for sentences from the penn wall street journal treebank . by further decomposing the local probability distributions of the base model , enriching the set of conditioning features used to estimate the models parameters, and using k-nn as opposed to the witten-bell estimation of the base model , we achieve an f-score of 89.2 % , representing a 4 % relative decrease in f-score error over the 1-best output of the base parser .

logarithmic opinion pools for conditional random fields division of informatics and software engineering division of informatics
recent work on conditional random fields ( crfs ) has demonstrated the need for regularisation to counter the tendency of these models to overfit . the standard approach to regularising crfs involves a prior distribution over the model parameters , typically requiring search over a hyperparameter space . in this paper we address the overfitting problem from a different perspective , by factoring the crf distribution into a weighted product of individual expert crf distributions . we call this model a logarithmic opinion pool ( lop ) of crfs ( lop-crfs ) . we apply the lop-crf to two sequencing tasks . our results show that unregularised expert crfs with an unregularised crf under a lop can outperform the unregularised crf , and attain a performance level close to the regularised crf . lop-crfs therefore provide a viable alternative to crf regularisation without the need for hyperparameter search .

By factoring the CRF distribution into a weighted product of individual expert CRF distributions, the authors offer an alternate approach. We call this new model a Logarithmic Opinion Pool (LOP) of CRFs (LOP-CRFs). Their results show that unregularized expert CRFs combined under a LOP can achieve performance levels comparable to a regularised CRF and outperform a single unregularized CRF when applied to two sequencing tasks. This suggests that LOP-CRFs provide a good substitute for conventional CRF regularisation, doing away with the need for intricate hyperparameter searching.

Human 2: Recent studies on Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) have highlighted their tendency to overfit, making some form of regularization essential. The conventional solution introduces priors over model parameters, which usually requires searching a hyperparameter space. In this work, we approach the overfitting issue differently: we decompose the CRF distribution into a weighted product of several expert CRF models, forming what we term a logarithmic opinion pool of CRFs (LOP-CRFs). We evaluate LOP-CRFs on two sequence-labeling tasks and find that combining unregularized expert CRFs with an unregularized main CRF through this pooling method yields better results than using a single unregularized CRF, achieving performance close to that of a regularized CRF. This shows that LOP-CRFs can serve as an effective alternative to traditional CRF regularization, eliminating the need for hyperparameter tuning.

efficient computation of entropy gradient for semi-supervised conditional random fields
entropy regularization is a straightforward and successful method of semi-supervised learning that augments the traditional conditional likelihood objective function with an additional term that aims to minimize the predicted label entropy on unlabeled data . it has previously been demonstrated to provide positive results in linear-chain crfs , but the published method for calculating the entropy gradient requires significantly more computation than supervised crf training . this paper presents a new derivation and dynamic program for calculating the entropy gradient that is significantly more efficienthaving the same asymptotic time complexity as supervised crf training . we also present efficient generalizations of this method for calculating the label entropy of all sub-sequences , which is useful for active learning , among other applications .
human1: The computational inefficiency of entropy regularisation in semi-supervised Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) is discussed in this paper. The gradient calculation for entropy regularisation has historically been much more costly than that of conventional supervised CRF training, despite the fact that it improves CRFs by reducing predicted label entropy on unlabelled data. A novel derivation and dynamic programming algorithm for calculating this entropy gradient are presented in the paper. This new method is much more efficient than supervised CRF training, achieving the same asymptotic time complexity. Additionally, the authors offer effective extensions of this technique for determining the label entropy of every subsequence, which has further uses in active learning, among other contexts.

Although prior studies have shown its benefits for linear-chain CRFs, the existing method for computing the entropy gradient is far more computationally expensive than standard supervised CRF training. improving pointwise mutual information ( pmi ) by incorporating
we design a new co-occurrence based word association measure by incorporating the concept of significant cooccurrence in the popular word association measure pointwise mutual information ( pmi ) . by extensive experiments with a large number of publicly available datasets we show that the newly introduced measure performs better than other co-occurrence based measures and despite being resource-light , compares well with the best known resource-heavy distributional similarity and knowledge based word association measures . we investigate the source of this performance improvement and find that of the two types of significant co-occurrence - corpus-level and document-level , the concept of corpus level significance combined with the use of document counts in place of word counts is responsible for all the performance gains observed . the concept of document level significance is not helpful for pmi adaptation .

domain adaptation via pseudo in-domain data selection
we explore efficient domain adaptation for the task of statistical machine translation based on extracting sentences from a large generaldomain parallel corpus that are most relevant to the target domain . these sentences may be selected with simple cross-entropy based methods , of which we present three . as these sentences are not themselves identical to the in-domain data , we call them pseudo in-domain subcorpora . these subcorpora 1 % the size of the original can then used to train small domain-adapted statistical machine translation ( smt ) systems which outperform systems trained on the entire corpus . performance is further improved when we use these domain-adapted models in combination with a true in-domain model . the results show that more training data is not always better , and that best results are attained via proper domain-relevant data selection , as well as combining in- and general-domain systems during decoding .
human1: An effective domain adaptation method for statistical machine translation (SMT) is examined in this work. The main concept is to create "pseudo in-domain subcorpora" by selecting the most pertinent sentences from a sizable general-domain parallel corpus. They show that SMT systems trained on these smaller pseudo-in-domain subcorpora can perform better than those trained on the full general-domain corpus, even if they are only 1% of the original corpus size. Additionally, when these domain-adapted models are combined with a true in-domain model during decoding, performance is further improved. The results show that the best results are achieved by carefully selecting domain-relevant data and strategically combining in-domain and general-domain systems during decoding, rather than by simply having a large amount of training data.

concreteness and corpora : a theoretical and practical analysis
an increasing body of empirical evidence suggests that concreteness is a fundamental dimension of semantic representation . by implementing both a vector space model and a latent dirichlet allocation ( lda ) model , we explore the extent to which concreteness is reflected in the distributional patterns in corpora . in one experiment , we show that that vector space models can be tailored to better model semantic domains of particular degrees of concreteness . in a second experiment , we show that the quality of the representations of abstract words in lda models can be improved by supplementing the training data with information on the physical properties of concrete concepts . we conclude by discussing the implications for computational systems and also for how concrete and abstract concepts are represented in the mind .

ranking help message candidates based on robust grammar verification results and utterance history in spoken dialogue systems
we address an issue of out-of-grammar ( oog ) utterances in spoken dialogue systems by generating help messages for novice users . help generation for oog utterances is a challenging problem because language understanding ( lu ) results based on automatic speech recognition ( asr ) results for such utterances are always erroneous as important words are often misrecognized or missed from such utterances . we first develop grammar verification for oog utterances on the basis of a weighted finite-state transducer ( wfst ) . it robustly identifies a grammar rule that a user intends to utter , even when some important words are missed from the asr result . we then adopt a ranking algorithm , rankboost , whose features include the grammar verification results and the utterance history representing the users experience .
human1: In order to address the issue of out-of-grammar (OOG) utterances in spoken dialogue systems, this paper suggests a technique for producing suitable help messages for inexperienced users. The authors first create a strong grammar verification system using a Weighted Finite-State Transducer (WFST) in order to address this. Even when important words are missing from the ASR output, this system can accurately determine the grammar rule a user intended. After that, they employ RankBoost, a ranking algorithm whose features include these reliable grammar verification results as well as the user's utterance history, which acts as a stand-in for experience level.

dynamic knowledge-base alignment for coreference resolution
coreference resolution systems can benefit greatly from inclusion of global context , and a number of recent approaches have demonstrated improvements when precomputing an alignment to external knowledge sources . however , since alignment itself is a challenging task and is often noisy , existing systems either align conservatively , resulting in very few links , or combine the attributes of multiple candidates , leading to a conflation of entities . our approach instead performs joint inference between within-document coreference and entity linking , maintaining ranked lists of candidate entities that are dynamically merged and reranked during inference . further , we incorporate a large set of surface string variations for each entity by using anchor texts from the web that link to the entity . these forms of global context enables our system to improve classifier-based coreference by 1.09 b3 f1 points , and improve over the previous state-of-art by 0.41 points , thus introducing a new state-of-art result on the ace 2004 data .
human1: By utilising global context from external knowledge sources and incorporating dynamic knowledge-base alignment, this paper suggests a novel method for coreference resolution. Although earlier approaches try to pre-computation of alignment, they frequently suffer from noise, which results in entity conflation (combining several candidates) or conservative linking (few links). The method used by the authors combines entity linking and within-document coreference to perform joint inference. Throughout the inference process, it keeps track of ranked lists of potential entities that are dynamically combined and reranked. Additionally, it includes an extensive collection of surface string variations for every entity, which are sourced from web anchor texts that connect to those entities. Their system surpasses the previous state-of-the-art by 0.41 points and improves classifier-based coreference performance by 1.09 B3 F1 points thanks to this rich global context, setting a new state-of-the-art result on the ACE 2004 data.

Human 2: Coreference resolution can be significantly improved by leveraging global contextual information, and several recent methods have shown gains by aligning mentions to external knowledge bases in advance. However, because the alignment step is itself difficult and often noisy, current systems either take an overly cautious approach yielding very few alignments or merge attributes from multiple possible entities, which can blur distinctions between them. Our method instead performs joint inference over within-document coreference and entity linking, keeping ranked lists of candidate entities that are continuously updated and re-ranked throughout the inference process. We further enhance each entity’s representation by incorporating a wide range of surface-form variants collected from web anchor texts. These sources of global context allow our system to boost a classifier-based coreference model by 1.09 B³ F1 points and to surpass the previous state-of-the-art on the ACE 2004 dataset by 0.41 points.

improving mention detection robustness to noisy input
information-extraction ( ie ) research typically focuses on clean-text inputs . however , an ie engine serving real applications yields many false alarms due to less-well-formed input . for example , ie in a multilingual broadcast processing system has to deal with inaccurate automatic transcription and translation . the resulting presence of non-target-language text in this case , and non-language material interspersed in data from other applications , raise the research problem of making ie robust to such noisy input text . we address one such ie task : entity-mention detection . we describe augmenting a statistical mention-detection system in order to reduce false alarms from spurious passages . the diverse nature of input noise leads us to pursue a multi-faceted approach to robustness . for our english-language system , at various miss rates we eliminate 97 % of false alarms on inputs from other latin-alphabet languages . in another experiment , representing scenarios in which genre-specific training is infeasible , we process real financial-transactions text containing mixed languages and data-set codes . on these data , because we do not train on data like it , we achieve a smaller but significant improvement .

Even though IE research frequently assumes clean text, many false alarms are caused by noisy input. Entity-mention detection as an IE task is the specific focus of the study. To lessen false alarms brought on by erroneous passages, the authors suggest enhancing a statistical mention-detection system. They take a multifaceted approach to achieve robustness because input noise can take many different forms. Experiments indicate that, even at different miss rates, they can eliminate 97% of false alarms on inputs from other Latin alphabet languages for their English-language system. In a different experiment, they processed real financial transaction text with mixed languages and dataset codes to simulate situations where genre-specific training is not feasible. chunk parsing revisited
chunk parsing is conceptually appealing but its performance has not been satisfactory for practical use . in this paper we show that chunk parsing can perform significantly better than previously reported by using a simple slidingwindow method and maximum entropy classifiers for phrase recognition in each level of chunking . experimental results with the penn treebank corpus show that our chunk parser can give high-precision parsing outputs with very high speed ( 14 msec/sentence ) . we also present a parsing method for searching the best parse by considering the probabilities output by the maximum entropy classifiers , and show that the search method can further improve the parsing accuracy .

Their chunk parser produces high-precision parsing outputs at a very high speed (14 milliseconds per sentence), according to experimental results on the Penn Treebank corpus. The study also presents a parsing technique that uses the probabilities produced by the maximum entropy classifiers to find the best parse, demonstrating that this search technique can improve parsing accuracy even more.

we propose a method for extracting semantic orientations of words : desirable or undesirable . regarding semantic orientations as spins of electrons , we use the mean field approximation to compute the approximate probability function of the system instead of the intractable actual probability function . we also propose a criterion for parameter selection on the basis of magnetization . given only a small number of seed words , the proposed method extracts semantic orientations with high accuracy in the experiments on english lexicon . the result is comparable to the best value ever reported .

The authors show that their approach extracts English semantic orientations with high accuracy, even with a limited set of seed words, and achieves results that are on par with the best values previously published.

public dialogue : analysis of tolerance in online discussions
social media platforms have enabled people to freely express their views and discuss issues of interest with others . while it is important to discover the topics in discussions , it is equally useful to mine the nature of such discussions or debates and the behavior of the participants . there are many questions that can be asked . one key question is whether the participants give reasoned arguments with justifiable claims via constructive debates or exhibit dogmatism and egotistic clashes of ideologies . the central idea of this question is tolerance , which is a key concept in the field of communications . in this work , we perform a computational study of tolerance in the context of online discussions . we aim to identify tolerant vs. intolerant participants and investigate how disagreement affects tolerance in discussions in a quantitative framework . to the best of our knowledge , this is the first such study . our experiments using real-life discussions demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed technique and also provide some key insights into the psycholinguistic phenomenon of tolerance in online discussions .
