extracting salient keywords from instructional videos using joint text ,
this paper presents a multi-modal featurebased system for extracting salient keywords from transcripts of instructional videos . specifically , we propose to extract domain-specific keywords for videos by integrating various cues from linguistic and statistical knowledge , as well as derived sound classes and characteristic visual content types . the acquisition of such salient keywords will facilitate video indexing and browsing , and significantly improve the quality of current video search engines . experiments on four government instructional videos show that 82 % of the salient keywords appear in the top 50 % of the highly ranked keywords . in addition , the audiovisual cues improve precision and recall by 1.1 % and 1.5 % respectively .

we built an open-source software platform intended to serve as a common infrastructure that can be of use in the development of new applications involving the processing of turkish . the platform incorporates a lexicon , a morphological analyzer/generator , and a dcg parser/generator that translates turkish sentences to predicate logic formulas , and a knowledge base framework . several developers have already utilized the platform for a variety of applications , including conversation programs and an artificial personal assistant , tools for automatic analysis of rhyme and meter in turkish folk poems , a prototype sentence-level translator between albanian , turkish , and english , natural language interfaces for generating sql queries and java code , as well as a text tagger used for collecting statistics about turkish morpheme order for a speech recognition algorithm . the results indicate the adaptability of the infrastructure to different kinds of applications and how it facilitates improvements and modifications .

this paper explores an automatic wordnet synset assignment to the bi-lingual dictionaries of languages having limited lexicon information . generally , a term in a bilingual dictionary is provided with very limited information such as part-of-speech , a set of synonyms , and a set of english equivalents . this type of dictionary is comparatively reliable and can be found in an electronic form from various publishers . in this paper , we propose an algorithm for applying a set of criteria to assign a synset with an appropriate degree of confidence to the existing bi-lingual dictionary . we show the efficiency in nominating the synset candidate by using the most common lexical information . the algorithm is evaluated against the implementation of thaienglish , indonesian-english , and mongolian-english bi-lingual dictionaries . the experiment also shows the effectiveness of using the same type of dictionary from different sources .

Such dictionaries typically contain only basic information for each entry part-of-speech labels, a small set of synonyms, and one or more English equivalents. Despite their simplicity, these dictionaries are generally reliable and widely available in electronic form from multiple publishers. We propose an algorithm that applies several criteria to select the most appropriate synset for each entry, along with a confidence estimate. a novel word segmentation approach for written languages with word boundary markers and hae-chang rim
most nlp applications work under the assumption that a user input is error-free ; thus , word segmentation ( ws ) for written languages that use word boundary markers ( wbms ) , such as spaces , has been regarded as a trivial issue . however , noisy real-world texts , such as blogs , e-mails , and sms , may contain spacing errors that require correction before further processing may take place . for the korean language , many researchers have adopted a traditional ws approach , which eliminates all spaces in the user input and re-inserts proper word boundaries . unfortunately , such an approach often exacerbates the word spacing quality for user input , which has few or no spacing errors ; such is the case , because a perfect ws model does not exist . in this paper , we propose a novel ws method that takes into consideration the initial word spacing information of the user input . our method generates a better output than the original user input , even if the user input has few spacing errors . moreover , the proposed method significantly outperforms a state-of-the-art korean ws model when the user input initially contains less than 10 % spacing errors , and performs comparably for cases containing more spacing errors . we believe that the proposed method will be a very practical pre-processing module .
human1: The problem of word segmentation (WS) in written languages that use word boundary markers (WBMs), like spaces, is frequently disregarded. This paper attempts to address this issue. For languages like Korean, traditional WS techniques usually entail eliminating all initial spaces before re-inserting the appropriate word boundaries. The authors suggest a brand-new WS technique that takes into account the user's initial word spacing data. Even when there are only minor spacing errors in the original user input, their method consistently yields better results. Additionally, for inputs with initial spacing errors of less than 10%, the suggested approach performs noticeably better than a state-of-the-art Korean WS model, and for inputs with more significant errors, it performs similarly. The authors come to the conclusion that their approach provides a very useful pre-processing module.

Human 2: Many NLP systems assume that user input is free of mistakes, which leads to the belief that word segmentation (WS) in languages that use word-boundary markers (WBMs), such as spaces, is trivial. In reality, however, noisy text from sources like blogs, emails, and SMS messages often contains spacing errors that must be corrected before further processing. For Korean, a common strategy has been to remove all spaces in the input and then reinsert word boundaries, but this approach can actually worsen spacing quality when the original text contains few or no errors, since no WS model is perfect. In this paper, we introduce a new WS technique that preserves and leverages the initial spacing patterns in the user’s input. Our method produces output that improves on the original text even when the input contains only minor spacing errors. Moreover, it significantly outperforms a state-of-the-art Korean WS system when spacing errors are under 10%, and performs similarly when the error rate is higher. We argue that this method is a highly practical preprocessing step.

automatic evaluation of linguistic quality in multi-document
to date , few attempts have been made to develop and validate methods for automatic evaluation of linguistic quality in text summarization . we present the first systematic assessment of several diverse classes of metrics designed to capture various aspects of well-written text . we train and test linguistic quality models on consecutive years of nist evaluation data in order to show the generality of results . for grammaticality , the best results come from a set of syntactic features . focus , coherence and referential clarity are best evaluated by a class of features measuring local coherence on the basis of cosine similarity between sentences , coreference information , and summarization specific features . our best results are 90 % accuracy for pairwise comparisons of competing systems over a test set of several inputs and 70 % for ranking summaries of a specific input .

In this paper, we provide the first comprehensive evaluation of several distinct families of metrics aimed at capturing different properties of well-formed text. improved tree-to-string transducer for machine translation
we propose three enhancements to the treeto-string ( tts ) transducer for machine translation : first-level expansion-based normalization for tts templates , a syntactic alignment framework integrating the insertion of unaligned target words , and subtree-based ngram model addressing the tree decomposition probability . empirical results show that these methods improve the performance of a tts transducer based on the standard bleu4 metric . we also experiment with semantic labels in a tts transducer , and achieve improvement over our baseline system .

parallel active learning : eliminating wait time with minimal staleness
a practical concern for active learning ( al ) is the amount of time human experts must wait for the next instance to label . we propose a method for eliminating this wait time independent of specific learning and scoring algorithms by making scores always available for all instances , using old ( stale ) scores when necessary . the time during which the expert is annotating is used to train models and score instancesin parallelto maximize the recency of the scores . our method can be seen as a parameterless , dynamic batch al algorithm . we analyze the amount of staleness introduced by various al schemes and then examine the effect of the staleness on performance on a part-of-speech tagging task on the wall street journal . empirically , the parallel al algorithm effectively has a batch size of one and a large candidate set size but eliminates the time an annotator would have to wait for a similarly parameterized batch scheme to select instances . the exact performance of our method on other tasks will depend on the relative ratios of time spent annotating , training , and scoring , but in general we expect our parameterless method to perform favorably compared to batch when accounting for wait time .

The main concept is to maximise the recency of the scores by using the time an expert spends annotating to train models and score instances concurrently. This method is described as a dynamic batch AL algorithm that is parameterless. Human 2: Active learning often suffers from a practical issue: human annotators must wait for the system to select the next item to be labeled. We present an approach that removes this waiting period, regardless of the learning or scoring method being used. The key idea is to maintain scores for every instance at all times, relying on older scores when updated ones are not yet ready. While the annotator works, the system trains models and assigns new scores to data in parallel so that the available scores remain as up to date as possible. This approach can be viewed as a dynamic and parameter free form of batch active learning. We first measure how much staleness different active learning strategies introduce, and then study how this staleness affects performance on a part of speech tagging task using data from the Wall Street Journal. In practice, our parallel method behaves as though it has a batch size of one and a very large candidate pool, yet it removes the waiting time associated with a traditional batch based selection strategy. Performance on other applications will depend on the relative time required for annotation, training, and scoring, but overall we expect this parameter free method to compare favorably with batch approaches once wait time is taken into account.

improving english-spanish statistical machine translation : experiments in
we describe the experiments of the uc berkeley team on improving english-spanish machine translation of news text , as part of the wmt08 shared translation task . we experiment with domain adaptation , combining a small in-domain news bi-text and a large out-of-domain one from the europarl corpus , building two separate phrase translation models and two separate language models . we further add a third phrase translation model trained on a version of the news bi-text augmented with monolingual sentencelevel syntactic paraphrases on the sourcelanguage side , and we combine all models in a log-linear model using minimum error rate training . finally , we experiment with different tokenization and recasing rules , achieving 35.09 % bleu score on the wmt07 news test data when translating from english to spanish , which is a sizable improvement over the highest bleu score achieved on that dataset at wmt07 : 33.10 % ( in fact , by our system ) . on the wmt08 english to spanish news translation , we achieve 21.92 % , which makes our team the second best on bleu score .

sentiment vector space model for lyric-based song sentiment classification
lyric-based song sentiment classification seeks to assign songs appropriate sentiment labels such as light-hearted and heavy-hearted . four problems render vector space model ( vsm ) -based text classification approach ineffective : 1 ) many words within song lyrics actually contribute little to sentiment ; 2 ) nouns and verbs used to express sentiment are ambiguous ; 3 ) negations and modifiers around the sentiment keywords make particular contributions to sentiment ; 4 ) song lyric is usually very short . to address these problems , the sentiment vector space model ( s-vsm ) is proposed to represent song lyric document . the preliminary experiments prove that the svsm model outperforms the vsm model in the lyric-based song sentiment classification task .

The Sentiment Vector Space Model (S-VSM), a novel approach to representing song lyric documents, is proposed in the paper as a solution to these problems. image description using visual dependency representations
describing the main event of an image involves identifying the objects depicted and predicting the relationships between them . previous approaches have represented images as unstructured bags of regions , which makes it difficult to accurately predict meaningful relationships between regions . in this paper , we introduce visual dependency representations to capture the relationships between the objects in an image , and hypothesize that this representation can improve image description . we test this hypothesis using a new data set of region-annotated images , associated with visual dependency representations and gold-standard descriptions . we describe two template-based description generation models that operate over visual dependency representations . in an image description task , we find that these models outperform approaches that rely on object proximity or corpus information to generate descriptions on both automatic measures and on human judgements .

inducing information extraction systems for new languages via cross-language projection
information extraction ( ie ) systems are costly to build because they require development texts , parsing tools , and specialized dictionaries for each application domain and each natural language that needs to be processed . we present a novel method for rapidly creating ie systems for new languages by exploiting existing ie systems via crosslanguage projection . given an ie system for a source language ( e.g. , english ) , we can transfer its annotations to corresponding texts in a target language ( e.g. , french ) and learn information extraction rules for the new language automatically . in this paper , we explore several ways of realizing both the transfer and learning processes using off-theshelf machine translation systems , induced word alignment , attribute projection , and transformationbased learning . we present a variety of experiments that show how an english ie system for a plane crash domain can be leveraged to automatically create a french ie system for the same domain .

The main concept is to transfer annotations from an existing Internet Explorer system in a source language (like English) to corresponding texts in a target language (like French). linguistically motivated complementizer choice in surface realization

the back-translation score : automatic mt evaluation at the sentence level without reference translations
automatic tools for machine translation ( mt ) evaluation such as bleu are well established , but have the drawbacks that they do not per-form well at the sentence level and that they presuppose manually translated reference texts . assuming that the mt system to be evaluated can deal with both directions of a language pair , in this research we suggest to conduct automatic mt evaluation by determining the orthographic similarity between a back-trans-lation and the original source text . this way we eliminate the need for human translated reference texts . by correlating bleu and back-translation scores with human judg-ments , it could be shown that the back-translation score gives an improved perfor-mance at the sentence level
human1: In this paper, a novel approach to automatic machine translation (MT) evaluation at the sentence level without the need for human-translated reference texts is presented: the "back-translation score." Although well-established, traditional automatic MT evaluation tools such as BLEU have two major shortcomings: they require manually translated reference texts and perform poorly at the sentence level. This study suggests assessing MT systems by utilising their capacity to translate in both directions of a language pair in order to get around these restrictions. Measuring the orthographic similarity between the original source text and a back-translation a translation from the target language back to the original source language is the main concept. The authors show that this new method outperforms existing methods such as BLEU at the sentence level by comparing back-translation scores with human judgements.

Experiments comparing these back-translation–based scores with BLEU and with human assessments show that this technique offers stronger reliability for evaluating individual sentences.

using inverse and generalization to translate english
we present a system to translate natural language sentences to formulas in a formal or a knowledge representation language . our system uses two inverse -calculus operators and using them can take as input the semantic representation of some words , phrases and sentences and from that derive the semantic representation of other words and phrases . our inverse operator works on many formal languages including first order logic , database query languages and answer set programming . our system uses a syntactic combinatorial categorial parser to parse natural language sentences and also to construct the semantic meaning of the sentences as directed by their parsing . the same parser is used for both . in addition to the inverse -calculus operators , our system uses a notion of generalization to learn semantic representation of words from the semantic representation of other words that are of the same category . together with this , we use an existing statistical learning approach to assign weights to deal with multiple meanings of words . our system produces improved results on standard corpora on natural language interfaces for robot command and control and database queries .

The updates include a Part-of-Speech (POS)-based word reordering strategy, the integration of larger statistical models, and improvements in data processing consistency. Chinese-English translation presents unique reordering challenges due to significant differences in syntactic structure; the POS-based method offers a lightweight and fast alternative to more complex syntax tree-based or neural reordering techniques while still capturing key ordering patterns. These improvements contribute to the system’s strong performance in the 2008 GALE evaluation, where it demonstrated competitive translation quality compared to state-of-the-art systems. This work highlights the continued relevance of statistical and linguistic features in enhancing SMT for challenging language pairs like Chinese–English.

using word dependent transition models in hmm based word alignment for statistical machine translation
in this paper , we present a bayesian learning based method to train word dependent transition models for hmm based word alignment . we present word alignment results on the canadian hansards corpus as compared to the conventional hmm and ibm model 4. we show that this method gives consistent and significant alignment error rate ( aer ) reduction . we also conducted machine translation ( mt ) experiments on the europarl corpus . mt results show that word alignment based on this method can be used in a phrase-based machine translation system to yield up to 1 % absolute improvement in bleu score , compared to a conventional hmm , and 0.8 % compared to a ibm model 4 based word alignment .
human1: In order to train word-dependent transition models within HMM-based word alignment for statistical machine translation, this paper presents a Bayesian learning-based approach. The authors compare their approach to traditional HMM and IBM Model 4 and present word alignment results on the Canadian Hansards corpus. They show that their method consistently and significantly lowers the Alignment Error Rate (AER). The paper also describes machine translation (MT) experiments on the Europarl corpus. According to the MT results, their suggested approach for word alignment can be incorporated into a phrase-based machine translation system, improving the absolute BLEU score by up to 1% when compared to a traditional HMM and 0.8% when compared to an IBM Model 4-based word alignment.

Source:
annotating corpus data for a quantitative , constructional analysis of motion verbs in modern standard arabic
this article proposes an annotation method of corpus data for the purposes of providing a constructionist account of lexical behavior . the lexical items in question are seven verbs of motion in modern standard arabic that pertain to the events of come ( at , a , aara , and qadima ) and go ( ahaba , ma , and ra ) . the tag set selected for the annotation of the come and go data frames consists of morphosyntactic tags that characterize verb usage as well as semantic tags that aim to highlight the semantic component of , for instance , adverbial and adpositional phrases that accompany the verb . i will briefly demonstrate the analytical potential of such data frame by discussing the various kinds of statistical tests such data frame is designed to undergo , as a means of better understanding lexical behavior in context , and , eventually , arriving at a better understanding of lexical and constructional choices made by native speakers of arabic , as demonstrated in corpora .

By better understanding how words behave in context, this method seeks to better understand the word and construction choices made by native Arabic speakers in written texts.

lexical computing ltd

streaming for large scale nlp : language modeling
in this paper , we explore a streaming algorithm paradigm to handle large amounts of data for nlp problems . we present an efficient low-memory method for constructing high-order approximate n-gram frequency counts . the method is based on a deterministic streaming algorithm which efficiently computes approximate frequency counts over a stream of data while employing a small memory footprint . we show that this method easily scales to billion-word monolingual corpora using a conventional ( 8 gb ram ) desktop machine . statistical machine translation experimental results corroborate that the resulting high-n approximate small language model is as effective as models obtained from other count pruning methods .
human1: In order to handle massive datasets for Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, this paper explores a streaming algorithm paradigm. An effective, low-memory technique for producing high-order approximate n-gram frequency counts is presented by the authors. The deterministic streaming algorithm used in this technique effectively computes approximate frequency counts across a data stream with a minimal memory footprint. They show that with a typical desktop computer (8 GB RAM), this method can readily scale to billion-word monolingual corpora. The resulting high-N approximate small language model is as effective as models derived from other count pruning techniques, as confirmed by statistical machine translation experiments.

constructing a turkish-english parallel treebank olcay taner yldz and razieh ehsani
in this paper , we report our preliminary efforts in building an english-turkish parallel treebank corpus for statistical machine translation . in the corpus , we manually generated parallel trees for about 5,000 sentences from penn treebank . english sentences in our set have a maximum of 15 tokens , including punctuation . we constrained the translated trees to the reordering of the children and the replacement of the leaf nodes with appropriate glosses . we also report the tools that we built and used in our tree translation task .

a generative entity-mention model for linking entities
linking entities with knowledge base ( entity linking ) is a key issue in bridging the textual data with the structural knowledge base . due to the name variation problem and the name ambiguity problem , the entity linking decisions are critically depending on the heterogenous knowledge of entities . in this paper , we propose a generative probabilistic model , called entitymention model , which can leverage heterogenous entity knowledge ( including popularity knowledge , name knowledge and context knowledge ) for the entity linking task . in our model , each name mention to be linked is modeled as a sample generated through a three-step generative story , and the entity knowledge is encoded in the distribution of entities in document p ( e ) , the distribution of possible names of a specific entity p ( s|e ) , and the distribution of possible contexts of a specific entity p ( c|e ) . to find the referent entity of a name mention , our method combines the evidences from all the three distributions p ( e ) , p ( s|e ) and p ( c|e ) . experimental results show that our method can significantly outperform the traditional methods .
human1: This paper discusses entity linking, a critical task that is made more difficult by name ambiguities and variations when connecting textual data to structured knowledge bases. Diverse entity knowledge is crucial to the accuracy of entity linking decisions. The entity-mention model is a generative probabilistic model that the authors suggest. For the entity linking task, this model is made to take advantage of heterogeneous entity knowledge, which includes name, context, and popularity information. Their model conceptualises each name mention that needs to be linked as a sample produced by a three-step generative process. Three distributions P(E) (the distribution of entities in a document), P(S∣E) (the distribution of potential names for a particular entity), and P(C∣E) (the distribution of potential contexts for a particular entity) are used to encode entity knowledge. Their approach integrates evidence from the three distributions P(E), P(S∣E), and P(C∣E) to determine the referent entity for a name mention. According to experimental results, their approach performs noticeably better than conventional methods.

predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key features of the model?
Human 2: This paper discusses an approach to entity linking, although the authors frame the problem somewhat differently from standard treatments. Instead of relying primarily on surface forms or database lookups, they outline a generative framework that imagines mentions in text as being produced through several intermediate steps, some of which are only loosely tied to actual usage patterns. The model is designed to draw on multiple sources of entity information, but in practice the balance between popularity, naming conventions, and contextual evidence varies depending on the corpus, and the paper notes that certain entities exhibit distributions that the model cannot represent cleanly. Each textual mention is treated as if it originated from a simplified three-stage narrative, but the authors acknowledge that many real-world mentions do not follow this pattern. Their system combines signals from several probability distributions tied to entities and then selects the most plausible match, though the decision criteria sometimes conflict. Despite these inconsistencies, experimental trials show improvements over some baseline techniques, particularly on datasets where ambiguous names are relatively rare.

machine transliteration : leveraging on third languages
this paper presents two pivot strategies for statistical machine transliteration , namely system-based pivot strategy and model-based pivot strategy . given two independent source-pivot and pivot-target name pair corpora , the model-based strategy learns a direct sourcetarget transliteration model while the system-based strategy learns a sourcepivot model and a pivot-target model , respectively . experimental results on benchmark data show that the systembased pivot strategy is effective in reducing the high resource requirement of training corpus for low-density language pairs while the model-based pivot strategy performs worse than the system-based one .
human1: The system-based pivot strategy and the model-based pivot strategy are the two pivot strategies for statistical machine transliteration that are presented in this paper. Two separate collections of source-pivot and pivot-target name pairs are provided: A direct source-target transliteration model is learnt by the model-based approach; A source-pivot model and a distinct pivot-target model are learnt by the system-based approach. The system-based pivot approach effectively lowers the high resource requirements of training corpora for low-density language pairs, according to experimental results on benchmark data. Conversely, the system-based pivot strategy outperformed the model-based one.

Human 2: This study examines transliteration across languages using an indirect, or “pivot,” setup, but the authors note that the methods they tested behave differently depending on the structure of the training data. They investigate two approaches: one that builds separate transliteration systems for each leg of the path through an intermediate language, and another that attempts to merge the information from both corpora into a single source–target model. Although both rely on paired name data from different language combinations, the paper reports that the integrated strategy often suffered from inconsistencies in the pivot corpora, occasionally producing mismatched character sequences. In contrast, the two-stage system, which first maps names into the pivot language and then into the final language, proved more stable, even when the training material for the low-resource languages was limited or uneven. Tests on standard datasets suggest that the simpler two-step method delivers more reliable results, while the unified model tended to degrade when corpus coverage was sparse.

lightly supervised transliteration for machine translation
we present a hebrew to english transliteration method in the context of a machine translation system . our method uses machine learning to determine which terms are to be transliterated rather than translated . the training corpus for this purpose includes only positive examples , acquired semi-automatically . our classifier reduces more than 38 % of the errors made by a baseline method . the identified terms are then transliterated . we present an smtbased transliteration model trained with a parallel corpus extracted from wikipedia using a fairly simple method which requires minimal knowledge . the correct result is produced in more than 76 % of the cases , and in 92 % of the instances it is one of the top-5 results . we also demonstrate a small improvement in the performance of a hebrew-to-english mt system that uses our transliteration module .
human1: This work presents a transliteration technique for Hebrew to English that is minimally supervised and intended for incorporation into a machine translation (MT) system. To decide which terms should be transliterated instead of translated directly, the method makes use of machine learning. Only positive examples, obtained semi-automatically, make up the classifier's training corpus. When compared to a baseline approach, this classifier shows a notable improvement, lowering errors by more than 38%. An SMT-based transliteration model processes terms after they have been selected for transliteration. A parallel corpus taken from Wikipedia is used to train this model in a comparatively straightforward process that calls for little specialised knowledge. In more than 76% of cases, the transliteration model produces the right result, and in 92% of cases, the correct transliteration ranks among the top 5 results. Lastly, the authors demonstrate how incorporating their transliteration module improves a Hebrew-to-English MT system's overall performance in a minor but noticeable way.

Human 2: This paper describes an approach for handling Hebrew terms in a translation pipeline, but the method focuses more on deciding when transliteration is appropriate than on improving the transliteration process itself. Incorporating this module into a full Hebrew English MT system leads to small but measurable gains, though the improvements vary considerably across different test sets.

the stanford corenlp natural language processing toolkit
we describe the design and use of the stanford corenlp toolkit , an extensible pipeline that provides core natural language analysis . this toolkit is quite widely used , both in the research nlp community and also among commercial and government users of open source nlp technology . we suggest that this follows from a simple , approachable design , straightforward interfaces , the inclusion of robust and good quality analysis components , and not requiring use of a large amount of associated baggage .

Source:
building a training corpus for word sense disambiguation in english-to-vietnamese machine translation
the most difficult task in machine translation is the elimination of ambiguity in human languages . a certain word in english as well as vietnamese often has different meanings which depend on their syntactical position in the sentence and the actual context . in order to solve this ambiguation , formerly , people used to resort to many hand-coded rules . nevertheless , manually building these rules is a time-consuming and exhausting task . so , we suggest an automatic method to solve the above-mentioned problem by using semantically tagged corpus . in this paper , we mainly present building a semantically tagged bilingual corpus to word sense disambiguation ( wsd ) in english texts . to assign semantic tags , we have taken advantage of bilingual texts via word alignments with semantic class names of lloce ( longman lexicon of contemporary english ) . so far , we have built 5,000,000-word bilingual corpus in which 1,000,000 words have been semantically annotated with the accuracy of 70 % . we have evaluated our result of semantic tagging by comparing with semcor on susanne part of our corpus . this semantically annotated corpus will be used to extract disambiguation rules automatically by tbl ( transformationbased learning ) method .
human1: The substantial problem of ambiguity resolution in human languages for machine translation (MT), specifically for English-to-Vietnamese translation, is discussed in this paper. In both Vietnamese and English, a single word frequently has several meanings depending on its context and syntactic function. This ambiguity has traditionally been addressed with lengthy, hand-coded rules, which is a tedious and time-consuming procedure. The authors suggest an automatic technique that makes use of a semantically tagged corpus to get around this. The creation of a bilingual corpus with semantic tags specifically for Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) in English texts is the primary goal of the paper. To assign these semantic tags, they make use of bilingual texts and word alignments with semantic class names from the Longman Lexicon of Contemporary English (LLOCE). They have so far constructed a bilingual corpus of 5,000,000 words, of which 1,000,000 have been semantically annotated with 70% accuracy. On the Susanne portion of their corpus, their semantic tagging accuracy was assessed by contrasting it with SemCor. The purpose of this recently developed semantically annotated corpus is to automatically extract disambiguation rules using the Transformation-Based Learning (TBL) technique.

Human 2: This paper discusses efforts to assemble a bilingual English–Vietnamese dataset for resolving word-sense ambiguity, although the project grew beyond its original scope. While the authors acknowledge that ambiguity in both languages can depend on syntax and context, they point out that earlier systems relied on rule sets that were neither consistent nor easy to maintain, and some of those rules were later discarded. To avoid this manual effort, they propose building a large corpus in which segments of text receive semantic tags derived partly from alignment information and partly from external lexical resources, though the mapping between these resources is not always straightforward. The current version of the corpus contains several million words, but only a portion has undergone detailed annotation, and the quality varies depending on the genre of the source text. A preliminary comparison with SemCor suggests that their tagging approach performs reasonably well, though it struggles with idiomatic expressions and rare senses. The authors intend to use the annotated material as a base for learning disambiguation patterns automatically, but note that additional preprocessing steps will likely be required.

incremental text structuring with online hierarchical ranking
many emerging applications require documents to be repeatedly updated . such documents include newsfeeds , webpages , and shared community resources such as wikipedia . in this paper we address the task of inserting new information into existing texts . in particular , we wish to determine the best location in a text for a given piece of new information . for this process to succeed , the insertion algorithm should be informed by the existing document structure . lengthy real-world texts are often hierarchically organized into chapters , sections , and paragraphs . we present an online ranking model which exploits this hierarchical structure representationally in its features and algorithmically in its learning procedure . when tested on a corpus of wikipedia articles , our hierarchically informed model predicts the correct insertion paragraph more accurately than baseline methods .
human1: The difficulty of adding new content to frequently updated documents, like newsfeeds, webpages, and shared community resources like Wikipedia, is the subject of this paper. Finding the best place for a new piece of information within an existing text is the main task. The algorithm needs to be aware of the document's current structure in order for this insertion process to work. Long texts in the real world are frequently arranged hierarchically (chapters, sections, paragraphs, etc.). Both representationally (in its features) and algorithmically (in its learning process), the authors' online ranking model makes use of this hierarchical structure. Their hierarchically informed model outperforms baseline methods in predicting the correct insertion paragraph when tested on a corpus of Wikipedia articles.

community evaluation and exchange of word vectors
vector space word representations are useful for many natural language processing applications . the diversity of techniques for computing vector representations and the large number of evaluation benchmarks makes reliable comparison a tedious task both for researchers developing new vector space models and for those wishing to use them . we present a website and suite of offline tools that that facilitate evaluation of word vectors on standard lexical semantics benchmarks and permit exchange and archival by users who wish to find good vectors for their applications .

a new approach to improving multilingual summarization using a genetic algorithm
automated summarization methods can be defined as language-independent , if they are not based on any languagespecific knowledge . such methods can be used for multilingual summarization defined by mani ( 2001 ) as processing several languages , with summary in the same language as input . in this paper , we introduce muse , a languageindependent approach for extractive summarization based on the linear optimization of several sentence ranking measures using a genetic algorithm . we tested our methodology on two languagesenglish and hebrewand evaluated its performance with rouge-1 recall vs. stateof-the-art extractive summarization approaches . our results show that muse performs better than the best known multilingual approach ( textrank1 ) in both languages . moreover , our experimental results on a bilingual ( english and hebrew ) document collection suggest that muse does not need to be retrained on each language and the same model can be used across at least two different languages .
human1: MuSe (Multilingual Summarisation Engine), a novel language-independent method for extractive summarisation intended for multilingual contexts, is presented in this paper. According to Mani (2001), language-independent methods for multilingual summarisation process several languages and generate a summary in the same language as the input. MuSe accomplishes this by linearly optimising multiple sentence ranking metrics using a genetic algorithm. English and Hebrew were the two languages used for testing, and ROUGE-1 recall was used to compare the methodology's performance to cutting-edge extractive summarisation techniques. The findings show that MuSe performs better in both languages than TextRank, a well-known multilingual technique. Additionally, tests on a collection of bilingual (English and Hebrew) documents point to a key benefit: MuSe does not need to be retrained for every language, suggesting that the same model can be used successfully in at least two typologically distinct languages.

a cross-lingual ilp solution to zero anaphora resolution
we present an ilp-based model of zero anaphora detection and resolution that builds on the joint determination of anaphoricity and coreference model proposed by denis and baldridge ( 2007 ) , but revises it and extends it into a three-way ilp problem also incorporating subject detection . we show that this new model outperforms several baselines and competing models , as well as a direct translation of the denis / baldridge model , for both italian and japanese zero anaphora . we incorporate our model in complete anaphoric resolvers for both italian and japanese , showing that our approach leads to improved performance also when not used in isolation , provided that separate classifiers are used for zeros and for explicitly realized anaphors .

automatic temporal expression normalization with reference
temporal expressions in texts contain significant temporal information . understanding temporal information is very useful in many nlp applications , such as information extraction , documents summarization and question answering . therefore , the temporal expression normalization which is used for transforming temporal expressions to temporal information has absorbed many researchers attentions . but previous works , whatever the hand-crafted rules-based or the machine-learnt rules-based , all can not address the actual problem about temporal reference in real texts effectively . more specifically , the reference time choosing mechanism employed by these works is not adaptable to the universal implicit times in normalization . aiming at this issue , we introduce a new reference time choosing mechanism for temporal expression normalization , called reference time dynamic-choosing , which assigns the appropriate reference times to different classes of implicit temporal expressions dynamically when normalizing . and then , the solution to temporal expression defuzzification by scenario dependences among temporal expressions is discussed . finally , we evaluate the system on a substantial corpus collected by chinese news articles and obtained more promising results than compared methods .
human1: The automatic temporal expression normalisation task, which entails transforming textual temporal expressions into structured temporal information, is the focus of this paper. Many NLP applications, such as information extraction, document summarisation, and question answering, benefit from this comprehension of temporal information. Regardless of whether they are based on manually created or automatically learnt rules, the authors point out a significant drawback of earlier normalisation techniques: their incapacity to manage temporal referencesvparticularly universal implicit times in actual texts. In particular, these earlier works' reference time choosing mechanisms are insufficiently flexible. The paper presents "reference time dynamic-choosing," a novel reference time choosing mechanism, as a solution to this problem. Throughout the normalisation process, this mechanism dynamically allocates suitable reference times to various categories of implicit temporal expressions. The use of scenario dependencies among temporal expressions as a method for temporal expression defuzzification is also covered in the paper. When the system is finally tested on a sizable corpus of Chinese news articles, it performs better than similar approaches.

To address this problem, they describe a method that attempts to select a suitable reference time while the expression is being analyzed, although the procedure sometimes assigns unexpected anchors when the surrounding sentences are inconsistent. They also outline a simple strategy for dealing with vague or partially overlapping time expressions by examining how events relate to each other in a loose scenario structure. Tests on a collection of Chinese news reports indicate that the approach can improve performance compared to several existing systems, although there remain situations where conflicting temporal cues inside the articles still lead to uncertainty.

Source:
criterion for judging request intention in response texts of open-ended questionnaires
our general research aim is to extract the actual intentions of persons when they respond to open-ended questionnaires . these intentions include the desire to make requests , complaints , expressions of resignation and so forth , but here we focus on extracting the intention to make a request . to do so , we first have to judge whether their responses contain the intent to make a request . therefore , as a first step , we have developed a criterion for judging the existence of request intentions in responses . this criterion , which is based on paraphrasing , is described in detail in this paper . our assumption is that a response with request intentions can be paraphrased into a typical request expression , e.g. , i would like to ... , while responses without request are not paraphrasable . the criterion is evaluated in terms of objectivity , reproducibility and effectiveness . objectivity is demonstrated by showing that machine learning methods can learn the criterion from a set of intention-tagged data , while reproducibility , that the judgments of three annotators are reasonably consistent , and effectiveness , that judgments based not on the criterion but on intuition do not agree . this means the criterion is necessary to achieve reproducibility . these experiments indicate that the criterion can be used to judge the existence of request intentions in responses reliably .
human1:
The goal of this paper is to identify genuine intentions more especially, the intention to make a request in responses to open-ended questionnaires. The authors created a criterion for determining whether request intentions were present in these responses as an essential first step.
This criterion is based on paraphrasing and is explained in the paper. The fundamental premise is that responses with a request intention can be paraphrased into a standard request expression (e.g., "I would like to..."), while responses without a request intention cannot. Three factors are used to assess the criterion's efficacy: The ability of machine learning techniques to learn the criterion from intention-tagged data serves as an example of objectivity; Reasonable consistency in the conclusions reached by three distinct annotators is a sign of reproducibility; Effectiveness: Illustrated by the inconsistency in conclusions drawn solely from intuition, underscoring the need for reproducibility in the criterion. According to these tests, the developed criterion can accurately identify whether or not questionnaire responses contain request intentions.

5. What are the limitations of relying solely on intuition for intention tagging, and how does the criterion address them?
irrelevant_question: 5. What are the limitations of relying solely on intuition for intention tagging, and how does the criterion address them?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain the criterion's evaluation?
Human 2: This study examines how to identify when people are expressing a desire for something in written answers to open ended surveys, although the authors broaden the discussion to include other types of attitudes that respondents might reveal. While the long term goal is to recover a range of underlying intentions, the paper concentrates on detecting whether the writer is trying to make a request. To explore this, the researchers propose a guideline that attempts to decide if a response can reasonably be restated in the form of a typical request sentence. The idea is that if a comment can be rephrased into a familiar pattern such as a sentence beginning with a polite expression of desire, then it likely carries a request, while responses that cannot be restated in that way probably do not. They evaluate this idea by examining how well it can be learned by simple classification methods, by checking that several human annotators reach reasonably similar decisions, and by showing that judgments based only on intuition tend to disagree. The results suggest that following a clear guideline is important for consistent annotation and that the proposed criterion can serve as a reliable way to tell whether a request is present in a written response.

named entity recognition for dialectal arabic
to date , majority of research for arabic named entity recognition ( ner ) addresses the task for modern standard arabic ( msa ) and mainly focuses on the newswire genre . despite some common characteristics between msa and dialectal arabic ( da ) , the significant differences between the two language varieties hinder such msa specific systems from solving ner for dialectal arabic . in this paper , we present an ner system for da specifically focusing on the egyptian dialect ( egy ) . our system delivers 16 % improvement in f1-score over state-of-theart features .

The effectiveness of the authors' system for Egyptian Arabic NER in tackling the particular difficulties of dialectal Arabic is demonstrated by its 16% improvement in F1-score over the state-of-the-art features.

The study suggests that dedicated resources for dialects are necessary, since relying solely on models trained on formal text cannot adequately capture the forms used in everyday communication.

Source:
an overview of microsoft web n - gram corpus and applications
this document describes the properties and some applications of the microsoft web ngram corpus . the corpus is designed to have the following characteristics . first , in contrast to static data distribution of previous corpus releases , this n-gram corpus is made publicly available as an xml web service so that it can be updated as deemed necessary by the user community to include new words and phrases constantly being added to the web . secondly , the corpus makes available various sections of a web document , specifically , the body , title , and anchor text , as separates models as text contents in these sections are found to possess significantly different statistical properties and therefore are treated as distinct languages from the language modeling point of view . the usages of the corpus are demonstrated here in two nlp tasks : phrase segmentation and word breaking .
human1: An overview of the Microsoft Web N-gram Corpus is given in this document, along with examples of some of its uses and design elements. Two essential features are built into the corpus: Dynamic Availability: This n-gram corpus is made publicly accessible as an XML Web Service, in contrast to earlier static corpus releases. This enables the user community to make updates continuously, guaranteeing that new terms and expressions are added to the web on a regular basis. Section-Specific Models: The corpus provides distinct models for the body, title, and anchor text, which are the different parts of a web page. This design decision stems from the finding that the statistical characteristics of the text content in these various sections are noticeably different. Consequently, they are regarded as distinct "languages" from the standpoint of language modelling. The use of this corpus in two Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks phrase segmentation and word breaking showcases the usefulness of the corpus in practice.
llm_questions:
1. What limitations in previous static n-gram corpora does the Microsoft Web N-gram Corpus aim to overcome — lack of freshness, limited coverage, or rigidity in access?
2. Why is real-time access to dynamic language data important for NLP tasks — does it improve relevance, accuracy, or adaptability?
3. What motivated the creation of section-specific models — what statistical differences exist between body text, titles, and anchor text?
4. What makes treating sections as distinct "languages" effective from a modeling perspective?
5. What is the main contribution: the corpus itself, the API-based delivery, or its utility in downstream NLP tasks?
irrelevant_question: 5. What is the main contribution: the corpus itself, the API-based delivery, or its utility in downstream NLP tasks?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key features?
Human 2: This paper gives a description of a large collection of word sequence statistics assembled from web data by Microsoft, although the authors emphasize features that differ from earlier public resources. Instead of distributing the data as a fixed archive, the collection is accessed through an online service in a format based on XML, which allows the content to be revised and expanded when users request coverage for newer vocabulary. The corpus also separates different parts of a web page, such as the main text, the title, and the surrounding link text, because these sections often follow different linguistic patterns and cannot be treated as a single variety of writing.

Source:
unsupervised part of speech tagging using unambiguous substitutes from a statistical language model
we show that unsupervised part of speech tagging performance can be significantly improved using likely substitutes for target words given by a statistical language model . we choose unambiguous substitutes for each occurrence of an ambiguous target word based on its context . the part of speech tags for the unambiguous substitutes are then used to filter the entry for the target word in the wordtag dictionary . a standard hmm model trained using the filtered dictionary achieves 92.25 % accuracy on a standard 24,000 word corpus .
human1: This study shows that using likely word substitutes from a statistical language model significantly improves unsupervised Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging performance. Finding clear replacements for each occurrence of an ambiguous target word based on its context is the main goal. The entry for the target word in a word-tag dictionary is then refined using the known POS tags of these clear substitutes. On a typical 24,000-word corpus, a standard Hidden Markov Model (HMM) trained with this filtered dictionary demonstrated an astounding 92.25% accuracy. This suggests that unsupervised POS tagging can be successfully improved by identifying unambiguous substitutes using contextual information from statistical language models.
llm_questions:
1. Why is unsupervised POS tagging still an important problem — is it due to the lack of annotated corpora in many languages or domains?
2. What’s the core challenge with ambiguous words in unsupervised POS tagging — insufficient context, multiple likely tags, or dictionary noise?
3. How are the “likely substitutes” for a target word identified — is it based on n-gram probability, masked language models, or co-occurrence statistics?
4. Why are substitutes with known POS tags useful — do they provide disambiguation via similarity or contextual alignment?
5. What is novel here — the use of contextual substitution in a dictionary refinement step, or the integration into a standard HMM training pipeline?
irrelevant_question: 3. How are the “likely substitutes” for a target word identified — is it based on n-gram probability, masked language models, or co-occurrence statistics?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the importance of unambiguous substitutes to improve unsupervised POS tagging performance?
Human 2: This study explores a method for improving automatic tagging of word categories without relying on labeled training data, but the authors frame the idea somewhat differently from earlier approaches. Instead of attempting to infer the part of speech of an ambiguous word directly from its surface context, they first consult a statistical language model that produces alternative words that could fit the same environment. From these candidates, they select those that usually have only one grammatical role, although the criteria used for this selection sometimes vary. The grammatical category of the chosen substitute is then used to narrow the possible tags for the original ambiguous word, and this restricted set of options becomes part of the dictionary used by a simple sequence model. Experiments on a small corpus show that this procedure yields strong accuracy numbers, though the authors acknowledge that the technique behaves unpredictably when the language model generates substitutes that do not reflect normal usage patterns.

Source: in this paper we investigate the automatic acquisition of arabic multiword expressions ( mwe ) . we propose three complementary approaches to extract mwes from available data resources . the first approach relies on the correspondence asymmetries between arabic wikipedia titles and titles in 21 different languages . the second approach collects english mwes from princeton wordnet 3.0 , translates the collection into arabic using google translate , and utilizes different search engines to validate the output . the third uses lexical association measures to extract mwes from a large unannotated corpus . we experimentally explore the feasibility of each approach and measure the quality and coverage of the output against gold standards .
human1: Our research focuses on the automatic obtaining of Arabic multiword expressions, called MWE. Our approach involves three methods to obtain MWE words from the given data. The first method focuses on asymmetries between Arabic Wikipedia titles and their corresponding titles in 21 other languages. The second method involves collecting English multivalued expressions and changing from the Princeton WordNet 3.0 and translating them into Arabic MWE, and verifying these translations with different search engines. Our third method employs lexical association measures to extract MWEs directly from a large Arabic text corpus. We experimented with all three methods and compared the outputs of the approaches with the golden standards.

6.In the second approach, you mention gathering English MWE expressions from WordNet—can you clarify how you ensure the accuracy of the translations into Arabic?

predicted_question: The second method involves collecting English multivalued expressions and changing from the Princeton WordNet 3.0 and translating them into Arabic MWE, and verifying translations with different search engines.
Human 2: This paper examines several ways of identifying Arabic multiword expressions, although the authors note that the usefulness of each technique varies depending on the source of the data. One line of work looks at uneven correspondences between article titles in Arabic Wikipedia and the titles used in a large collection of other languages, even though many of the mismatches are caused by editorial differences rather than true linguistic properties. A second strategy begins with a list of English expressions drawn from Princeton WordNet, translates them into Arabic through an online service, and then checks their plausibility using a variety of search tools, although some of the returned phrases are influenced by regional spelling conventions. The final method scans a large unannotated text collection and applies several statistical association measures to select candidate expressions, but the authors point out that many high scoring strings are not actual multiword units. The study reports preliminary evaluations comparing the output of each method with curated reference lists and discusses the strengths and limitations of the three approaches.

Source: we study multi-source transfer parsing for resource-poor target languages ; specifically methods for target language adaptation of delexicalized discriminative graph-based dependency parsers . we first show how recent insights on selective parameter sharing , based on typological and language-family features , can be applied to a discriminative parser by carefully decomposing its model features . we then show how the parser can be relexicalized and adapted using unlabeled target language data and a learning method that can incorporate diverse knowledge sources through ambiguous labelings . in the latter scenario , we exploit two sources of knowledge : arc marginals derived from the base parser in a self-training algorithm , and arc predictions from multiple transfer parsers in an ensemble-training algorithm . our final model outperforms the state of the art in multi-source transfer parsing on 15 out of 16 evaluated languages .
human1: In this paper, we are concerned with the issue of doing language parsing using limited resources namely, we are interested in adapting existing parsers so that they run efficiently in handling these target languages. New approaches to the sharing of parameters and features, such as language families and typological characteristics, help us to make the parser more effective. Our work also demonstrates how the parser could then be relexicalized by being exposed to unlabeled data in the new language to modify its interpretation, with the advantage of introducing alternative sources of knowledge information, even when labels are ambiguous. We employ two categories of knowledge: arc marginals of the original parser, which is embedded utilising a self-training scheme, and arc predictions of several transfer parsers in an ensemble-training algorithm. Eventually, our model even surpasses the best existing method in multi-source transfer parsing, achieving the superior performance on 15 of the 16 tested languages.

Human 2: This work explores how to improve syntactic parsing for languages that have little annotated material, but the discussion focuses on combining information from several better resourced languages rather than relying on a single source. The authors begin by noting that certain structural features can be shared across related languages, although they also caution that these relationships are not always reflected cleanly in the features used by standard discriminative dependency parsers. By separating different groups of model features, they attempt to control which parameters are shared and which are kept specific to each language.

Source: a key problem in document classification and clustering is learning the similarity between documents . traditional approaches include estimating similarity between feature vectors of documents where the vectors are computed using tf-idf in the bag-of-words model . however , these approaches do not work well when either similar documents do not use the same vocabulary or the feature vectors are not estimated correctly . in this paper , we represent documents and keywords using multiple layers of connected graphs . we pose the problem of simultaneously learning similarity between documents and keyword weights as an edge-weight regularization problem over the different layers of graphs . unlike most feature weight learning algorithms , we propose an unsupervised algorithm in the proposed framework to simultaneously optimize similarity and the keyword weights . we extrinsically evaluate the performance of the proposed similarity measure on two different tasks , clustering and classification . the proposed similarity measure outperforms the similarity measure proposed by ( muthukrishnan et al , 2010 ) , a state-of-theart classification algorithm ( zhou and burges , 2007 ) and three different baselines on a variety of standard , large data sets .
human1: One of the major problems with automatic document classification and clustering is how to measure the similarity between documents. Traditional methods use feature vectors created with the tf-idf scheme in a bag-of-words model, but these methods do not scale effectively when similar documents do not use the same words or when feature vectors are not estimated well. Instead of crafting document and keyword representations based on only those traditional tools, we represent documents and keywords using multiple layers of connected graphs. The problem of learning the similarity of the document and the importance of the keywords is then solved by the edge-weight regularization in graph layers.. The advantage of our method is that, unlike most other algorithms that learn feature weights, our method is unsupervised and determines the similarity weights and the weights of our keywords simultaneously. We test this by comparing our measure of similarity to two tasks: clustering and classification, where we compare it to both existing (Muthukrishnan et al., 2010) and state-of-theart (Zhou and Burges, 2007) algorithms, and also several baseline models when using large datasets, and it outperforms all of them.

3. Are there any sentences that could be made more concise? For example, "Instead of crafting document and keyword representations based on only those traditional tools..." could be simplified for clarity.

6. When you say your method "outperforms all of them," could you provide more detail on the specific improvements? For example, is it more accurate, faster, or more scalable? Mentioning the metrics or benchmarks would strengthen this claim

Human 2: This paper investigates how to measure the relatedness of documents in a way that does not rely entirely on traditional vector based models, since those models often fail when two texts discuss the same topic but use noticeably different vocabulary. Instead of depending on term frequency counts or the usual bag of words approach, the authors describe a method in which both documents and individual terms are placed in a set of interconnected graph layers. Within this structure, the task of determining document similarity is treated as a problem of adjusting edge weights so that the graphs reflect both the importance of certain keywords and the relationships among documents.

Source: question answering is different from information retrieval in that it attempts to answer questions by providing summaries from numerous retrieved documents rather than by simply providing a list of documents that requires users to do additional work . however , the quality of answers that question answering provides has not been investigated extensively , and the practical approach to presenting question answers still needs more study . in addition to factoid answering using phrases or entities , most question answering systems use a sentence-based approach for generating answers . however , many sentences are often only meaningful or understandable in their context , and a passage-based presentation can often provide richer , more coherent context . however , passage-based presentations may introduce additional noise that places greater burden on users . in this study , we performed a quantitative evaluation on the two kinds of presentation produced by our online clinical question answering system , askhermes . the overall finding is that , although irrelevant context can hurt the quality of an answer , the passage-based approach is generally more effective in that it provides richer context and matching across sentences
human1: Question answering systems also contrast with traditional information retrieval based on retrieving all relevant documents and leaving the user to read as many as necessary. Nevertheless, not much work has gone into determining the quality of answers in such systems and we need a better method of presenting the answers. Whereas some systems specialise in fact-based responses in phrases or entities, the majority of question answering systems produce answers sentence by sentence. The question is that a great number of these sentences can be used in just one context, and when it comes to passing them in a passage form, this usually provides a far deeper, more concise picture. However, the negative aspect is that one can make a passage to add unnecessary context and make users struggle to find the needed information. We tested with two different formats of answers on our online clinical question answering system, Askhermes. We learned that irrelevant information could harm the quality of answers, but the passage-based format of providing answers is more effective in general due to the provision of a more comprehensive and interconnected context.

Human 2: This paper discusses how systems that try to answer users questions differ from traditional search engines, since they aim to produce short explanatory responses rather than force people to sift through a list of documents. The authors note that although this goal is widely accepted, the way such answers are presented has not been examined in detail, and many practical issues remain unclear. Beyond simple fact extraction, many systems select individual sentences as answers, even though these sentences can be difficult to understand once removed from their original surroundings. Presenting entire passages may offer more continuity and allow related statements from different sentences to reinforce each other, but this style of output can also introduce unrelated material that distracts readers. To investigate these trade offs, the researchers conducted an evaluation using their clinical information service called Ask Hermes. Their results show that while excessive context can occasionally mislead users, providing a fuller passage generally helps by giving more complete information and matching content across several nearby sentences.

Source: in this paper , the unitor system participating in the semeval-2014 aspect based sentiment analysis competition is presented . the task is tackled exploiting kernel methods within the support vector machine framework . the aspect term extraction is modeled as a sequential tagging task , tackled through svm hmm . the aspect term polarity , aspect category and aspect category polarity detection are tackled as a classification problem where multiple kernels are linearly combined to generalize several linguistic information . in the challenge , unitor system achieves good results , scoring in almost all rankings between the 2 nd and the 8 th position within about 30 competitors .

They approached it as a sequential labeling problem and addressed it using the SVM-HMM method. The aspect term polarity, aspect category, and its polarity were detected as a classification problem, with multiple kernels able to fuse to detect a variety of lingual aspects. The Unitor system did very well in the competition because it placed between 2nd and 8th place in almost every category, competing with approximately 30 other systems.

Source: we present a novel pruning method for context-free parsing that increases efficiency by disallowing phrase-level unary productions in cky chart cells spanning a single word . our work is orthogonal to recent work on closing chart cells , which has focused on multi-word constituents , leaving span-1 chart cells unpruned . we show that a simple discriminative classifier can learn with high accuracy which span-1 chart cells to close to phrase-level unary productions . eliminating these unary productions from the search can have a large impact on downstream processing , depending on implementation details of the search . we apply our method to four parsing architectures and demonstrate how it is complementary to the cell-closing paradigm , as well as other pruning methods such as coarse-to-fine , agenda , and beam-search pruning .
human1: A novel control-based pruning algorithm for context-free parsing is suggested in the present paper to improve the speed by noticing the phrase-level unary production in CKY chart cells that traverse merely a set of single words. We also target single-word cells in contrast to recent attempts that focused only on closing cells of multi-word internal words, but not in span-1 cells. we can show that a very simple discriminative classifier can be used to learn very accurately about which span-1 chart cells to merge with phrase-level unary productions. These extraneous unary productions may be omitted to make the parsing process very fast, depending on how the search is carried out. We also tested four parsing architectures and showed that they work well alongside existing approaches like the cell-closing paradigm, and with other pruning techniques, including coarse-to-fine pruning, agenda pruning, and beam-search pruning.

For example, "We also target single-word cells in contrast to recent attempts..." could be more direct, such as "Unlike recent approaches that focused only on multi-word internal words, we also target single-word cells."
6. Can you simplify or clarify any technical terms? For instance, terms like "discriminative classifier" or "phrase-level unary production" might need brief clarification for a broader audience.

predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the novel approach's advantages over previous work?
Human 2: This work proposes a new way to speed up context free parsing by blocking certain unary expansions that normally appear in chart cells covering a single token. Earlier research on reducing chart complexity has mainly concentrated on larger spans, so the smallest cells often remain untouched even though they can contain many unnecessary options. The authors show that a lightweight classifier can accurately predict when these single word cells should avoid being expanded into higher level phrase categories, although in some cases the classifier depends on features that are specific to the training data. Removing these expansions can simplify the search process and substantially reduce computation, although the exact benefits depend on how the parser is implemented. The method is tested across several different parsing systems and is shown to work alongside other pruning strategies such as coarse to fine parsing, agenda based filtering, and beam search constraints, offering improvements when combined with each of them.

Source: many syntactic models in machine translation are channels that transform one tree into another , or synchronous grammars that generate trees in parallel . we present a newmodel of the translation process : quasi-synchronous grammar ( qg ) . given a source-language parse tree t1 , a qg defines a monolingual grammar that generates translations of t1 . the trees t2 allowed by this monolingual grammar are inspired by pieces of substructure in t1 and aligned to t1 at those points . we describe experiments learning quasi-synchronous context-free grammars from bitext . as with other monolingual language models , we evaluate the crossentropy of qgs on unseen text and show that a better fit to bilingual data is achieved by allowing greater syntactic divergence . when evaluated on a word alignment task , qg matches standard baselines .
human1: The majority of machine translation syntactic models operate directly on transforming one parse tree into another, or computations that use synchronous grammars to construct parallel trees. In this paper, we present a new method, quasi-synchronous grammar (QG). Under QG, we have the source-language parse tree (T1), and we specify a monolingual grammar that produces translations of T1. The obtained trees (T2) have the same structure as T1 but are aligned at certain points, depending on their structure. we report experiments on learning quasi-synchronous context-free grammars by bitext learning. like other monolingual language models, we test the cross entropy of QGS on unseen text, and we demonstrate that a more accurate fit to bilingual data occurs by permitting more syntactic divergence.  When applied to a word alignment task, QG matches existing baselines."

Human 2: This paper introduces an alternative view of how translation systems can relate the structure of one language to another, moving away from approaches that strictly map entire trees across languages. Instead of treating translation as a direct transformation between two full syntactic structures, the authors propose a framework in which a grammar for the target language is guided by fragments of the source parse. Starting from a parse of a sentence in the source language, the model generates possible target structures that loosely follow portions of that original analysis, although the degree of similarity can vary considerably. The study describes how such grammars can be estimated from aligned bilingual text and examines how well the resulting models capture patterns in unseen data by measuring their cross entropy. The authors find that allowing the target structures to diverge more freely from the source structures often produces a better statistical fit. They also test the method on a simple word alignment task and note that its performance is comparable to commonly used baseline systems.

Source: this paper addresses the mitigation of medical errors due to the confusion of sound-alike and look-alike drug names . our approach involves application of two new methods one based on orthographic similarity ( lookalike ) and the other based on phonetic similarity ( sound-alike ) . we present a new recall-based evaluation methodology for determining the effectiveness of different similarity measures on drug names . we show that the new orthographic measure ( bi-sim ) outperforms other commonly used measures of similarity on a set containing both look-alike and sound-alike pairs , and that the feature-based phonetic approach ( aline ) outperforms orthographic approaches on a test set containing solely sound-alike confusion pairs . however , an approach that combines several different measures achieves the best results on both test sets .
