4.        You mention techniques like POS tagging, new similarity forms, and stop word expansion — can you briefly explain how each of these contributes to improving detection performance?
irrelevant_question: 2.        How much improvement did your methods achieve compared to a baseline?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the efficiency of the proposed techniques?
Human 2: This paper examines how systems that monitor streams of news reports can identify when different stories are related or when a genuinely new event has appeared. The authors treat both problems as variations of retrieving relevant documents from a collection and explore how the balance between precision and recall affects system behaviour. Building on this view, they experiment with several additions such as incorporating simple grammatical information, adjusting similarity calculations and enlarging lists of uninformative words to ignore. Tests on news data indicate that these adjustments support their initial expectations and lead to noticeable improvements in performance. The discussion suggests that understanding retrieval dynamics is essential for refining methods that track evolving news topics.

Source: at present , adapting an information extraction system to new topics is an expensive and slow process , requiring some knowledge engineering for each new topic . we propose a new paradigm of information extraction which operates 'on demand ' in response to a user 's query . on-demand information extraction ( odie ) aims to completely eliminate the customization effort . given a users query , the system will automatically create patterns to extract salient relations in the text of the topic , and build tables from the extracted information using paraphrase discovery technology . it relies on recent advances in pattern discovery , paraphrase discovery , and extended named entity tagging . we report on experimental results in which the system created useful tables for many topics , demonstrating the feasibility of this approach .
human1: At present, it takes time and is expensive to customize an existing information extraction system to a different topic and sometimes may even involve manual processing and expert knowledge. We will suggest a special technique that deals with real time information extraction called on-demand information extraction (ODIE) in this paper. ODIE has the goal of eliminating manual customization. When presented with a query, the system will automatically form patterns to derived significant relations contained within matters of interest in the text and forming organized tables through the techniques discovered as paraphrase. It takes advantage of the new advances in term discovery, plagiarism detection, and sophisticated named entity recognition. As we demonstrate in our experiments, this way is viable and convenient because the system is able to produce helpful tables on a number of subjects.
llm_questions:
1.        What are the main bottlenecks in customizing current IE systems for new domains?
2.        On what datasets or domains did you test ODIE?
3.        How was performance measured — accuracy of relations, coverage, speed, utility of tables?
4.        How does it compare to existing IE systems?
5.        How does paraphrase discovery help in this process?
irrelevant_question: 3.        How was performance measured — accuracy of relations, coverage, speed, utility of tables?
predicted_question: Does the abstract define on-demand information extraction?
Human 2: This paper describes an approach to extracting information from text that seeks to avoid the usual effort of tailoring systems to individual subject areas. Instead of preparing topic specific rules in advance, the authors suggest letting the system respond directly to whatever question a user asks. When a query is submitted, the system searches for recurring expressions in the relevant documents, identifies repeated ways of stating related ideas and attempts to organize these findings into simple tables. To achieve this, it draws on methods that discover patterns in text, locate alternative phrasings of similar statements and recognize a wide range of entity mentions. In preliminary tests, the system was able to construct reasonably informative summaries for a variety of subject areas, suggesting that this reactive style of extraction may offer a promising alternative to traditional handcrafted approaches.

Source: in this paper , we describe a syntax based source side reordering method for phrasebased statistical machine translation ( smt ) systems . the source side training corpus is first parsed , then reordering rules are automatically learnt from source-side phrases and word alignments . later the source side training and test corpus are reordered and given to the smt system . reordering is a common problem observed in language pairs of distant language origins . this paper describes an automated approach for learning reorder rules from a word-aligned parallel corpus using association rule mining . reordered and generalized rules are the most significant in our approach . our experiments were conducted on an english-hindi eilmt corpus .
human1: One of the systems described in this paper is an approach to syntax- based reordering of the source side of a phrase based statistical machine translation (SMT) system. The training data in the source language is parsed and alignment information as well as word mappings are learned automatically to obtain reordering rules. These rules are then relied to reshuffle both the training and the test data and then this is fed into SMT system. The problem of reordering is to be expected particularly where language pairs differ widely in word order. We offer a system of learning to reorder rules that automates an association rule mining on a parallel corpus which is aligned in words. Those which are reordered and generalized are the most important rules in our method. On an EILMT project English-Hindi corpus, we examined our methodology.
llm_questions:
1.        Why is reordering so critical in SMT for language pairs like English-Hindi?
2.        What problems arise if reordering isn't addressed before translation?
3.        How is alignment information used to extract reordering patterns?
4.        What format do the reordering rules take — tree transformations, phrase swaps, etc.?
5.        What evaluation metric did you use — BLEU, TER, reordering accuracy? How does your approach compare to baseline or non-reordered SMT?
6.        How do you generalize rules — are syntactic categories or dependency patterns involved?
irrelevant_question: 5.        What evaluation metric did you use — BLEU, TER, reordering accuracy? How does your approach compare to baseline or non-reordered SMT?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the importance of reordering to the reader?
Human 2: This paper outlines a method for adjusting the structure of sentences in the language that serves as input to a translation system. The idea is to analyze the training data with a syntactic parser and examine how words and phrases tend to be rearranged when translated. From these observations, the system derives general rules that can later be used to rearrange new sentences before they are sent to the translation engine. Such preprocessing is especially relevant for language pairs where the usual order of sentence parts differs greatly. The authors rely on a technique that looks for frequently recurring patterns across aligned sentences and uses these patterns to propose reordering strategies. They report experiments involving English and Hindi material and note that the automatically discovered rules play a central role in their approach. The results suggest that learning reordering behaviour directly from aligned examples can support translation across languages with different structural preferences.

Source: in this study we explore a novel technique for creation of polarity lexicons from the twitter streams in russian and english . with this aim we make preliminary filtering of subjective tweets using general domain-independent lexicons in each language . then the subjective tweets are used for extraction of domain-specific sentiment words . relying on co-occurrence statistics of extracted words in a large unlabeled twitter collections we utilize the markov random field framework for the word polarity classification . to evaluate the quality of the obtained sentiment lexicons they are used for tweet sentiment classification and outperformed previous results .
human1: We have proposed a novel method of the creation of the polarity lexicons on the basis of tweets streams in Russian and in English in our work. Then we eliminate subjective tweets according to an overall language-specific word list. These filtered tweets help in the identification of domain-specific sentiment words. The patterns of co-occurrence of a large set of uninvited tweets of a Markov Random Field algorithm would be utilized by us in order to differentiate polarities of such words. In order to place the quality of the resulting sentiment lexicons into perspective, we have used them in the application of sentiment classification of tweets, where they faired better than their predecessors

Human 2: In this study, we investigate a novel approach for constructing polarity lexicons from Russian and English Twitter streams. Initially, subjective tweets are filtered using general, domain-independent lexicons for each language. These subjective tweets are then employed to extract domain-specific sentiment words. Using co-occurrence statistics of the extracted words across a large unlabeled Twitter corpus, we apply a Markov Random Field framework to classify word polarity. To assess the quality of the resulting sentiment lexicons, they are used for tweet sentiment classification, achieving performance superior to previous methods

Source: learning of new words is assisted by contextual information . this context can come in several forms , including observations in nonlinguistic semantic domains , as well as the linguistic context in which the new word was presented . we outline a general architecture for word learning , in which structural alignment coordinates this contextual information in order to restrict the possible interpretations of unknown words . we identify spatial relations as an applicable semantic domain , and describe a system-in-progress for implementing the general architecture using video sequences as our non-linguistic input . for example , when the complete system is presented with the bird dove to the rock , with a video sequence of a bird flying from a tree to a rock , and with the meanings for all the words except the preposition to , the system will register the unknown to with the corresponding aspect of the birds trajectory .
human1: The context that words are set in facilitates learning. Such a context may involve both non-linguistic data, such as what we see in the world, and the words that are around in a sentence. In the present paper, we present a framework where such different forms of context are related with the help of structural alignment to reduce the semantics of words in unknown meanings. We concentrate on the spatial relations as another important type of meaning and explain a work-in-progress system where video is the non-linguistic input. To give an example, when such a system is introduced to the sentence the bird dove to the rock in addition to a video where a bird flies a tree to a rock and it understands all the words other than the word to, it will understand that the word to describes that particular part of the movement that the bird went to the rock.
llm_questions:
1.        Why is non-linguistic context (like video) important for understanding spatial relations or function words (like "to")?
2.        What is the structural alignment method you're using? How is semantic meaning inferred from alignment?
3.        What kind of video data are used?
4.        Is this a proof of concept, a partial implementation, or a proposed full system?
5.        Are there early experimental results, or is this still design-stage?
irrelevant_question: 5.        Are there early experimental results, or is this still design-stage?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the importance of contextual information?
Human 2: The acquisition of new words is supported by contextual information, which can include both non-linguistic semantic cues and the linguistic context surrounding the novel word. We propose a general architecture for word learning in which structural alignment integrates these contextual signals to narrow down possible interpretations of unknown words. Spatial relations are highlighted as a relevant semantic domain, and we describe a system-in-progress that applies this architecture using video sequences as non-linguistic input. For instance, if the system is presented with the sentence “the bird dove to the rock,” along with a video showing a bird flying from a tree to a rock, and the meanings of all words except the preposition “to,” it will associate the unknown word “to” with the corresponding aspect of the bird’s trajectory.

Source: several state-of-the-art event extraction systems employ models based on support vector machines ( svms ) in a pipeline architecture , which fails to exploit the joint dependencies that typically exist among events and arguments . while there have been attempts to overcome this limitation using markov logic networks ( mlns ) , it remains challenging to perform joint inference in mlns when the model encodes many high-dimensional sophisticated features such as those essential for event extraction . in this paper , we propose a new model for event extraction that combines the power of mlns and svms , dwarfing their limitations . the key idea is to reliably learn and process high-dimensional features using svms ; encode the output of svms as low-dimensional , soft formulas in mlns ; and use the superior joint inferencing power of mlns to enforce joint consistency constraints over the soft formulas . we evaluate our approach for the task of extracting biomedical events on the bionlp 2013 , 2011 and 2009 genia shared task datasets . our approach yields the best f1 score to date on the bionlp13 ( 53.61 ) and bionlp11 ( 58.07 ) datasets and the second-best f1 score to date on the bionlp09 dataset ( 58.16 ) .
human1: Support vector machine (SVM) models are used in pipeline architecture in many of the most successful event extraction systems. Nevertheless, this strategy fails to utilize associations between the events and their arguments. Others have attempted to resolve this by applying Markov Logic Networks (MLNs), but joint inference in MLNs is problematic when the high dimensional variable high-level features are required to support accurate event extraction.In this paper we propose the model that inherits strengths of both SVMs and MLNs and improves upon both their shortcomings. The essence of it is to allow the learning of rich, high-dimensional features to SVMs and turn their output into simpler, soft rules which MLNs can learn. Then through their collective inference properties MLNs can apply constraints on the consistency of these rules.Our model was tested, using BioNLP 2009, 2011 and 2013 shared tasks data sets on biomedical events extraction. The parameters we are using scored the highest F1 results as compared to the other results reported on the BioNLP 2013 (53.61), 2011 (58.07) and 2009 (58.16): thus leading to optimal results on the 2009 and 2013 datasets, and second-best results on the 2011 dataset.

1.        Do you clearly separate background information from your original contribution?
For example, is it obvious which parts describe prior work (SVMs, MLNs) and which parts describe your own method?

4.        What specific limitations arise when using SVM pipelines (e.g., lack of global consistency)?
5.        Are there any phrases that feel vague or imprecise, such as “the essence of it” or “parameters we are using”? Could you rephrase them to be more academically precise or specific?
irrelevant_question: 3.        What does it mean to convert SVM outputs into soft rules?
predicted_question: How can the abstract summarize key results in a concise way to make it easier to understand?
Human 2: Many state-of-the-art event extraction systems rely on support vector machine (SVM) models in a pipeline setup, which fails to capture the joint dependencies typically present among events and their arguments. Although Markov Logic Networks (MLNs) have been proposed to address this limitation, performing joint inference with MLNs remains challenging when incorporating high-dimensional, complex features crucial for event extraction. In this paper, we introduce a new model that combines the strengths of SVMs and MLNs while mitigating their respective limitations. The approach involves using SVMs to reliably learn and handle high-dimensional features, encoding their outputs as low-dimensional soft formulas in MLNs, and leveraging the superior joint inference capability of MLNs to enforce consistency constraints over these soft formulas. We evaluate our method on biomedical event extraction tasks using the BioNLP 2013, 2011, and 2009 GENIA shared task datasets. Our approach achieves the highest F1 scores to date on BioNLP13 (53.61) and BioNLP11 (58.07), and the second-highest F1 score on BioNLP09 (58.16).

Source: standard ir systems can process queries such as web not internet , enabling users who are interested in arachnids to avoid documents about computing . the documents retrieved for such a query should be irrelevant to the negated query term . most systems implement this by reprocessing results after retrieval to remove documents containing the unwanted string of letters . this paper describes and evaluates a theoretically motivated method for removing unwanted meanings directly from the original query in vector models , with the same vector negation operator as used in quantum logic . irrelevance in vector spaces is modelled using orthogonality , so query vectors are made orthogonal to the negated term or terms . as well as removing unwanted terms , this form of vector negation reduces the occurrence of synonyms and neighbours of the negated terms by as much as 76 % compared with standard boolean methods . by altering the query vector itself , vector negation removes not only unwanted strings but unwanted meanings .
human1: Ordinary information retrieval (IR) systems are capable of processing queries in the form of web NOT internet and allowing people seeking information on spiders to be excluded those about computing. Such systems usually eliminate undesired results by eliminating the documents that contain the omitted word after returning the documents.And we propose another more theoretically sound method which jettisons unwanted meanings right out of the original query, the method inspired by quantum logicThe orthogonality of our query vector to the vectors of negated terms in our model of a vector space should be noted: the orthogonality of the query tells that the query is orthogonal to something (irrelevant to it).This method is known as vector negation, it not only eliminates the occurrence of specific terms but also ensures that the occurrence of similar terms (such as synonyms or semantically close words) is additionally reduced by up to 76% in comparison with the normal Boolean filtering. Since the method also changes the query vector itself, it does not only eliminate unwanted words, but also the unwanted meaning.
llm_questions:
1.        Why is eliminating unwanted meaning (not just term occurrence) important in IR?
2.        What limitations of conventional Boolean filtering are you addressing?
3.        What IR benchmark or dataset did you use for this evaluation?
4.        Does the final sentence make a strong conclusion about your contribution?
5.        Are transitions between ideas clear and smooth?
6.        Are the quantitative results (e.g., “76% reduction”) introduced with enough context?
irrelevant_question: 3.        What IR benchmark or dataset did you use for this evaluation?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain the method's motivation?
Human 2: Standard information retrieval systems can handle queries like “web not internet,” allowing users interested in arachnids to avoid documents related to computing. Typically, such systems remove documents containing the unwanted term after retrieval. This paper presents and evaluates a theoretically grounded approach to eliminate undesired meanings directly from the original query in vector-space models, using a vector negation operator inspired by quantum logic. Irrelevance is modeled through orthogonality, so query vectors are made orthogonal to the negated term(s). Beyond removing unwanted terms, this method also reduces occurrences of synonyms and related terms by up to 76% compared with traditional Boolean approaches. By modifying the query vector itself, vector negation effectively removes both undesired strings and meanings.

Source: we study the use of rich syntax-based statistical models for generating grammatical case for the purpose of machine translation from a language which does not indicate case explicitly ( english ) to a language with a rich system of surface case markers ( japanese ) . we propose an extension of n-best re-ranking as a method of integrating such models into a statistical mt system and show that this method substantially outperforms standard n-best re-ranking . our best performing model achieves a statistically significant improvement over the baseline mt system according to the bleu metric . human evaluation also confirms the results .
human1: The background is research on applicability of complex syntax based statistical models to generate a grammatical case in English  to Japanese. We suggest a better implementation of the n-best re-ranking technique in order to change these models into a statistical machine translation (MT) system. Our method is much more effective than a general re-ranking one. Based on the BLEU score our best model gives a distinct increase as compared to the baseline system, human evaluations are also making the same statement.

Human 2: This study explores the application of rich syntax-based statistical models to generate grammatical case for machine translation from a language without explicit case marking (English) to one with a complex system of surface case markers (Japanese). We introduce an extension of n-best re-ranking to incorporate these models into a statistical MT system, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms standard n-best re-ranking. Our top-performing model achieves a statistically significant improvement over the baseline MT system according to BLEU scores, which is further supported by human evaluation.

Source: this paper presents a method for bootstrapping a fine-grained , broad-coverage part-of-speech ( pos ) tagger in a new language using only one personday of data acquisition effort . it requires only three resources , which are currently readily available in 60-100 world languages : ( 1 ) an online or hard-copy pocket-sized bilingual dictionary , ( 2 ) a basic library reference grammar , and ( 3 ) access to an existing monolingual text corpus in the language . the algorithm begins by inducing initial lexical pos distributions from english translations in a bilingual dictionary without pos tags . it handles irregular , regular and semi-regular morphology through a robust generative model using weighted levenshtein alignments . unsupervised induction of grammatical gender is performed via global modeling of contextwindow feature agreement . using a combination of these and other evidence sources , interactive training of context and lexical prior models are accomplished for fine-grained pos tag spaces . experiments show high accuracy , fine-grained tag resolution with minimal new human effort .
human1: This paper describes a technique to rapidly train a fine grained and broad coverage part-of-speech (POS) tagger in the language that requires no more than one day of human effort to add some data to it. What is needed to apply the method are three resources, any of which can be found in 60 to 100 languages in the world: (1) a small bilingual dictionary (online or on paper), (2) a basic reference grammar, and (3), access to a monolingual target-language text corpus.The algorithm begins its job with provision of initial POS distributions on the basis of English translation of the bilingual dictionary which in turn lacks the POS tags in spite of the fact that it does not have POS tags. It deals with other kind of word forms, such as regular, irregular and semi-regular with a generative model including weighted Levenshtein paths. It is also able to learn grammatical gender unsupervised that is, it does it by modeling the agreement patterns of words in contexts.With the combination of these and other resources of information, the system trains context and lexical features models in an interactive fashion, which helps to achieve fine-grained POS tagging. Experiments revel that the method has high precision/accuracy and detailed POS classification and very minimal human effort is required.

Can you define what "fine-grained" means here—how many tags are you talking about, and how is this different from existing POS taggers?

Can you provide a quantitative measure (e.g., F1 score, tagging accuracy, etc.)?

5.        Is this POS tagger intended for a specific application domain (e.g., NLP pipelines, machine translation, information extraction)?
irrelevant_question: 1.        You mention a “fine-grained and broad-coverage POS tagger.”
predicted_question: How can the abstract summarize the method concisely?
Human 2: This paper introduces a method for bootstrapping a fine-grained, broad-coverage part-of-speech (POS) tagger for a new language using only one person-day of data collection. The approach relies on three resources commonly available for 60–100 languages: (1) a pocket-sized bilingual dictionary (online or print), (2) a basic reference grammar, and (3) a monolingual text corpus in the target language. The algorithm starts by inducing initial lexical POS distributions from English translations in the bilingual dictionary, even without POS annotations. It models irregular, regular, and semi-regular morphology using a robust generative framework with weighted Levenshtein alignments. Grammatical gender is induced unsupervisedly through global modeling of context-window feature agreement. By combining these and other sources of evidence, the system allows interactive training of context and lexical prior models for fine-grained POS tagging. Experiments demonstrate high accuracy and fine-grained tag resolution with minimal human effort.

Source: the ambiguity of person names in the web has become a new area of interest for nlp researchers . this challenging problem has been formulated as the task of clustering web search results ( returned in response to a person name query ) according to the individual they mention . in this paper we compare the coverage , reliability and independence of a number of features that are potential information sources for this clustering task , paying special attention to the role of named entities in the texts to be clustered . although named entities are used in most approaches , our results show that , independently of the machine learning or clustering algorithm used , named entity recognition and classification per se only make a small contribution to solve the problem .
human1: Name ambiguity in the web has turned out to be a budding concern of research in NLP. It is commonly treated in a similar way as the problem of clustering web search results, such as the ones received as a result of a query of a person name, and based on whom they are talking about. In this paper, we consider some of the characteristics that can be utilized within this clustering task based on their coverage, reliability and independence. Particular attention is paid to the role of the so-called named entities in the text we cluster. Even though the majority of the techniques employ named entities, in our findings, it was clear that no matter the type of machine learning or clustering algorithm implemented, the named identified together with the named entity recognition and classification have little effect on resolving the name ambiguity problem.

Source: goal-oriented dialog agents are expected to recognize user-intentions from an utterance and execute appropriate tasks . typically , such systems use a semantic parser to solve this problem . however , semantic parsers could fail if user utterances contain out-of-grammar words/phrases or if the semantics of uttered phrases did not match the parsers expectations . in this work , we have explored a more robust method of task prediction . we define task prediction as a classification problem , rather than parsing and use semantic contexts to improve classification accuracy . our classifier uses semantic smoothing kernels that can encode information from knowledge bases such as wordnet , nell and freebase.com . our experiments on two spoken language corpora show that augmenting semantic information from these knowledge bases gives about 30 % absolute improvement in task prediction over a parserbased method . our approach thus helps make a dialog agent more robust to user input and helps reduce number of turns required to detected intended tasks .

These systems typically exploit a semantic -testIn most cases, these systems would incorporate a semantic parser that retrieves meaning behind the user input. The parsers however may crack when the user enters words that are not grammatically correct or unexpected words. The method adopted in this paper is more sound because it does not do the same task prediction on the basis of parsing by all means. we just use the fact that it is a classification problem and do the better classification using the semantic context. The semantic smoothing kernels of our classifier uses such knowledge base information as WordNet, NELL and Freebase. Spoken language datasets as two show that this semantic knowledge embedded brings an almost 30 % advantage in the prediction of the task over the parser-based approaches. Typically, such systems rely on a semantic parser to achieve this. However, parsers can fail when utterances contain out-of-grammar words or when the semantics of phrases do not match the parser’s expectations. In this work, we explore a more robust approach to task prediction by framing it as a classification problem rather than parsing, leveraging semantic contexts to improve accuracy. Our classifier employs semantic smoothing kernels that incorporate information from knowledge bases such as WordNet, NELL, and Freebase. This approach enhances the robustness of dialogue agents to varied user input and reduces the number of turns needed to identify intended tasks.

Source: this paper describes the results of some experiments exploring statistical methods to infer syntactic categories from a raw corpus in an unsupervised fashion . it shares certain points in common with brown et at ( 1992 ) and work that has grown out of that : it employs statistical techniques to derive categories based on what words occur adjacent to a given word . however , we use an eigenvector decomposition of a nearest-neighbor graph to produce a two-dimensional rendering of the words of a corpus in which words of the same syntactic category tend to form clusters and neighborhoods . we exploit this technique for extending the value of automatic learning of morphology . in particular , we look at the suffixes derived from a corpus by unsupervised learning of morphology , and we ask which of these suffixes have a consistent syntactic function ( e.g. , in english , -ed is primarily a mark of verbal past tense , does but s marks both noun plurals and 3rd person present on verbs ) .
human1: The paper introduces experiments with automatically determining the syntactic categories (such as nouns or verbs) upon raw text, with the statistical approach and zero labeled data. We are not dissimilar to Brown and et al (1992) because we base our approach on the analysis of the most frequently used combinations of words. But that is not what we do, instead we use eigenvector decomposition of a nearest-neighbor graph. This allows us to make a two-dimensional display of the corpus (words in the same syntactic category tending to cluster together).It is also used in making automatic morphology learning more effective. In particular we analyze suffixes identified in an unsupervised morphological analysis and see whether they always or often indicate certain syntactic roles. One example would be in English, -ed usually denotes past tense of a verb in English, whereas -s is used to denote the plurality of nouns or third person singular present tense of the verb. We are aimed at finding out which suffixes are similar in their syntactic behavior throughout the corpus.

Source: this paper describes a simple patternmatching algorithm for recovering empty nodes and identifying their co-indexed antecedents in phrase structure trees that do not contain this information . the patterns are minimal connected tree fragments containing an empty node and all other nodes co-indexed with it . this paper also proposes an evaluation procedure for empty node recovery procedures which is independent of most of the details of phrase structure , which makes it possible to compare the performance of empty node recovery on parser output with the empty node annotations in a goldstandard corpus . evaluating the algorithm on the output of charniaks parser ( charniak , 2000 ) and the penn treebank ( marcus et al , 1993 ) shows that the patternmatching algorithm does surprisingly well on the most frequently occuring types of empty nodes given its simplicity .

To a large extent this method is resistant to details of phrase structure, allowing easier comparison between recovery performance on parser output and against the annotations of resources such as the Penn Treebank.The pattern matching algorithm yields surprisingly good results given its simplicity on the output of Charniak (2000) parser and the Penn Treebank (Marcus et al., 1993).

Source: the study addresses the problem of automatic acquisition of entailment relations between verbs . while this task has much in common with paraphrases acquisition which aims to discover semantic equivalence between verbs , the main challenge of entailment acquisition is to capture asymmetric , or directional , relations . motivated by the intuition that it often underlies the local structure of coherent text , we develop a method that discovers verb entailment using evidence about discourse relations between clauses available in a parsed corpus . in comparison with earlier work , the proposed method covers a much wider range of verb entailment types and learns the mapping between verbs with highly varied argument structures .

Source: this paper presents the ongoing project computational models of first language acquisition , together with its current product , the learning algorithm grasp . grasp is designed specifically for inducing grammars from large , unlabelled corpora of spontaneous ( i.e . unscripted ) speech . the learning algorithm does not assume a predefined grammatical taxonomy ; rather the determination of categories and their relations is considered as part of the learning task . while grasp learning can be used for a range of practical tasks , the long-term goal of the project is to contribute to the debate of innate linguistic knowledge under the hypothesis that there is no such .

The most important aspect of the algorithm is that it is not provided with any pre-defined grammatical terms, such as nouns or verbs. Rather, it must determine these categories and rules linking all of them, itself. Though the learning system will be useful in practical situations, the longer-term project goal is the enter into the discussion of the innate linguistic knowledge. Source: we introduce a novel ranking algorithm called grasshopper , which ranks items with an emphasis on diversity . that is , the top items should be different from each other in order to have a broad coverage of the whole item set . many natural language processing tasks can benefit from such diversity ranking . our algorithm is based on random walks in an absorbing markov chain . we turn ranked items into absorbing states , which effectively prevents redundant items from receiving a high rank . we demonstrate grasshoppers effectiveness on extractive text summarization : our algorithm ranks between the 1st and 2nd systems on duc 2004 task 2 ; and on a social network analysis task that identifies movie stars of the world .
human1: Using Grasshopper We are also going to introduce a new ranking algorithm that places emphasis on diversity. That is to say, it does not only find the best items but also makes sure that all the best results are dissimilar so as to have a wide-ranging picture of all the available possibilities. Such a diverse ranking comes in handy in various natural language processing applications. The procedure performed by the algorithm draws on similarities to a random walk process but with one twist: an item having been ranked is an absorbing state. This effectively avoids giving other items a high rank because they are too closely similar with what is already been selected, and as such avoids redundancy. We have shown that Grasshopper can be extremely useful in two distinct ways. It reached the best level in text summarization, being in the 1st  or 2nd place on the DUC 2004 competition. On a totally different task of social network analysis, it achieved successful results too, being used to identify a varied list of movie stars worldwide.

Source: obtaining labeled data is a significant obstacle for many nlp tasks . recently , online games have been proposed as a new way of obtaining labeled data ; games attract users by being fun to play . in this paper , we consider the application of this idea to collecting semantic relations between words , such as hypernym/hyponym relationships . we built three online games , inspired by the real-life games of scattergoriestm and tabootm . as of june 2008 , players have entered nearly 800,000 data instances , in two categories . the first type of data consists of category/answer pairs ( types of vehicle , car ) , while the second is essentially free association data ( submarine , underwater ) . we analyze both types of data in detail and discuss potential uses of the data . we show that we can extract from our data set a significant number of new hypernym/hyponym pairs not already found in wordnet .

A new concept is to gather such data using online games, which are appealing to play, thus making the users willing to participate. In this paper, we use that concept to collect semantic relations between words, e.g., hypernym/hyponym pairs. We wrote three online games that were based on life games such as Scattergories and Taboo. By June 2008, any player had contributed almost 800,000 pieces of data in two categories, category/answer pairs (e.g., types of vehicle - car) and free associations (e.g., submarine - underwater). We compare the two kinds and also discuss the ways the data is to be used. Notably we also show that our data set would provide us with enough new examples in hypernym/hyponym compounds that are not currently found in wordnet.

Human 2: Acquiring labeled data remains a major challenge for many NLP tasks. Recently, online games have been suggested as a novel method for gathering such data, leveraging their entertainment value to engage users. In this paper, we explore this approach for collecting semantic relations between words, including hypernym/hyponym relationships. We developed three online games inspired by the real-world games Scattergories™ and Taboo™. By June 2008, players had contributed nearly 800,000 data points across two categories. The first category contains explicit category/answer pairs (e.g., vehicle, car), while the second consists of free association data (e.g., submarine, underwater). We analyze both types of data in depth, discuss potential applications, and demonstrate that a substantial number of new hypernym/hyponym pairs absent from WordNet can be extracted from our dataset.

Source: consumers increasingly inform their purchase decisions with opinions and other information found on the web . unfortunately , the ease of posting content online , potentially anonymously , combined with the public 's trust and growing reliance on this content , creates opportunities and incentives for abuse . this is especially worrisome in the case of online reviews of products and services , where businesses may feel pressure to post deceptive opinion spam -- -fictitious reviews disguised to look like authentic customer reviews . in recent years , several approaches have been proposed to identify deceptive opinion spam based on linguistic cues in a review 's text . in this talk i will summarize a few of these approaches . i will additionally discuss some of the challenges researchers face when studying this problem , including the difficulty of obtaining labeled data , uncertainties surrounding the prevalence of deception , and how linguistic cues to deceptive opinion spam vary with the text 's sentiment ( e.g. , 5-star vs 1- and 2star reviews ) , domain ( e.g. , hotel vs. restaurant reviews ) and the domain expertise of the author ( e.g. , crowdsourced vs. employee-written deceptive opinion spam ) .
human1: This presentation summarises some of these methods and some of the important challenges in this field such as the fact that it is hard to access labeled data to train on, there is confusion over the prevalence of false reviews and how this varies based on a number of factors such as the sentiment of the review (e.g., is it 5-star or 1-star), or the domain (a hotel review or a restaurant review) or the experience of the writer (e.g., crowdworkers or employees writing reviews as part of test). Increasingly consumers of service use online reviews and opinion as source of information in decision to buy something. Nevertheless, the convenience of publishing content usually anonymously, heightened by the increased faith of people in such reviews, provides the potential for abuse. Deceptive opinion spam is one of the concerns: fake reviews, supposed to resemble authentic customer feedback, and are usually posted by businesses under compulsion to improve their image. During recent years, scientists have come up with techniques for identifying such false reviews by tracking the language patterns in the text.

Human 2: Consumers increasingly rely on online opinions and information to guide their purchasing decisions. However, the ease of posting content online often anonymously combined with public trust in such content, creates opportunities for misuse. This is particularly concerning for online product and service reviews, where businesses might be tempted to post deceptive opinion spam, i.e., fake reviews that appear as genuine customer feedback. In recent years, multiple approaches have been developed to detect deceptive opinion spam using linguistic cues in review texts. In this talk, I will summarize several of these methods and discuss challenges faced by researchers, such as the scarcity of labeled data, uncertainties about the prevalence of deception, and the variation of linguistic indicators depending on the review’s sentiment (e.g., 5-star versus 1- or 2-star), domain (e.g., hotels versus restaurants), and the author’s expertise (e.g., crowdsourced versus employee-generated deceptive reviews).

Source: in this paper , we argue that in it-clefts as in it was ohno who won , the cleft pronoun ( it ) and the cleft clause ( who won ) form a discontinuous syntactic constituent , and a semantic unit as a definite description , presenting arguments from percus ( 1997 ) and hedberg ( 2000 ) . we propose a syntax of it-clefts using tree-local multicomponent tree adjoining grammar and a compositional semantics on the proposed syntax using synchronous tree adjoining grammar .
human1: In the present paper we claim that the cleft pronoun (it) and the cleft clause (who won), constitute in it-clefts (It was Ohno who won) a single semantic entity sharing a discontinuous syntactic arrangement and working as a definite description. In order to justify this opinion we refer to the arguments provided by Percus (1997) and Hedberg (2000). Our proposal is next to give it-clefts a syntactic analysis in terms of tree-local multicomponent Tree Adjoining Grammar (TAG) and we provide a synchronous Tree Adjoining Grammar semantics of it-clefts.

Source: beam search is a fast and empirically effective method for translation decoding , but it lacks formal guarantees about search error . we develop a new decoding algorithm that combines the speed of beam search with the optimal certificate property of lagrangian relaxation , and apply it to phrase- and syntax-based translation decoding . the new method is efficient , utilizes standard mt algorithms , and returns an exact solution on the majority of translation examples in our test data . the algorithm is 3.5 times faster than an optimized incremental constraint-based decoder for phrase-based translation and 4 times faster for syntax-based translation .
human1: It is a faster broadly implemented machine translation decoding algorithm that fails to give formal error avoidance bound in the search using Beam search. This paper proposes new decoding algorithm which exploits both the performance of the beam search, and the optimality guarantees of Lagrangian relaxation. We use this technique on phrase-based translation and syntax-based. It is an efficient method that operates using conventional machine translation methods and gives precise solutions to the majority of translation examples in our testing set. It is also much faster, 3.5 times for phrase-based translation, and 4 times faster for syntax-based translation as compared to a highly optimized constraint-based decoder.

3.        Who benefits from your work? Developers of MT systems? Researchers in optimization for NLP?

irrelevant_question: 3.        Who benefits from your work? Developers of MT systems? Researchers in optimization for NLP?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the benefits of the new method?
Human 2: Beam search is a fast and practically effective method for translation decoding, but it provides no formal guarantees regarding search errors. In this work, we introduce a new decoding algorithm that merges the efficiency of beam search with the optimality guarantees of Lagrangian relaxation, applying it to both phrase-based and syntax-based translation decoding. The proposed method is efficient, leverages standard MT algorithms, and produces exact solutions for most translation examples in our test sets. Source: syntactic knowledge is important for pronoun resolution . traditionally , the syntactic information for pronoun resolution is represented in terms of features that have to be selected and defined heuristically . in the paper , we propose a kernel-based method that can automatically mine the syntactic information from the parse trees for pronoun resolution . specifically , we utilize the parse trees directly as a structured feature and apply kernel functions to this feature , as well as other normal features , to learn the resolution classifier . in this way , our approach avoids the efforts of decoding the parse trees into the set of flat syntactic features . the experimental results show that our approach can bring significant performance improvement and is reliably effective for the pronoun resolution task .
human1: The knowledge of syntax is of great importance in the resolution of pronouns. Conventional methods to capture such information comprise manually chosen features which are heuristically designed. This paper will offer a kernel-based solution that in an automatic manner will retrieve words utilized in the pronoun resolution by using syntactic information out of parse trees. Rather than flattening out parse trees to features we take the trees to be structured features and apply kernel functions to them, along with some standard features, to train a resolution classifier. Using this approach bypasses the human work in the process of feature engineering and the results prove to be quite good in testing, making it an effective solution when it comes to pronoun resolution.

6.        Why are syntactic cues particularly helpful in resolving pronouns ?
irrelevant_question: 6.        Why are syntactic cues particularly helpful in resolving pronouns ?
predicted_question: Does the abstract explain the kernel-based approach?
Human 2: Syntactic knowledge plays a crucial role in pronoun resolution. Traditionally, this information is represented through features that must be manually selected and defined heuristically. In this paper, we propose a kernel-based approach that automatically extracts syntactic information from parse trees for pronoun resolution. Specifically, parse trees are used directly as structured features, and kernel functions are applied to them alongside standard features to train the resolution classifier. This approach eliminates the need to convert parse trees into flat syntactic features manually. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves performance and is consistently effective for pronoun resolution.

Source: sentiment analysis of short texts such as single sentences and twitter messages is challenging because of the limited contextual information that they normally contain . effectively solving this task requires strategies that combine the small text content with prior knowledge and use more than just bag-of-words . in this work we propose a new deep convolutional neural network that exploits from character- to sentence-level information to perform sentiment analysis of short texts . we apply our approach for two corpora of two different domains : the stanford sentiment treebank ( sstb ) , which contains sentences from movie reviews ; and the stanford twitter sentiment corpus ( sts ) , which contains twitter messages . for the sstb corpus , our approach achieves state-of-the-art results for single sentence sentiment prediction in both binary positive/negative classification , with 85.7 % accuracy , and fine-grained classification , with 48.3 % accuracy . for the sts corpus , our approach achieves a sentiment prediction accuracy of 86.4 % .
human1: The shortness of such texts such short as single sentences or tweets is quite difficult to perform sentiment analysis, as there is little context available. As part of the remedy, a novel deep convolutional neural network is proposed, that encodes the information at the character level, all the way to the sentence level, in place of the basic bag-of-words approach. Our model is tested on two datasets, namely, the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SSTB) composed of sentences of movie reviews and the Stanford Twitter Sentiment Corpus (STS) composed of tweets. Our model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy of 85.7 percent on the binary (positive/negative) classification as well as 48.3 percent on the fine-grained classification .It records 86.4% on the STS corpus, proving that it is effective in various fields.

1.        Are there specific limitations of bag-of-words approaches that your model overcomes? (e.g., inability to capture word order or subword information?)
2.        What existing methods fail to do well on short texts—and how? Are you addressing poor generalization, weak handling of rare words, or lack of deep structure?
3.        Is the 86.4% accuracy on STS comparable to previous benchmarks or models? Can you briefly say how your model outperforms others?
4.        Is your model applicable beyond movie reviews and tweets (e.g., SMS, headlines)? If so, can you briefly hint at that?
5.        Is your CNN strictly character-level, or does it combine character and word embeddings? What are the architectural highlights of your model?
irrelevant_question: 5.        Is your CNN strictly character-level, or does it combine character and word embeddings? What are the architectural highlights of your model?
predicted_question: How can the abstract summarize key results concisely?
Human 2: Sentiment analysis of short texts, such as individual sentences or Twitter posts, is challenging due to the limited contextual information they provide. Addressing this task effectively requires methods that combine the sparse text content with prior knowledge, going beyond simple bag-of-words representations. In this work, we propose a deep convolutional neural network that captures information from the character level up to the sentence level for sentiment analysis of short texts. We evaluate our approach on two corpora from different domains: the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SSTB), containing movie review sentences, and the Stanford Twitter Sentiment Corpus (STS), containing Twitter messages. On the SSTB, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance for single-sentence sentiment prediction, attaining 85.7% accuracy in binary classification (positive/negative) and 48.3% accuracy in fine-grained classification. For the STS corpus, it achieves 86.4% accuracy in sentiment prediction.

Source: the average results obtained by unsupervised statistical parsers have greatly improved in the last few years , but on many specific sentences they are of rather low quality . the output of such parsers is becoming valuable for various applications , and it is radically less expensive to create than manually annotated training data . hence , automatic selection of high quality parses created by unsupervised parsers is an important problem . in this paper we present pupa , a pos-based unsupervised parse assessment algorithm . the algorithm assesses the quality of a parse tree using pos sequence statistics collected from a batch of parsed sentences . we evaluate the algorithm by using an unsupervised pos tagger and an unsupervised parser , selecting high quality parsed sentences from english ( wsj ) and german ( negra ) corpora . we show that pupa outperforms the leading previous parse assessment algorithm for supervised parsers , as well as a strong unsupervised baseline . consequently , pupa allows obtaining high quality parses without any human involvement .
human1: Over the past years, unsupervised statistical parsers have become much better, however, even today, they can produce extremely poor results on a per-sentence basis. It is desirable to know how to automatically find high-quality parses since their output will be helpful in a great variety of applications, and since it will be far less costly to generate than any manually annotated data. In this paper we propose PUPA, a POS-based unsupervised parse evaluation algorithm. PUPA checks on the quality of the parse trees based on the statistics of the part-of-speech sequences, which has been gathered in a batch of automatically parselled sentences.We evaluate it using unsupervised POS tagger and parser on the English (WSJ) and German (NEGRA) corpora. PUPA outperforms a strong unsupervised baseline, and the best unpublished technique to date on supervised parsers, so high quality parses can be selected easily, without manual effort.

2.        What’s missing in existing unsupervised parse selection methods? Do they lack reliability, speed, or domain independence?
3.        Why are high-quality parses so critical for downstream NLP tasks (e.g., semantic role labeling, machine translation)?
4.        What kind of batch statistics are gathered, and how large are the batches?
5.        Can you provide numeric results that support your claims? For example: “PUPA achieved X% accuracy in selecting parses, surpassing baseline’s Y%.”
6.        Who benefits most from this—developers of unsupervised parsers, researchers creating silver-standard treebanks, or downstream NLP systems?
irrelevant_question: 5.        Can you provide numeric results that support your claims? For example: “PUPA achieved X% accuracy in selecting parses, surpassing baseline’s Y%.”
predicted_question: How can the abstract describe pupa's strengths and weaknesses?
Human 2: Although the average performance of unsupervised statistical parsers has improved significantly in recent years, their output can still be of low quality for certain sentences. Since parser outputs are increasingly useful for various applications and far less costly to produce than manually annotated data, automatically selecting high-quality parses is an important problem. In this paper, we introduce PUPA, a POS-based unsupervised parse assessment algorithm. PUPA evaluates the quality of a parse tree using POS sequence statistics collected from a batch of parsed sentences. We assess the algorithm using an unsupervised POS tagger and parser, selecting high-quality parses from English (WSJ) and German (NEGRA) corpora. Results show that PUPA outperforms the leading supervised parse assessment algorithm as well as a strong unsupervised baseline, enabling the acquisition of high-quality parses without human intervention.

Source: apply support vector machines to detect and classify the relations in automatic content extraction ( ace ) corpus . we use a set of features including lexical tokens , syntactic structures , and semantic entity types for relation detection and classification problem . besides these linguistic features , we successfully utilize the distance between two entities to improve the performance . in relation detection , we filter out the negative relation candidates using entity distance threshold . in relation classification , we use the entity distance as a feature for support vector classifier . the system is evaluated in terms of recall , precision , and fmeasure , and errors of the system are analyzed with proposed solution .

Source: we considered a wide range of features for the disco 2011 shared task about compositionality prediction for word pairs , including coals-based endocentricity scores , compositionality scores based on distributional clusters , statistics about wordnet-induced paraphrases , hyphenation , and the likelihood of long translation equivalents in other languages . many of the features we considered correlated significantly with human compositionality scores , but in support vector regression experiments we obtained the best results using only coals-based endocentricity scores . our system was nevertheless the best performing system in the shared task , and average error reductions over a simple baseline in cross-validation were 13.7 % for english and 50.1 % for german .
human1: In the DISCo 2011 shared task in predicting the compositionality of word pairs we conducted an experiment with a large number of features, including scores indicating endocentricity following the COALS paradigm, scores indicating compositionality following the distributional cluster methodology, statistics on word-level paraphrases in WordNet, statistics on hyphenation patterns and the expected statistical length of translations in other languages. Although most of these characteristics were highly correlated with human compositionality scores, our support vector regression tests demonstrated that sustained use of endocentricity scores produced by COALS yielded the best results alone. Although our system only deployed this feature, the system achieved to be at the top of the shared task with average errors of 13.7 percent on English and 50.1 percent on German at cross-validation atop a simple baseline.

1.        What makes compositionality prediction particularly hard for bilingual or cross-lingual tasks (e.g., English vs. German)?

4.        How did your system compare to the baseline and other competing systems in the shared task rankings?
5.        Why did you choose support vector regression (SVR) for this task—did it outperform other models you tried?
6.        What limitations of existing models or features were you trying to address in this shared task?
irrelevant_question: 1.        What makes compositionality prediction particularly hard for bilingual or cross-lingual tasks (e.g., English vs. German)?
predicted_question: Does the abstract highlight key features?
Human 2: For the DISCO 2011 shared task on predicting compositionality of word pairs, we examined a broad set of features, including COALS-based endocentricity scores, distributional-cluster-based compositionality scores, statistics from WordNet-derived paraphrases, hyphenation, and the probability of long translation equivalents in other languages. While many of these features showed significant correlation with human compositionality judgments, support vector regression experiments achieved the best results using only the COALS-based endocentricity scores. Despite this simplification, our system was the top performer in the shared task, achieving average error reductions over a simple baseline of 13.7% for English and 50.1% for German in cross-validation.

Source: news comments on the web express readers attitudes or opinions about an event or object in the corresponding news article . and opinion target extraction from news comments is very important for many useful web applications . however , many sentences in the comments are irregular and informal , and sometimes the opinion targets are implicit . thus the task is very challenging and it has not been investigated yet . in this paper , we propose a new approach to uniformly extracting explicit and implicit opinion targets from news comments by using centering theory . the approach uses global information in news articles as well as contextual information in adjacent sentences of comments . our experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach .
human1: The readers who comment on news on the web usually express their views or attitude on the matters or events discussed in the related articles. Opinion targets extraction of these comments is significant to most web applications. This is not an easy task, though, since the comments are frequently informal, irregular, and with an implicit opinion target. This paper will illustrate a novel approach to pick up explicit and implicit opinion targets in news comments based on the theory of Centering. We use the general context provided in the news article as well as the sentences invested in the comment. Our method is effective, as experimental results depict.
llm_questions:
1.        How do news comments differ from product reviews or tweets in terms of linguistic structure or target inference?

Source: recent work has shown how a parallel corpus can be leveraged to build syntactic parser for a target language by projecting automatic source parse onto the target sentence using word alignments . the projected target dependency parses are not always fully connected to be useful for training traditional dependency parsers . in this paper , we present a greedy non-directional parsing algorithm which doesnt need a fully connected parse and can learn from partial parses by utilizing available structural and syntactic information in them . our parser achieved statistically significant improvements over a baseline system that trains on only fully connected parses for bulgarian , spanish and hindi . it also gave a significant improvement over previously reported results for bulgarian and set a benchmark for hindi .
human1: The fact that parallel corpora may be used to create syntactic parsers in a target language, based on word alignments and projection of source-language parses to target-language sentences, was demonstrated in recent literature. The resulting target-language parses however, are frequently incomplete and under-connected, so they cannot be readily used to train regular dependency parsers. Such partial parses may be learned with a greedy but non-directional parsing algorithm that we propose in this paper by exploiting the existing structural and syntactic information. On the Bulgarian, Spanish, and Hindi languages, our parser exhibits statistically significant improvements over a baseline that is trained on fully connected parsers. It also improves earlier performances on Bulgarian and forms a new reference point on Hindi.

Human 2: Recent studies have demonstrated that parallel corpora can be used to build syntactic parsers for a target language by projecting automatic source parses onto the target sentences via word alignments. However, the projected target dependency parses are often not fully connected, limiting their usefulness for training conventional dependency parsers. In this paper, we introduce a greedy, non-directional parsing algorithm that does not require fully connected parses and can learn from partial parses by exploiting the available structural and syntactic information. Our parser shows statistically significant improvements over a baseline system trained only on fully connected parses for Bulgarian, Spanish, and Hindi. It also achieves notable gains over previously reported results for Bulgarian and establishes a benchmark for Hindi.

Source: keyword-matching systems based on simple models of semantic relatedness are inadequate at modelling the ambiguities in natural language text , and can not reliably address the increasingly complex information needs of users . in this paper we propose novel methods for computing semantic relatedness by spreading activation energy over the hyperlink structure of wikipedia . we demonstrate that our techniques can approach state-of-the-art performance , while requiring only a fraction of the background data .

Source: we introduce a new publicly available tool that implements efficient indexing and retrieval of large n-gram datasets , such as the web1t 5-gram corpus . our tool indexes the entire web1t dataset with an index size of only 100 mb and performs a retrieval of any n-gram with a single disk access . with an increased index size of 420 mb and duplicate data , it also allows users to issue wild card queries provided that the wild cards in the query are contiguous . furthermore , we also implement some of the smoothing algorithms that are designed specifically for large datasets and are shown to yield better language models than the traditional ones on the web1t 5gram corpus ( yuret , 2008 ) . we demonstrate the effectiveness of our tool and the smoothing algorithms on the english lexical substitution task by a simple implementation that gives considerable improvement over a basic language model .

It stores duplicate data, can also store the index of up to 420 MB and supports wildcard queries, as long as the wildcards are adjacent. Also, we use improved methods of smoothing on a large dataset and does better of the traditional ones on the Web1T 5-gram corpus (Yuret, 2008). We can prove the power of the tool, applying it to the English lexical substitution task, which gave significant results regarding a simple language model.

Source: speech recognition in many morphologically rich languages suffers from a very high out-of-vocabulary ( oov ) ratio . earlier work has shown that vocabulary decomposition methods can practically solve this problem for a subset of these languages . this paper compares various vocabulary decomposition approaches to open vocabulary speech recognition , using estonian speech recognition as a benchmark . comparisons are performed utilizing large models of 60000 lexical items and smaller vocabularies of 5000 items . a large vocabulary model based on a manually constructed morphological tagger is shown to give the lowest word error rate , while the unsupervised morphology discovery method morfessor baseline gives marginally weaker results . only the morfessor-based approach is shown to adequately scale to smaller vocabulary sizes .
human1: The major problem of speech recognition that uses morphologically rich languages is the high out of vocabulary (OOV) rate. Research conducted in the past has demonstrated that to counter this problem, it is possible to divide words into small parts. This paper will compare various types of vocabulary decomposition of open-vocabulary speech recognition by giving an example of Estonian. These are large models with 60,000 lexical items, as well as smaller models with 5,000 items. Of the methods, a small vocabulary model that is constructed on the basis of a manually-constructed morphological tagger has the lowest word error rate. The unsupervised Morfessor Baseline system is slightly less good, yet is the only one that can be effectively applied to vocabulary of smaller dimensions.

Previous studies have indicated that vocabulary decomposition techniques can effectively address this issue for some of these languages. In this paper, we compare different vocabulary decomposition methods for open-vocabulary speech recognition, using Estonian as a benchmark. Experiments are conducted with large models containing 60,000 lexical items as well as smaller vocabularies of 5,000 items. A large vocabulary model using a manually constructed morphological tagger achieves the lowest word error rate, whereas the unsupervised morphology discovery method, Morfessor, shows slightly lower performance. However, only the Morfessor-based approach is shown to scale effectively to smaller vocabularies.

Source: this paper presents the design and construction of an annotated chinese collocation bank as the resource to support systematic research on chinese collocations . with the help of computational tools , the bi-gram and n-gram collocations corresponding to 3,643 headwords are manually identified . furthermore , annotations for bi-gram collocations include dependency relation , chunking relation and classification of collocation types . currently , the collocation bank annotated 23,581 bigram collocations and 2,752 n-gram collocations extracted from a 5-million-word corpus . through statistical analysis on the collocation bank , some characteristics of chinese bigram collocations are examined which is essential to collocation research , especially for chinese .

Source: modern automatic speech recognition is ineffective at understanding relatively unintelligible speech caused by neuro-motor disabilities collectively called dysarthria . since dysarthria is primarily an articulatory phenomenon , we are collecting a database of vocal tract measurements during speech of individuals with cerebral palsy . in this paper , we demonstrate that articulatory knowledge can remove ambiguities in the acoustics of dysarthric speakers by reducing entropy relatively by 18.3 % , on average . furthermore , we demonstrate that dysarthric speech is more precisely portrayed as a noisy-channel distortion of an abstract representation of articulatory goals , rather than as a distortion of non-dysarthric speech . we discuss what implications these results have for our ongoing development of speech systems for dysarthric speakers .
human1: Speech of individuals with a disability referred to as dysarthia, which is an articulation malfunction triggered by neuro-motor disability is difficult to understand using modern-day automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems. To rectify this we are creating a database of relation to measures of the vocal tract taken on cerebral palsy patients by them whilst they are saying something. In the present article we present that by applying articulatory information, it is possible to reduce ambiguity in speech with dysarthia, which reduces acoustic uncertainty (entropy) by a mean of 18.3%. It also happens that we understand dysarthric speech as noisy variant of some abstract model of articulatory goals, rather than as distorted variant of normal speech. These findings guide our ways to a more functional ASR system among the dysarthric speakers.

Because dysarthria primarily affects articulation, we are compiling a database of vocal tract measurements from individuals with cerebral palsy while speaking. In this paper, we show that incorporating articulatory knowledge can reduce acoustic ambiguities in dysarthric speech, lowering entropy by an average of 18.3%. Additionally, we demonstrate that dysarthric speech is better modeled as a noisy-channel distortion of an abstract representation of articulatory goals rather than as a distortion of typical speech. We also discuss the implications of these findings for the development of speech systems tailored to dysarthric speakers.

Source: in this paper we propose a new methodology to exploit wikipedia features and structure to automatically develop an arabic ne annotated corpus . each wikipedia link is transformed into an ne type of the target article in order to produce the ne annotation . other wikipedia features - namely redirects , anchor texts , and inter-language links - are used to tag additional nes , which appear without links in wikipedia texts . furthermore , we have developed a filtering algorithm to eliminate ambiguity when tagging candidate nes . herein we also introduce a mechanism based on the high coverage of wikipedia in order to address two challenges particular to tagging nes in arabic text : rich morphology and the absence of capitalisation . the corpus created with our new method ( wdc ) has been used to train an ne tagger which has been tested on different domains . judging by the results , an ne tagger trained on wdc can compete with those trained on manually annotated corpora .
human1: This is the first paper that describes an automatic technique to generate an Arabic labeled corpus of named entities (NE) based on the features and structure of Wikipedia. We transform every Wikipedia link to an NE type according to the target article producing the NE annotations. Some other Wikipedia features that we also use to find and label additional NEs that have not been linked across the original text include redirects, anchor texts and inter-language links. To minimize the number of errors we have created a filtering algorithm to deal with ambiguous cases. Also, our solution addresses two critical problems that appear in Arabic NE tagging, namely intricate morphology and absence of capital letters through using the broad coverage of Wikipedia. The resulting corpus, known as WDC was also utilized in training an NE tagger and this was tested on diverse domains. In our experiments we have shown that our tagger is competitive with taggers trained on manually-labeled sets.

Human 2: In this paper, we introduce a methodology to leverage Wikipedia’s features and structure to automatically create an Arabic named-entity (NE) annotated corpus. Each Wikipedia link is converted into an NE type corresponding to the target article to generate the NE annotation. Additional Wikipedia features—such as redirects, anchor texts, and inter-language links—are used to label NEs that appear without links in the text. We also developed a filtering algorithm to resolve ambiguity when tagging candidate NEs. Moreover, we present a mechanism that exploits Wikipedia’s high coverage to address two specific challenges in Arabic NE tagging: rich morphology and the lack of capitalization. The resulting corpus (WDC) was used to train an NE tagger, which was evaluated across different domains. Results indicate that an NE tagger trained on WDC performs competitively with those trained on manually annotated corpora.

Source: we present a derivation of the alignment template model for statistical machine translation and an implementation of the model using weighted finite state transducers . the approach we describe allows us to implement each constituent distribution of the model as a weighted finite state transducer or acceptor . we show that bitext word alignment and translation under the model can be performed with standard fsm operations involving these transducers . one of the benefits of using this framework is that it obviates the need to develop specialized search procedures , even for the generation of lattices or n-best lists of bitext word alignments and translation hypotheses . we evaluate the implementation of the model on the frenchto-english hansards task and report alignment and translation performance .
human1: In the present paper, we explain how the alignment template model of statistical machine translation may be generated and applied with the help of weighted finite state transducers (WFSTs). All components of the model can be mapped onto weighted transducers or acceptors which enables us to do both word alignment and translation via the usual operations of finite-state machines. This method is regularly one of the main advantages of this method since there is no need to develop special search algorithms, even when these are supposed to produce lattices or n-best lists of matching and translation possibilities. We use our implementation on the French-English Hansards corpus and give the results concerning the alignment and the translation accuracy.

Human 2: We present a derivation of the alignment template model for statistical machine translation along with an implementation using weighted finite state transducers (WFSTs). This approach enables each constituent distribution of the model to be represented as a weighted finite state transducer or acceptor. We demonstrate that bitext word alignment and translation under this model can be performed using standard FSM operations on these transducers. A major advantage of this framework is that it eliminates the need for specialized search procedures, even for generating lattices or n-best lists of bitext alignments and translation hypotheses. We evaluate the model on the French-to-English Hansards task and report results for both alignment and translation performance.

Source: we present a novel approach to the word sense disambiguation problem which makes use of corpus-based evidence combined with background knowledge . employing an inductive logic programming algorithm , the approach generates expressive disambiguation rules which exploit several knowledge sources and can also model relations between them . the approach is evaluated in two tasks : identification of the correct translation for a set of highly ambiguous verbs in englishportuguese translation and disambiguation of verbs from the senseval-3 lexical sample task . the average accuracy obtained for the multilingual task outperforms the other machine learning techniques investigated . in the monolingual task , the approach performs as well as the state-of-the-art systems which reported results for the same set of verbs .

The scheme is applied on two experiments the selection of the appropriate translation of ambiguous English verbs in English-Portuguese translation, and lexical sample disambiguation task of Senseval-3. In multilingual task, this method is more accurate than other machine learning approaches applied. It is competitive when used in monolingual task compared to the best existing systems.
