3.        Is the cross-lingual alignment feature based on word alignments from parallel corpora? If so, how is this represented and used in inference?
4.        What evaluation metrics were used to compare systems? On what kind of datasets were the 25 corpora based?
5.        What’s novel about your approach? Is it the Bayesian multinomial mixture model structure?
irrelevant_question: 4.        What evaluation metrics were used to compare systems? On what kind of datasets were the 25 corpora based?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the advantages of a mixture model over a sequence model?
Human 2: In this paper, we introduce a fully unsupervised system for syntactic class induction, formulated as a Bayesian multinomial mixture model in which each word type is restricted to a single class. By employing a mixture model instead of a sequence model (e.g., HMM), we can easily incorporate multiple feature types, including type-level features (such as morphology) and token-level features (such as context and alignment features derived from parallel corpora). Using only context features, our system achieves results comparable to the state of the art and substantially outperforms a similar model without the one-class-per-type restriction. Incorporating additional features further improves performance, and our final system surpasses the best published results on most of the 25 corpora evaluated.

Source: it is possible to reduce the bulk of phrasetables for statistical machine translation using a technique based on the significance testing of phrase pair co-occurrence in the parallel corpus . the savings can be quite substantial ( up to 90 % ) and cause no reduction in bleu score . in some cases , an improvement in bleu is obtained at the same time although the effect is less pronounced if state-of-the-art phrasetable smoothing is employed .
human1: It is demonstrated in this paper how the size of phrasetables in statistical machine translation could be significantly reduced, through significance testing to filter phrase pairings by the degree to which they are reliable co-occurring phrase pairs in a parallel corpus. By using such an approach, it is possible to reduce the phrasetable size by 90% without any decline in the quality of translation, quantified in BLEU score. In special situations it can even enhance performance in translation but again the improvement is less when already modern smoothing methods are in play.

Source: this paper describes nadir ( naive distributional response generation ) , a corpus-based system that , from a set of word stimuli as an input , generates a response word relying on association strength and distributional similarity . nadir participated in the cogalex 2014 shared task on multiword associations ( restricted systems track ) , operationalizing the task as a ranking problem : candidate words from a large vocabulary are ranked by their average association or similarity to a given set of stimuli . we also report on a number of experiments conducted on the shared task data , comparing first-order models ( based on co-occurrence and statistical association ) to second-order models ( based on distributional similarity ) .
human1: In this paper, NADIR (Naive Distributional Response Generation) is presented, a system that, given a set of input words and information obtained by a text corpus, generates a related word. It also sorts candidate words by their strength of association with or similarity to the input, based both on direct co-occurrence statistics, and on distributional similarity. NADIR was entered in CogALex 2014 shared task on multiword associations, in which NADIR treated the task as a sort of ranking problem. In the paper, experiments comparing first-order models (direct co-occurrence) and second-order models (contextual similarity) normalised to contextual victories of words are also provided.

1.        What problem is NADIR designed to solve? Is it intended for word prediction, creativity simulation, or linguistic modeling?

Source: this paper deals with the relationship between weblog content and time . with the proposed temporal mutual information , we analyze the collocations in time dimension , and the interesting collocations related to special events . the temporal mutual information is employed to observe the strength of term-to-term associations over time . an event detection algorithm identifies the collocations that may cause an event in a specific timestamp . an event summarization algorithm retrieves a set of collocations which describe an event . we compare our approach with the approach without considering the time interval . the experimental results demonstrate that the temporal collocations capture the real world semantics and real world events over time .
human1: The paper deals with temporal aspect of weblog content evolution by introducing temporal mutual information, a new measure. This technique provides a monitoring of the strength of the associations of word pairs at various time points, and allows identifying and examining significant pairing of the terms with real life events. An event detection algorithm identifies word pairs that can identify an event on a particular time frame; an event summarization algorithm identifies key blocks to define the event. We contrast our time conscious with a time oblivious approach and demonstrate that using time related information will better capture the actual meaning and event as it happens in the real world.

Human 2: This paper examines the relationship between weblog content and time. Using the proposed temporal mutual information, we analyze collocations along the time dimension and identify those related to significant events. Temporal mutual information measures the strength of term-to-term associations over time. An event detection algorithm selects collocations that may signal an event at a particular timestamp, while an event summarization algorithm retrieves a set of collocations that describe the event. Source: our goal is to make note-taking easier in meetings by automatically detecting noteworthy utterances in verbal exchanges and suggesting them to meeting participants for inclusion in their notes . to show feasibility of such a process we conducted a wizard of oz study where the wizard picked automatically transcribed utterances that he judged as noteworthy , and suggested their contents to the participants as notes . over 9 meetings , participants accepted 35 % of these suggestions . further , 41.5 % of their notes at the end of the meeting contained wizard-suggested text . next , in order to perform noteworthiness detection automatically , we annotated a set of 6 meetings with a 3-level noteworthiness annotation scheme , which is a break from the binary in summary/ not in summary labeling typically used in speech summarization . we report kappa of 0.44 for the 3way classification , and 0.58 when two of the 3 labels are merged into one . finally , we trained an svm classifier on this annotated data ; this classifiers performance lies between that of trivial baselines and inter-annotator agreement .
human1: The proposed tool in this paper will aim at simplifying the process of taking notes in meetings by automatically marking valuable things said during such meetings and proposing it to the meeters as possible notes. In order to validate this notion, we performed an experiment by simulating the speech recognition system as a wizard of oz where a human person made the choice of what is interesting to say based on the automatically transcribed speech and then during the meeting, they present their choices as ideas. In more than nine sessions, users agreed on 35 percent of these propositions and 41.5 percent of their ultimate notes contained wizard-written text. In order to step towards automation, we applied labeling to six meetings in applying a novel three-level noteworthiness scheme, not limited by the binary view applied in speech summarization. The kappa value of the agreement between annotators was 0.44 on the three-level labeling and 0.58 in case two categories were combined. We afterward trained a support vector machine (SVM) classifier with this data. Its performance was in that mid-way between simple baselines and inter-annotator agreement.

predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key performance metrics?
Human 2: Our aim is to simplify note-taking during meetings by automatically identifying noteworthy utterances and suggesting them to participants for inclusion in their notes. To demonstrate the feasibility of this approach, we conducted a Wizard-of-Oz study in which a “wizard” selected automatically transcribed utterances deemed noteworthy and proposed their content as notes to participants. Across nine meetings, participants accepted 35% of these suggestions, and 41.5% of their final notes incorporated wizard-suggested text. To enable automatic detection of noteworthiness, we annotated six meetings using a three-level noteworthiness scheme, moving beyond the traditional binary summary/not-summary labeling used in speech summarization. The annotation achieved a kappa of 0.44 for the three-way classification, increasing to 0.58 when two labels were combined. Finally, we trained an SVM classifier on this annotated dataset; its performance fell between simple baseline methods and inter-annotator agreement.

Source: we present procedures which pool lexical information estimated from unlabeled data via the inside-outside algorithm , with lexical information from a treebank pcfg . the procedures produce substantial improvements ( up to 31.6 % error reduction ) on the task of determining subcategorization frames of novel verbs , relative to a smoothed penn treebank-trained pcfg . even with relatively small quantities of unlabeled training data , the re-estimated models show promising improvements in labeled bracketing f-scores on wall street journal parsing , and substantial benefit in acquiring the subcategorization preferences of low-frequency verbs
human1: The current paper proposes a way to enhance grammatical analysis based on the use of lexical data deriving from two lexical sources: unlabeled text that is run through the inside-outside algorithm, and a probabilistic grammar learnt on treebank data. Combining these sources, the method is useful in achieving up to 31.6 percent fewer errors in subcategorization frame identification of never-before seen verbs, than with a smoothed PCFG trained with the use of the Penn Treebank alone. When using small quantities of unlabeled information, the models which are re-estimated produce statistically significant enhancements in the syntactic parsing accuracy on the texts of the Wall Street Journal and are particularly useful at inducing the argument structures of low-frequency verbs.

Human 2: We describe methods that combine lexical information derived from unlabeled data using the inside-outside algorithm with lexical knowledge from a treebank PCFG. Source: in order to bridge the semantic gap between the visual context of an image and semantic concepts people would use to interpret it , we propose a multi-layered image representation model considering different amounts of knowledge needed for the interpretation of the image at each layer . interpretation results on different semantic layers of corel images related to outdoor scenes are presented and compared . obtained results show positive correlation of precision and recall with the abstract level of classes used for image annotation , i.e . more generalized classes have achieved better results .
human1: Closing our gap between visual and abstract representation in order to disentangle what the image literally represents and the conceptual knowledge to which people apply in describing them, we present multi-level hierarchical representation of images. Image contents within this model are described at different semantic layers in which each layer of description considers the degree of knowledge required to interpret the contents. Experiments The model is evaluated on outdoor scenes by Corel image dataset, and it is tested against these semantic layers. In our results, we find general, abstract labels give an improved performance in annotation, more than the specific labels, the precision and recall are also better in general categories.

Source: a crucial step toward the goal of automatic extraction of propositional information from natural language text is the identification of semantic relations between constituents in sentences . we examine the problem of distinguishing among seven relation types that can occur between the entities treatment and disease in bioscience text , and the problem of identifying such entities . we compare five generative graphical models and a neural network , using lexical , syntactic , and semantic features , finding that the latter help achieve high classification accuracy .

This paper deals with a task of recognizing seven types of relationship that may exist among treatments and diseases in biomedical text and the challenge of identifying these entities. To do this we contrast five generative graphical models and a neural network using a combination of syntactic, lexical, and semantic feature. It has been our discovery that the application of semantic information has been to boost the accuracy of classification in a remarkable way.

Source: this paper briefly sketches new work-inprogress ( i ) developing task-based scenarios where human-robot teams collaboratively explore real-world environments in which the robot is immersed but the humans are not , ( ii ) extracting and constructing multi-modal interval corpora from dialog , video , and lidar messages that were recorded in ros bagfiles during task sessions , and ( iii ) testing automated methods to identify , track , and align co-referent content both within and across modalities in these interval corpora . the pre-pilot study and its corpora provide a unique , empirical starting point for our longerterm research objective : characterizing the balance of explicitly shared and tacitly assumed information exchanged during effective teamwork .

The consequent pre-pilot study and its corpora not only provide a distinct empirical background to our overarching research aim to comprehend how teams of humans and robots can coordinate the transfer of information that is clearly spelled out and information that is implicitly assumed to work effectively as a team.

Source: we investigate the novel task of online dispute detection and propose a sentiment analysis solution to the problem : we aim to identify the sequence of sentence-level sentiments expressed during a discussion and to use them as features in a classifier that predicts the dispute/non-dispute label for the discussion as a whole . we evaluate dispute detection approaches on a newly created corpus of wikipedia talk page disputes and find that classifiers that rely on our sentiment tagging features outperform those that do not . the best model achieves a very promising f1 score of 0.78 and an accuracy of 0.80 .
human1: We venture into the new challenge of identifying the controversy of online discussions and suggest a sentiment-based solution to the issue. We aim at detecting the path followed by the sentiment expressed at the sentence level during a conversation and then using these sentiments as features in a classifier to figure out whether a conflict appears in the conversation or not. We apply our approach to another alternative new data of Wikipedia talk page conversations and demonstrate a great improvement in classification when sentiment-based feature is incorporated in the classifier. The highest performing model has the F1 score of 0.78 and the accuracy score of 0.80, demonstrating a possible success of the sentence analysis in dispute detection.

Human 2: We explore the novel task of online dispute detection and introduce a sentiment analysis–based approach. Our goal is to capture the sequence of sentence-level sentiments within a discussion and use them as features in a classifier to determine whether the discussion constitutes a dispute. We evaluate various dispute detection methods on a newly developed corpus of Wikipedia talk page disputes and observe that classifiers incorporating our sentiment features outperform those that do not. Source: word alignment plays a central role in statistical mt ( smt ) since almost all smt systems extract translation rules from word aligned parallel training data . while most smt systems use unsupervised algorithms ( e.g . giza++ ) for training word alignment , supervised methods , which exploit a small amount of human-aligned data , have become increasingly popular recently . this work empirically studies the performance of these two classes of alignment algorithms and explores strategies to combine them to improve overall system performance . we used two unsupervised aligners , giza++ and hmm , and one supervised aligner , itg , in this study . to avoid language and genre specific conclusions , we ran experiments on test sets consisting of two language pairs ( chinese-to-english and arabicto-english ) and two genres ( newswire and weblog ) . results show that the two classes of algorithms achieve the same level of mt performance . modest improvements were achieved by taking the union of the translation grammars extracted from different alignments . significant improvements ( around 1.0 in bleu ) were achieved by combining outputs of different systems trained with different alignments . the improvements are consistent across languages and genres .
human1: It is an essential ingredient in statistical machine translation (SMT) because the rules of translation extracted are usually those in the word-aligned parallel corpora. Most SMT systems use either an unsupervised alignment algorithm like GIZA++, but there has been increasing interest in supervised ones which use a very small volume of human aligned data. The current study compares on an empirical level the performance of supervised and unsupervised aligners, as well as explores the options of combining them to improve overall translation quality across two different languages (Chinese and Arabic into English) and two genres (newswire and weblog). Findings indicate that both alignment modes work comparatively the same in machine translation performance. A general increase on around 5 to 10 BLEU points was noted by combining both translation grammars of different alignments and also a significant improvement on up to 1.0 BLEU point was experienced by combining system outputs that have been trained to use different alignments. Such gains are found to be the same between languages and genres.

irrelevant_question: 5.        Could this strategy scale to low-resource languages or be extended to other NLP tasks like parsing?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the strengths of the two classes of alignment algorithms?
Human 2: Word alignment is crucial in statistical machine translation (SMT), as nearly all SMT systems derive translation rules from word-aligned parallel training data. While most systems rely on unsupervised algorithms, such as GIZA++, for word alignment, supervised approaches that utilize a limited amount of human-aligned data have gained popularity recently. This study empirically examines the performance of these two types of alignment algorithms and investigates strategies to combine them for enhanced system performance. We employed two unsupervised aligners, GIZA++ and HMM, and one supervised aligner, ITG. To ensure that the findings were not specific to a particular language or genre, experiments were conducted on test sets covering two language pairs (Chinese–English and Arabic–English) and two genres (newswire and weblog). The results indicate that both algorithm classes achieve similar MT performance. Slight gains were observed by merging translation grammars from different alignments, while significant improvements (around 1.0 BLEU) were obtained by combining outputs from systems trained with distinct alignments. These enhancements were consistent across both languages and genres.

Source: software requirements are commonly written in natural language , making them prone to ambiguity , incompleteness and inconsistency . by converting requirements to formal semantic representations , emerging problems can be detected at an early stage of the development process , thus reducing the number of ensuing errors and the development costs . in this paper , we treat the mapping from requirements to formal representations as a semantic parsing task . we describe a novel data set for this task that involves two contributions : first , we establish an ontology for formally representing requirements ; and second , we introduce an iterative annotation scheme , in which formal representations are derived through step-wise refinements .
human1: It is common that the software requirements are expressed in plain language and, as such, these requirements are prone to ambiguity, incompleteness, inconsistency. Putting these requirements into formal semantics representation may provide a chance to spot the emerging problems early in the development process and thus eliminate the number of possible errors and the related expenses. As a solution, we choose to regard the task of mapping natural language requirements to formal representations as semantic parsing problem in this paper. To address this we present a new data set specifically intended to do so, and contribute two main aspects: (1) the specification of an ontology dedicated to the representation of software requirements and (2) an annotation scheme that in turn builds on a formal representation through successive refinement, in an iterative way.

6.        How does your work compare to existing efforts in semantic parsing or requirement formalization?

Human 2: Software requirements are often expressed in natural language, which makes them susceptible to ambiguity, incompleteness, and inconsistency. Transforming these requirements into formal semantic representations allows potential issues to be identified early in the development cycle, thereby reducing errors and lowering development costs. In this work, we approach the conversion of requirements to formal representations as a semantic parsing problem. We also present a novel dataset for this task, comprising two main contributions: first, the creation of an ontology for formally representing requirements, and second, the introduction of an iterative annotation process, where formal representations are generated through successive refinements.

Source: in this paper we present a quantitative and qualitative analysis of annotation in the hinoki treebank of japanese , and investigate a method of speeding annotation by using part-of-speech tags . the hinoki treebank is a redwoods-style treebank of japanese dictionary definition sentences . 5,000 sentences are annotated by three different annotators and the agreement evaluated . an average agreement of 65.4 % was found using strict agreement , and 83.5 % using labeled precision . exploiting pos tags allowed the annotators to choose the best parse with 19.5 % fewer decisions .
human1: The paper offers results of quantitative and qualitative study of annotation practices in the Hinoki Treebank, a Japanese treebank based on the Redwoods model and targeting definition sentences in dictionaries. We consider such an approach as the acceleration of the annotation process by means of integration of part-of-speech (POS) tags. The set of 5 000 annotated sentences was checked by three annotators and the inter-annotator agreement was evaluated. We have seen the average match rate of 65.4 percent on the strict matching criteria and 83.5 percent on the labeled precision. Also, the use of POS tags cut the decisions needed to pick the best parse by 19.5%, showing that they have the esthese of accelerating the annotation operation.

Human 2: In this study, we provide both quantitative and qualitative analyses of the Hinoki Treebank for Japanese and explore a method to accelerate annotation through the use of part-of-speech tags. The Hinoki Treebank is a Redwoods-style corpus consisting of Japanese dictionary definition sentences. A total of 5,000 sentences were annotated by three distinct annotators, and their agreement was assessed. Using strict agreement metrics, the average agreement was 65.4%, while labeled precision yielded 83.5%. Leveraging POS tags enabled annotators to select the optimal parse while making 19.5% fewer decisions

Source: we describe a model for the lexical analysis of arabic text , using the lists of alternatives supplied by a broad-coverage morphological analyzer , sama , which include stable lemma ids that correspond to combinations of broad word sense categories and pos tags . we break down each of the hundreds of thousands of possible lexical labels into its constituent elements , including lemma id and part-of-speech . features are computed for each lexical token based on its local and document-level context and used in a novel , simple , and highly efficient two-stage supervised machine learning algorithm that overcomes the extreme sparsity of label distribution in the training data . the resulting system achieves accuracy of 90.6 % for its first choice , and 96.2 % for its top two choices , in selecting among the alternatives provided by the sama lexical analyzer . we have successfully used this system in applications such as an online reading helper for intermediate learners of the arabic language , and a tool for improving the productivity of arabic treebank annotators .
human1: We introduce a model of lexical analysis of Arabic text which makes use of candidate lists produced using the SAMA morphological analyser. Such lists contain fixed lemma IDs, where a fixed combination of broad word sense categories and part-of-speech (POS) tags can be found. We break down the large number of potential lexical labels into their component parts e.g. lemma ID and POS. Source: the paper presents the position specific posterior lattice , a novel representation of automatic speech recognition lattices that naturally lends itself to efficient indexing of position information and subsequent relevance ranking of spoken documents using proximity . in experiments performed on a collection of lecture recordings mit icampus data the spoken document ranking accuracy was improved by 20 % relative over the commonly used baseline of indexing the 1-best output from an automatic speech recognizer . the mean average precision ( map ) increased from 0.53 when using 1-best output to 0.62 when using the new lattice representation . the reference used for evaluation is the output of a standard retrieval engine working on the manual transcription of the speech collection . albeit lossy , the pspl lattice is also much more compact than the asr 3-gram lattice from which it is computed which translates in reduced inverted index size as well at virtually no degradation in word-error-rate performance . since new paths are introduced in the lattice , the oracle accuracy increases over the original asr lattice .
human1: The paper presents the Position Specific Posterior Lattice (PSPL) which is a new representation of automatic speech recognition (ASR) lattices that enables word position information to be efficiently indexed, and document relevance to be managed based on word proximity. Clearly from experiments on the MIT iCampus lecture recordings, application of PSPL can actually enhance the spoken document ranking performance on top of the typical baseline by 20 percent compared to the standard baseline (the standard baseline indexes only the ASR 1-best output). The MAP is raised by PSPL to 0.62 as compared to 0.53 in 1-best. Comparisons are made with a baseline retrieval system working on a manual transcript and in spite of being lossy, PSPL is much more compact than the original ASR 3-gram lattice that it is derived and inverted index sizes are much smaller with almost no increase in word error rate. In addition, PSPL has the potential to improve accuracy of oracle by using fresher paths in the lattice as compared to the original ASR lattice.

Human 2: This paper introduces the position-specific posterior lattice (PSPL), a new way to represent automatic speech recognition (ASR) lattices that facilitates efficient indexing of positional information and enables relevance ranking of spoken documents based on proximity. Experiments on a set of lecture recordings from MIT iCampus data show that spoken document ranking accuracy improved by 20% compared to the standard baseline of indexing the 1-best output from an ASR system. The mean average precision (MAP) rose from 0.53 using the 1-best output to 0.62 with the PSPL representation. Evaluation was conducted using the output of a conventional retrieval engine applied to manual transcripts of the recordings. Despite being lossy, the PSPL lattice is significantly more compact than the ASR 3-gram lattice it is derived from, reducing inverted index size without noticeably affecting word-error-rate performance. Additionally, by introducing new paths in the lattice, the oracle accuracy surpasses that of the original ASR lattice.

Source: we present a method for automatically generating input parsers from english specifications of input file formats . we use a bayesian generative model to capture relevant natural language phenomena and translate the english specification into a specification tree , which is then translated into a c++ input parser . we model the problem as a joint dependency parsing and semantic role labeling task . our method is based on two sources of information : ( 1 ) the correlation between the text and the specification tree and ( 2 ) noisy supervision as determined by the success of the generated c++ parser in reading input examples . our results show that our approach achieves 80.0 % f-score accuracy compared to an f-score of 66.7 % produced by a state-of-the-art semantic parser on a dataset of input format specifications from the acm international collegiate programming contest ( which were written in english for humans with no intention of providing support for automated processing ) .1
human1: We introduce the automatic creation of input parsers on the basis of the description on file format in English. This involves a Bayesian generative language mode of view of natural language specifications and mapping into structured specification trees which are subsequently mapped into C++ input parsers. We put it in the form of a joint dependency parsing and semantic role labeling. It takes advantage of two useful links to information (1) the one-to-one correspondence between the natural language text and structure of the specification tree, and (2) weak supervision, based on whether or not the parser generated by the technique can read input files of examples in the specified format.Applied to a set of specifications of input formats written in English and in such a different style that they would not have a prospective of being read by computers, our technique obtains a F-score of 80.0 per cent, by far out-performing any attempt to guess or model the specification

Our approach employs a Bayesian generative model to capture relevant natural language patterns and converts the English specification into a specification tree, which is then used to generate a C++ input parser. Source: the purpose of this work is to investigate the use of machine learning approaches for confidence estimation within a statistical machine translation application . specifically , we attempt to learn probabilities of correctness for various model predictions , based on the native probabilites ( i.e . the probabilites given by the original model ) and on features of the current context . our experiments were conducted using three original translation models and two types of neural nets ( single-layer and multilayer perceptrons ) for the confidence estimation task .

Source: less-configurational languages such as german often show not just morphological variation but also free word order and nonprojectivity . german is not exceptional in this regard , as other morphologically-rich languages such as czech , tamil or greek , offer similar challenges that make context-free constituent parsing less attractive . advocates of dependency parsing have long pointed out that the free ( r ) word order and nonprojective phenomena are handled in a more straightforward way by dependency parsing . however , certain other phenomena in language , such as gapping , ellipses or verbless sentences , are difficult to handle in a dependency formalism . in this paper , we show that parsing of discontinuous constituents can be achieved using easy-first parsing with online reordering , an approach that previously has only been used for dependencies , and that the approach yields very fast parsing with reasonably accurate results that are close to the state of the art , surpassing existing results that use treebank grammars . we also investigate the question whether phenomena where dependency representations may be problematic in particular , verbless clauses can be handled by this model .
human1: Other types of languages, with less fixed word order like German often have not only a rich morphology, but also liberal word order as well as non-projective patterns. German is not the only linguistic context that acts as a hindrance in this regard; ensure the profound molossus such as the Czech language, Tamil, and Greek act in the same way and context-free constituent parsing approach is inappropriate. Although dependency parsing is commonly preferred to depend on its natural approach to nonprojectivity and free word order, it is difficult to deal with some linguistic phenomena like gapping, ellipses, and verbless sentences. In this paper, we show that an easy-first online reordering parsing approach is useful when applied to discontinuous constituent structures, not just to dependency parsing as in the past. Our approach has not only high speed but also competitive accuracy compared with the results of treebank-based parser-based approaches. We also investigated the usefulness of the model in resolving structures which are problematic under dependency-based formalisms like verbless clauses, and they were also encouraging.

Human 2: This paper demonstrates how finite approximations for resolving long-distance dependencies (LDDs) can be automatically derived for wide-coverage, robust, probabilistic Lexical-Functional Grammar (LFG) resources obtained from treebanks. We extract LFG subcategorization frames and paths connecting LDD reentrancies from F-structures generated automatically for the Penn-II treebank trees and employ them in an LDD resolution algorithm to parse new texts. Unlike approaches by Collins (1999) and Johnson (2002), our method resolves LDDs at the F-structure level attribute-value representations of predicate-argument or dependency structures without relying on empty productions, traces, or coindexation in CFG parse trees. Our best automatically induced grammars currently achieve an F-score of 80.97% for F-structure parsing on Section 23 of the WSJ portion of the Penn-II treebank, and 80.24% when evaluated against the PARC 700 dependency bank (King et al., 2003), matching or slightly surpassing state-of-the-art hand-crafted grammars (Kaplan et al., 2004).

Source: this paper shows how finite approximations of long distance dependency ( ldd ) resolution can be obtained automatically for wide-coverage , robust , probabilistic lexical-functional grammar ( lfg ) resources acquired from treebanks . we extract lfg subcategorisation frames and paths linking ldd reentrancies from f-structures generated automatically for the penn-ii treebank trees and use them in an ldd resolution algorithm to parse new text . unlike ( collins , 1999 ; johnson , 2002 ) , in our approach resolution of ldds is done at f-structure ( attribute-value structure representations of basic predicate-argument or dependency structure ) without empty productions , traces and coindexation in cfg parse trees . currently our best automatically induced grammars achieve 80.97 % f-score for fstructures parsing section 23 of the wsj part of the penn-ii treebank and evaluating against the dcu 1051 and 80.24 % against the parc 700 dependency bank ( king et al , 2003 ) , performing at the same or a slightly better level than state-of-the-art hand-crafted grammars ( kaplan et al , 2004 ) .

Unlike in previous systems (e.g., Collins, 1999; Johnson, 2002), our approach has the advantage of not only a direct resolution of LDD, but also within the f-structure, i.e. the attribute- value representations of predicateargument (or dependency) structure itself and with no indirect references to the context free grammar parse trees as in terms of empty production, traces, or coindexing notations. Our own best automatically discovered grammars now attain an F-score on Section 23 of the Wall Street Journal part of the Penn-II Treebank (compared with the DCU 1051 benchmark) of 80.97% and on the PARC 700 Dependency Bank (King et al., 2003), 80.24% (King et al., 2003). These are comparable or marginally better in their results than the hand-crafted state-of-the-art LFG grammars (Kaplan et al., 2004).

Source: augmented and alternative communication ( aac ) devices enable users with certain communication disabilities to participate in everyday conversations . such devices often rely on statistical language models to improve text entry by offering word predictions . these predictions can be improved if the language model is trained on data that closely reflects the style of the users intended communications . unfortunately , there is no large dataset consisting of genuine aac messages . in this paper we demonstrate how we can crowdsource the creation of a large set of fictional aac messages . we show that these messages model conversational aac better than the currently used datasets based on telephone conversations or newswire text . we leverage our crowdsourced messages to intelligently select sentences from much larger sets of twitter , blog and usenet data . compared to a model trained only on telephone transcripts , our best performing model reduced perplexity on three test sets of aac-like communications by 60 82 % relative . this translated to a potential keystroke savings in a predictive keyboard interface of 511 % .
human1: The Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) technology aids people with communication impairments as they help them actively participate in the day-to-day discussions. Such devices tend to rely on statistical language models in order to provide word guessing and enhance the text input. The effectiveness of such predictions can be drastically improved when the language model is trained using the data that matches the intended style of communication used by the user. Yet, no big, authentic dataset of AAC messages is available at present. In this paper we show how a crowdsourcing task can be utilized to build a large (multiple individuals) but fictional set of AAC messages that will be more representative of conversational AAC usage than existing datasets that are based on telephone transcripts or companion to newswire text. We provide as well how these crowdsourced messages may instruct the intelligent collection of sentences in bigger sources such as Twitter, blogs, and Usenet. Relative improvements in perplexity were 60-82 percent on three AAC-style test sets of our best-performing model relative to models trained on plain telephone transcripts.This result added up to 511 per cent viable keystroke economy in a predictive keyboarding interface, which demonstrated the practical usefulness of the users of AAC.

predicted_question: How can the abstract summarize the work in a concise way to make it easier to understand?
Human 2: Augmented and Alternative Communication (AAC) devices allow individuals with certain communication impairments to engage in daily conversations. These devices frequently use statistical language models to enhance text entry through word predictions. Prediction accuracy improves when the language model is trained on data that closely mirrors the user’s intended communication style. However, no large dataset of authentic AAC messages exists. In this paper, we show how crowdsourcing can generate a substantial collection of fictional AAC messages. We demonstrate that these crowdsourced messages better represent conversational AAC than existing datasets based on telephone dialogues or newswire texts. Using this data, we selectively extract sentences from larger corpora such as Twitter, blogs, and Usenet. Compared to a model trained solely on telephone transcripts, our best model reduces perplexity on three AAC-like test sets by 60–82%, which could translate into potential keystroke savings of 5–11% in a predictive keyboard interface.

Source: this paper studies sentiment analysis from the user-generated content on the web . in particular , it focuses on mining opinions from comparative sentences , i.e. , to determine which entities in a comparison are preferred by its author . a typical comparative sentence compares two or more entities . for example , the sentence , the picture quality of camera x is better than that of camera y , compares two entities camera x and camera y with regard to their picture quality . clearly , camera x is the preferred entity . existing research has studied the problem of extracting some key elements in a comparative sentence . however , there is still no study of mining opinions from comparative sentences , i.e. , identifying preferred entities of the author . this paper studies this problem , and proposes a technique to solve the problem . our experiments using comparative sentences from product reviews and forum posts show that the approach is effective .
human1: The given paper is about sentiment analysis of the user-generated data on the web, more particularly, about the extraction of an opinion expressed in comparative sentences i.e. the sentences expressing a preference between two or more entities. As an example, when the author writes, "The picture quality of a camera X is superior to that of camera Y," he is obviously inclined towards camera X. Although the issues concerning the extraction of structural elements of comparative sentences date back to the past, the ability to isolate the most preferred one at that point i.e. the thoughts of the author has not been the focus of the past researchers. In the presented work, we fill this gap suggesting the method of automatic selection of a preferred entity in a comparative statement. When testing our method on the data compiled on product reviews and posts in forums, experiments show that our approach is useful in determining the choices of a user in a comparative setting.

5.        What is the name of the method or model you used to identify the preferred entity? Is it rule-based, supervised learning, or another approach?
irrelevant_question: 2.        Can you summarize in one phrase the core technique (rule-based, machine learning, or neural network-based)?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key elements of comparative sentences?
Human 2: This work examines sentiment analysis applied to user-generated content on the web, with a particular emphasis on extracting opinions from comparative sentences that is, determining which entities the author favors in a comparison. Such sentences typically contrast two or more items. For instance, the picture quality of camera X is better than that of camera Y compares camera X and camera Y in terms of image quality, and it is clear that camera X is preferred. While prior studies have explored how to extract key components from comparative sentences, none have directly addressed the task of identifying the author’s favored entity. This paper investigates that problem and introduces a method for resolving it. Experiments conducted on comparative sentences from product reviews and online forums demonstrate that the proposed approach performs effectively.

Source: in this paper , we address the problem of extracting data records and their attributes from unstructured biomedical full text . there has been little effort reported on this in the research community . we argue that semantics is important for record extraction or finer-grained language processing tasks . we derive a data record template including semantic language models from unstructured text and represent them with a discourse level conditional random fields ( crf ) model . we evaluate the approach from the perspective of information extraction and achieve significant improvements on system performance compared with other baseline systems .
human1: The paper proposes a solution to the problem of deriving data records and its attributes in unstructured versus :biomedical full text which is not much addressed in previous research. We pose that useful record extraction as well as other fine-grained language tasks require the integration of semantic data. On this mission, we make a template of a data record and fill it up with semantic language models and model it as a discourse-level Conditional Random Fields (CRF) model. We compare our approach in terms of information extraction perspective, and it is seen to have made a substantial amount of performance gains under the available baseline systems.

Source: in this paper , we address the challenges posed by large amounts of text data by exploiting the power of hashing in the context of streaming data . we explore sketch techniques , especially the countmin sketch , which approximates the frequency of a word pair in the corpus without explicitly storing the word pairs themselves . we use the idea of a conservative update with the count-min sketch to reduce the average relative error of its approximate counts by a factor of two . we show that it is possible to store all words and word pairs counts computed from 37 gb of web data in just 2 billion counters ( 8 gb ram ) . the number of these counters is up to 30 times less than the stream size which is a big memory and space gain . in semantic orientation experiments , the pmi scores computed from 2 billion counters are as effective as exact pmi scores .

Our attention will be on sketching techniques especially Count-Min Sketch where we do not literally store a pair of words but attempt to approximate the frequency of word pairs. To enhance accuracy we employ the conservative mechanism in updating, whereby we reduce the mean relative error in estimating the frequency by half. That enables us to keep a 37 GB web corpus of counts between words and word pairs in less than 2 billion counters (8 GB RAM), down to a 30x of the original memory as related the stream size. In the semantic orientation task, Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) scores based upon our approximated counts are similar to exact PMI scores, which indicates their efficiency and effectiveness.

Human 2: In this work, we tackle the difficulties of handling massive text streams by leveraging hashing techniques suited for streaming scenarios. We focus on sketch-based methods, particularly the Count-Min Sketch, which can estimate how often word pairs appear in a corpus without explicitly storing the pairs themselves. By applying a conservative-update strategy, we cut the average relative error of the Count-Min Sketch estimates by roughly half. Our results demonstrate that word and word-pair counts derived from 37 GB of web data can be maintained in only 2 billion counters (about 8 GB of RAM). This represents a memory footprint up to 30 times smaller than the size of the original stream. Source: topic detection and tracking approaches monitor broadcast news in order to spot new , previously unreported events and to track the development of the previously spotted ones . the dynamical nature of the events makes the use of state-of-the-art methods difficult . we present a new topic definition that has potential to model evolving events . we also discuss incorporating ontologies into the similarity measures of the topics , and illustrate a dynamic hierarchy that decreases the exhaustive computation performed in the tdt process . this is mainly work-in-progress .
human1: Topics Detection and Tracking (TDT) systems ensure surveillance of the available broadcast news to detect new previously unreported topics and segue of old ones. Nevertheless, the real world is dynamic and always changing and such dynamic and changing nature is a problem to many methods that are state of the art. In this paper we introduce a new topic definition that would be able to model topic progression more accurately over time. There is also a discussion of the use of ontologies, together with topic similarity so as to provide a better interpretation of the semantics. We also propose a dynamic topic tree with the goal of lowering the processing cost that usually applies during the TDT process. This research work is continuing and brings early results of further adaptive and efficient TDT systems.

Human 2: Topic Detection and Tracking (TDT) systems analyze broadcast news to identify newly emerging events and follow the progression of those already detected. Because events evolve over time, many existing methods struggle to keep up. In this work, we introduce a revised topic representation designed to better capture the dynamic nature of unfolding events. We also explore how ontologies can be integrated into topic-similarity measures and present a dynamic hierarchical structure that reduces the amount of exhaustive computation required in the TDT pipeline. The work is still in progress, but the proposed ideas show promising potential.

Source: extractive summarization typically uses sentences as summarization units . in contrast , joint compression and summarization can use smaller units such as words and phrases , resulting in summaries containing more information . the goal of compressive summarization is to find a subset of words that maximize the total score of concepts and cutting dependency arcs under the grammar constraints and summary length constraint . we propose an efficient decoding algorithm for fast compressive summarization using graph cuts . our approach first relaxes the length constraint using lagrangian relaxation . then we propose to bound the relaxed objective function by the supermodular binary quadratic programming problem , which can be solved efficiently using graph max-flow/min-cut . since finding the tightest lower bound suffers from local optimality , we use convex relaxation for initialization . experimental results on tac2008 dataset demonstrate our method achieves competitive rouge score and has good readability , while is much faster than the integer linear programming ( ilp ) method .
human1: Conventional extractive summarization chooses complete sentences to be used as units of summary. Conversely, compressive summarization has room to have more fine-grained units, including words and phrases, and have fewer and information-dense summaries. The main problem to solve is to pick a sub-set of possible words, the total score of a given concepts and a trimmed dependency arcs should be maximized, whereas grammar rules should be followed and the summary should have a specific length. In the present paper we propose an efficient graph cuts decoding algorithms to do compressive summarization. To handle the length constraint we Lagrangian-relax it along with the objective and rewrite the objective that arises as a supermodular binary quadratic program which we can solve using graph max-flow/min-cut programs which scales efficiently. During a pursuit of tight lower bounds we encounter the local optima problem, and introduce a suggestion of using a convex relaxation as an initiation point.TAC 2008 experiments demonstrates that our approach is competitive both in terms of ROUGE and readability but its run time is far quicker than any Integer Linear Programming (ILP) method being currently available.

4.        Does your approach support multilingual summarization or require retraining for each language?
5.        What makes your use of graph cuts unique compared to prior compressive summarization methods ? How generalizable is your method to domains beyond TAC 2008, like news, reviews, or legal docs?
irrelevant_question: 2.        What kind of grammar constraints are enforced—dependency grammar, constituency, or something else?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the advantages of compressive summarization?
Human 2: Extractive summarization generally relies on sentences as the basic units, whereas approaches that combine compression with summarization can operate on smaller elements like words or phrases, leading to summaries that capture more content. Compressive summarization aims to choose a subset of words that maximizes concept scores while minimizing broken dependency arcs, all under grammatical and length constraints. We introduce an efficient decoding technique for fast compressive summarization based on graph-cut methods. The procedure begins by relaxing the length constraint through Lagrangian relaxation, after which we approximate the relaxed objective with a supermodular binary quadratic programming formulation that can be solved using max-flow/min-cut. Because obtaining the tightest lower bound can get stuck in local optima, we initialize the process with a convex relaxation. Experiments on the TAC2008 dataset show that our method produces competitive ROUGE scores and readable summaries, while being significantly faster than traditional integer linear programming (ILP) approaches.

Source: conversational participants tend to immediately and unconsciously adapt to each others language styles : a speaker will even adjust the number of articles and other function words in their next utterance in response to the number in their partners immediately preceding utterance . this striking level of coordination is thought to have arisen as a way to achieve social goals , such as gaining approval or emphasizing difference in status . but has the adaptation mechanism become so deeply embedded in the language-generation process as to become a reflex we argue that fictional dialogs offer a way to study this question , since authors create the conversations but dont receive the social benefits ( rather , the imagined characters do ) . indeed , we find significant coordination across many families of function words in our large movie-script corpus . we also report suggestive preliminary findings on the effects of gender and other features ; e.g. , surprisingly , for articles , on average , characters adapt more to females than to males .
human1: Conversational interlocutors tend to converge to one another in language styles, even the differences in the usage of the number of articles and other functional words in the recent speech production. It is supposed that this is an eye-catching coordination aiding the social purposes, e.g. approval-seeking or indicating a status distinction. This paper examines two issues; one is whether such adaptation has become an automatic process during the language-generation process. To explore this we look at fictional conversation, when writers, produce conversational dialogue we do not get direct social pay-off, with this accruing to the created characters. With a huge corpus of movie scripts, we observe a high quality of linguistic coordination of several families of functional words. We also present initial results regarding how such features as gender can affect coordination, e.g., characters more thoroughly adjust to female characters than to male characters as far as usage of the articles is concerned.

5.        Is there any annotation or manual validation of your computational findings to ensure their interpretability?

irrelevant_question: 3.        Do you plan to study real-life, spontaneous conversations to compare coordination levels with fictional dialogue?
predicted_question: Does the abstract highlight coordination?
Human 2: People engaged in conversation often, without conscious effort, adjust their linguistic style to match one another. A speaker may even alter the number of articles or other function words in their next turn based on how many their partner used in the immediately preceding one. This high degree of linguistic coordination is believed to support social objectives, such as seeking approval or signaling differences in status. A key question, however, is whether this adaptive behavior has become so ingrained in language production that it operates almost automatically. We propose that fictional dialogue provides a useful testbed for this idea, since authors script conversations without personally gaining the social advantages those benefits accrue only to the characters. Using a large corpus of movie scripts, we observe clear evidence of coordination across several groups of function words. We also present initial patterns involving gender and other factors; for instance, and somewhat unexpectedly, characters tend to align more strongly with females than with males in their use of articles.

Source: searching online information is increasingly a daily activity for many people . the multilinguality of online content is also increasing ( e.g . the proportion of english web users , which has been decreasing as a fraction the increasing population of web users , dipped below 50 % in the summer of 2001 ) . to improve the ability of an english speaker to search mutlilingual content , we built a system that supports cross-lingual search of an arabic newswire collection and provides on demand translation of arabic web pages into english . the cross-lingual search engine supports a fast search capability ( sub-second response for typical queries ) and achieves state-of-the-art performance in the high precision region of the result list . the on demand statistical machine translation uses the direct translation model along with a novel statistical arabic morphological analyzer to yield state-of-the-art translation quality . the on demand smt uses an efficient dynamic programming decoder that achieves reasonable speed for translating web documents . overview morphologically rich languages like arabic ( beesley , k. 1996 ) present significant challenges to many natural language processing applications as the one described above because a word often conveys complex meanings decomposable into several morphemes ( i.e . prefix , stem , suffix ) . by segmenting words into morphemes , we can improve the performance of natural language systems including machine translation ( brown et al .
human1: Accessing online information has been an ordinary practice among many individuals. Meanwhile, the online content is becoming more multilingual at a fast rate-- to give an example, the number of web users who speak English dropped below 50 percent in mid-2001, indicating the spread of internet access all over the world. Our cross-lingual search of Arabic newswire sets is a way to make English-speaking users find content in other languages. The system is geared towards response time of query and has state-of-the art accuracy, especially in the top-most items of a search. We also offer to translate the Arabic web pages on demand based on a statistical machine translation (SMT) system into English. The proposed system is a direct translation-based but with the application of a new statistical Arabic morphological analyzer, making translation of high quality. The decoder has an effective dynamic programming algorithm and achieves a reasonable translation speed that can be applied to web-scale operations. In conclusion, we note that morphologically rich languages such as Arabic present specific difficulties to natural language processing tasks because words may have numerous morphemes (prefixes, stems, and suffixes). When decomposing words into morphemes, we can gain considerable improvement in the performance of the system, e.g., in machine translation.

Human 2: Searching for information online has become a routine part of everyday life for many users. At the same time, the amount of multilingual material on the web continues to grow for example, the share of English-speaking web users fell below 50% in mid-2001 as the overall internet population diversified. To help English speakers access information written in other languages, we developed a system that performs cross-lingual search over an Arabic newswire corpus and offers on-demand translation of Arabic web pages into English. The cross-language retrieval component provides rapid responses (typically under a second) and delivers state-of-the-art performance in the high-precision portions of the results. The on-demand statistical machine translation system employs a direct translation model together with a novel statistical analyzer for Arabic morphology, producing competitive translation quality. Its dynamic-programming decoder also enables practical translation speeds for web content. Languages with rich morphology, such as Arabic (Beesley, 1996), pose substantial difficulties for NLP systems like ours because a single word may contain multiple meaningful morphemes prefixes, stems, and suffixes. Breaking words into their morphemic components can enhance the effectiveness of many NLP applications, including machine translation (Brown et al.).

Source: two of the main corpora available for training discourse relation classifiers are the rst discourse treebank ( rst-dt ) and the penn discourse treebank ( pdtb ) , which are both based on the wall street journal corpus . most recent work using discourse relation classifiers have employed fully-supervised methods on these corpora . however , certain discourse relations have little labeled data , causing low classification performance for their associated classes . in this paper , we attempt to tackle this problem by employing a semi-supervised method for discourse relation classification . the proposed method is based on the analysis of feature cooccurrences in unlabeled data . this information is then used as a basis to extend the feature vectors during training . the proposed method is evaluated on both rst-dt and pdtb , where it significantly outperformed baseline classifiers . we believe that the proposed method is a first step towards improving classification performance , particularly for discourse relations lacking annotated data .

The vast majority of the recent research applies fully supervised machine learning, that is, only labeled data as examples are used to train models. Nevertheless, there are certain discourse relations that hardly have any labeled examples, and models can hardly learn to identify them properly. The paper presents a semi-supervised solution, which uses massive quantities of unlabeled data as well in assisting in better performance. We have a model which looks at how things (like words or patterns) tend to co-occur in unlabelled texts and can use such statistics to augment the feature sets applied when training our models. Our approach is employed on PDTB and RST-DT and it provides large performance improvement over the standard approaches in terms of classification accuracy. This demonstrates that our method is a good initial direction towards managing underrepresented discourse relations in a better way.

Human 2: Two major corpora used to train discourse-relation classifiers are the RST Discourse Treebank (RST-DT) and the Penn Discourse Treebank (PDTB), both derived from the Wall Street Journal corpus. Most recent studies rely on fully supervised models trained on these datasets. However, some discourse relations have very limited annotated examples, which leads to poor performance for those categories. In this work, we address this issue by applying a semi-supervised approach to discourse-relation classification. Our method analyzes how features co-occur in unlabeled text and incorporates this information to expand feature representations during training. We test the approach on both RST-DT and PDTB, and it achieves notable improvements over standard baseline models. We view this as an initial step toward enhancing performance, especially for discourse relations with scarce labeled data.

Source: this paper describes the iirg 1 system entered in semeval-2013 , the 7th international workshop on semantic evaluation . we participated in task 5 evaluating phrasal semantics . we have adopted a token-based approach to solve this task using 1 ) nave bayes methods and 2 ) word overlap methods , both of which rely on the extraction of syntactic features . we found that the word overlap method significantly out-performs the nave bayes methods , achieving our highest overall score with an accuracy of approximately 78 % .

We use analysis of individual words (a token-based technique), and we tried both a Naive Bayes classifier and a method which is based on the measurement of overlap between phrases. The similarity between the two approaches is that they both depend on extraction of syntactic characteristics, that is, they look at the arrangement of words in a sentence. Following the experimental runs on the two methods, we discovered that the word overlap method fared much better in comparison to Naive Bayes method, with the best performance by the former being an accuracy of 78 percent.

Source: in this paper we present a novel transliteration technique which is based on deep belief networks . common approaches use finite state machines or other methods similar to conventional machine translation . instead of using conventional nlp techniques , the approach presented here builds on deep belief networks , a technique which was shown to work well for other machine learning problems . we show that deep belief networks have certain properties which are very interesting for transliteration and possibly also for translation and that a combination with conventional techniques leads to an improvement over both components on an arabic-english transliteration task .

We show that DBNs have features that make them very suitable towards transliteration and we also reveal that a combination of the two improves the performance. Findings of an Arabic-English transliteration task using machine learning methods prove that this hybrid approach beats single components.

Source: the goal of this study is to evaluate an offthe-shelf pos-tagger for modern german on historical data from the early modern period ( 1650-1800 ) . with no specialised tagger available for this particular stage of the language , our findings will be of particular interest to smaller , humanities-based projects wishing to add pos annotations to their historical data but which lack the means or resources to train a pos tagger themselves . our study assesses the effects of spelling variation on the performance of the tagger , and investigates to what extent tagger performance can be improved by using normalised input , where spelling variants in the corpus are standardised to a modern form . our findings show that adding such a normalisation layer improves tagger performance considerably

Source: in this paper we present a novel approach to automatic creation of anchor texts for hyperlinks in a document pointing to similar documents . methods used in this approach rank parts of a document based on the similarity to a presumably related document . ranks are then used to automatically construct the best anchor text for a link inside original document to the compared document . a number of different methods from information retrieval and natural language processing are adapted for this task . automatically constructed anchor texts are manually evaluated in terms of relatedness to linked documents and compared to baseline consisting of originally inserted anchor texts . additionally we use crowdsourcing for evaluation of original anchors and automatically constructed anchors . results show that our best adapted methods rival the precision of the baseline method .
human1: This paper introduces a new method that will be used to automatically generate anchor texts of hyper links that ties a document to related documents of similar nature. The given approach will order fragments of a document in according to their closeness to a target one and apply those rankings in order to create optimal anchor texts in the source document. Several methods of information retrieval and natural language processing are adjusted to this. The anchor texts automatically generated are judged on their relevancy as compared to the documents they are linked to and this aspect is done manually and results are compared to a primary of originally composed anchors. The assessment of the original and generated anchors is additionally performed on the basis of a crowdsourced evaluation. Findings exemplify that the highest-scoring modified practices attain the degree of accuracy that can be characterized by the benchmark.

Source: we introduce recurrent neural networkbased minimum translation unit ( mtu ) models which make predictions based on an unbounded history of previous bilingual contexts . traditional back-off n-gram models suffer under the sparse nature of mtus which makes estimation of highorder sequence models challenging . we tackle the sparsity problem by modeling mtus both as bags-of-words and as a sequence of individual source and target words . our best results improve the output of a phrase-based statistical machine translation system trained on wmt 2012 french-english data by up to 1.5 bleu , and we outperform the traditional n-gram based mtu approach by up to 0.8 bleu .
human1: In this paper, we present recurrent neural network models of Minimum Translation Units (MTUs) that utilize unrestricted context that spans both languages in order to produce more informed translation predictions. The problem that the traditional back-off n-gram type faces with the sparsity present in the MTUs is that it becomes hard to estimate the higher-order sequences. To manage this we suggest modeling the MTUs as a bag of words as well as sequences of target words and individual source words. At last, compared to basic approaches, our approach achieves considerable improvements over phrase-based statistical machine translation systems both in terms of matching their output in addition to surpassing the ancient n-gram MTU models, with improvements of as much as 1.5 BLEU on the WMT 2012 French-English test set and 0.8 BLEU, respectively.

6.        How do you combine or select between bag-of-words and sequential modeling during inference
irrelevant_question: 3.        How much training data (e.g., size of corpus, number of MTUs) was used for training and testing?  What baseline systems were compared against?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key improvements over basic approaches?
Human 2: We propose recurrent neural network–based minimum translation unit (MTU) models that predict translations using an unbounded history of prior bilingual contexts. Traditional back-off n-gram models struggle with the sparsity of MTUs, making high-order sequence modeling difficult. To address this, we model MTUs both as bags-of-words and as sequences of individual source and target words. Our approach improves the output of a phrase-based statistical machine translation system trained on the WMT 2012 French-English data by up to 1.5 BLEU and surpasses the traditional n-gram MTU model by up to 0.8 BLEU.

Source: we present a series of methods for deriving conceptual representations from corpora and investigate the usefulness of the fmri data and machine learning methodology of mitchell et al ( 2008 ) as a basis for evaluating the different models . within this framework , the quality of a semantic model is quantified by its ability to predict the fmri activation associated with conceptual stimuli . mitchell et al used a manually-acquired set of verbs as the basis for their semantic model ; in this paper , we also consider automatically acquired feature-norm-like semantic representations . these models make different assumptions about the kinds of information available in corpora that is relevant to representing conceptual knowledge . our results indicate that automatically-acquired representations can make equally powerful predictions about the brain activity associated with the stimuli .
human1: We describe a list of conceptual representation derivations from corpora strategies and measure them against fMRI data and the machine learning framework that was introduced by Mitchell et al. (2008). This framework measures the quality of the semantic model based on the capacity of predicting the brain activation pattern evoked by conceptual stimuli. Although Mitchell et al. used a manually curated set of verbs to constrain their semantic model, instead, we examined automatically word-norm-like representations, which vary in assumptions about the nature of the information in corpora, which is relevant to conceptual knowledge. We have found that these automatically constructed representations allow predicting brain activity with a similar precision, which indicates their potential to represent semantic cognition.

(2008) as a benchmark for evaluating these models. In this framework, the quality of a semantic model is measured by its ability to predict fMRI activation corresponding to conceptual stimuli. While Mitchell et al. relied on a manually curated set of verbs to construct their semantic model, we also explore automatically acquired, feature-norm-like semantic representations. Source: as information extraction ( ie ) becomes more central to enterprise applications , rule-based ie engines have become increasingly important . in this paper , we describe systemt , a rule-based ie system whose basic design removes the expressivity and performance limitations of current systems based on cascading grammars . systemt uses a declarative rule language , aql , and an optimizer that generates high-performance algebraic execution plans for aql rules . we compare systemts approach against cascading grammars , both theoretically and with a thorough experimental evaluation . our results show that systemt can deliver result quality comparable to the state-of-theart and an order of magnitude higher annotation throughput .
human1: With growing importance of information extraction (IE) in enterprise applications, there has been a rising demand of query and expression rich rule-based information extraction engines. This article introduces SystemT which is a rule-based IE system that addresses the problem of poor expressivity and performance level of the traditional systems using the cascading grammars. SystemT adds declarative rule language, AQL and uses a cost-based optimizer to construct efficient algebraic execution plans. Not only do we provide theoretical comparison of our work, we have also provided a complete experimental comparison, demonstrating that SystemT can achieve state-of-the-art extraction quality, and have over an order of magnitude faster throughput in annotation performance than using older style grammar-based systems.

Source: this paper provides a preliminary analysis of american sign language predicate motion signatures , obtained using a motion capture system , toward identification of a predicates event structure as telic or atelic . the pilot data demonstrates that production differences between signed predicates can be used to model the probabilities of a predicate belonging to telic or atelic classes based on their motion signature in 3d , using either maximal velocity achieved within the sign , or maximal velocity and minimal acceleration data from each predicate . the solution to the problem of computationally identifying predicate types in asl video data could significantly simplify the task of identifying verbal complements , arguments and modifiers , which compose the rest of the sentence , and ultimately contribute to solving the problem of automatic asl recognition .
human1: The aim of the following paper is to show an initial analysis of the motion signature of predicates in American Sign Language (ASL) that can be recorded using a motion capture system in order to be able to automatically identify the predicate event structure as either telic or atelic. These two main aspects being the outcome of a pilot study to demonstrate that production differences, especially motion parameter settings like maximal velocity and minimal acceleration, monotonically allow predicates to be probabilistically assigned to either of these two aspects. Through the aspect of 3D replication of data differing in motion, the research makes a step forward in the direction of computationally identifying predicate types in video of ASL. This kind of grouping is highly effective in structurally simplifying processes of identifying verbal complements, arguments as well as those types of modifiers, which eventual culminates into this greater aim of automatic ASL recognition.

Human 2: This paper presents a preliminary study of American Sign Language (ASL) predicate motion signatures captured via a motion tracking system, aimed at classifying predicates as telic or atelic. The pilot data indicate that differences in the production of signed predicates can be used to estimate the likelihood of a predicate belonging to either class, based on its 3D motion signature.
