Source: while reading a document , a user may encounter concepts , entities , and topics that she is interested in exploring more . we propose models of interestingness , which aim to predict the level of interest a user has in the various text spans in a document . we obtain naturally occurring interest signals by observing user browsing behavior in clicks from one page to another . we cast the problem of predicting interestingness as a discriminative learning problem over this data . we leverage features from two principal sources : textual context features and topic features that assess the semantics of the document transition . we learn our topic features without supervision via probabilistic inference over a graphical model that captures the latent joint topic space of the documents in the transition . we train and test our models on millions of realworld transitions between wikipedia documents as observed from web browser session logs . on the task of predicting which spans are of most interest to users , we show significant improvement over various baselines and highlight the value of our latent semantic model .
human1: The problem that the paper contants to discuss is that readers are interested to read about some kinds of concepts, entities or topics more that others, and it is proposed to build a model predicting what spans of text are interesting to a reader in a particular document. To get the most out of web session logs, and to capitalise on the fact that interestingness is often defined as the likelihood of future use, we use naturally occurring signals generated by the browsing activity itself, document to document transitions, as the basis of our discriminative task learning problem. The text context features are combined using unsupervised topic features of similar documents as defined by probi-distinguish graphical model through topic space shared by the documents of different topic. As is further evidence of the value of latent semantics in the context of interest tracking the models do much better than baselines on large scale inference in millions of real transitions on Wikipedia.

1.        Why is this a valuable or novel problem? Is the challenge in modeling user attention span? Semantic understanding? Real-time adaptation?

4.        How does your approach generalize? Could it be used in education, news summarization, or content recommendation?
5.        What are the baseline models you compare against, and how much improvement does your model achieve over them? Is there any ground truth labeling of interesting spans beyond user click data?
irrelevant_question: 4.        How does your approach generalize? Could it be used in education, news summarization, or content recommendation?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the value of the model?
Human 2: When reading a document, users may come across concepts, entities, or topics they want to explore further. We introduce models of interestingness designed to predict the level of user interest in different spans of text. Natural interest signals are derived from observing user browsing behavior, specifically clicks from one page to another. We frame the prediction of interestingness as a discriminative learning task over this data. Our models utilize two main sources of features: textual context features and topic features that capture the semantics of document transitions. The topic features are learned in an unsupervised manner through probabilistic inference over a graphical model representing the latent joint topic space of the documents. We train and evaluate our models on millions of real-world transitions between Wikipedia pages, collected from web browser session logs. Experiments show substantial improvements over baseline methods and demonstrate the effectiveness of incorporating latent semantic information.

Source:
a machine learning approach to automatic term extraction using a rich feature set
in this paper we propose an automatic term extraction approach that uses machine learning incorporating varied and rich features of candidate terms . in our preliminary experiments , we also tested different attribute selection methods to verify which features are more relevant for automatic term extraction . we achieved state of the art results for unigram extraction in brazilian portuguese .

To determine the most pertinent features for automatic term extraction, the authors also tested a number of attribute selection techniques in their early tests. Their method produced cutting-edge outcomes for unigram extraction, particularly in Brazilian Portuguese.

Source:
generalizing local translation models
we investigate translation modeling based on exponential estimates which generalize essential components of standard translation models . in application to a hierarchical phrasebased system the simplest generalization allows its models of lexical selection and reordering to be conditioned on arbitrary attributes of the source sentence and its annotation . viewing these estimates as approximations of sentence-level probabilities motivates further elaborations that seek to exploit general syntactic and morphological patterns . dimensionality control with `1 regularizers makes it possible to negotiate the tradeoff between translation quality and decoding speed . putting together and extending several recent advances in phrase-based translation we arrive at a flexible modeling framework that allows efficient leveraging of monolingual resources and tools . experiments with features derived from the output of chinese and arabic parsers and an arabic lemmatizer show significant improvements over a strong baseline .
human1: In order to generalise important elements of conventional translation models, this paper investigates translation modelling using exponential estimates. The simplest generalisation allows lexical selection and reordering models to be conditioned on arbitrary properties of the source sentence and its annotation when used in a hierarchical phrase-based system. The study goes on to develop elaborations that aim to take advantage of general syntactic and morphological patterns, considering these estimates as approximations of sentence-level probabilities. The authors balance translation quality and decoding speed by using L 1 regularizers for dimensionality control. They create a versatile modelling framework that effectively makes use of monolingual resources and tools by incorporating and expanding on a number of recent developments in phrase-based translation. Experiments with features extracted from Arabic and Chinese parser output and an Arabic lemmatizer show notable gains over a robust baseline.

3. How did the use of L1 regularization help manage feature dimensionality, and what was the tradeoff between sparsity and performance?
4. Why was conditioning on morphological and syntactic patterns particularly useful for languages like Arabic and Chinese?
5. How are sentence-level probabilities approximated within this model, and how do they influence translation decisions?

Human 2: We explore translation modeling using exponential estimates that extend key elements of standard translation models. When applied to a hierarchical phrase-based system, the simplest extension allows lexical selection and reordering models to depend on various attributes of the source sentence and its annotations. Treating these estimates as approximations of sentence-level probabilities motivates further refinements that exploit syntactic and morphological patterns. Dimensionality control through L1 regularization enables balancing translation quality with decoding speed. Experiments incorporating features from Chinese and Arabic parsers, along with an Arabic lemmatizer, demonstrate substantial improvements over a strong baseline.

syntax is from mars while semantics from venus ! insights from spectral analysis of distributional similarity networks
we study the global topology of the syntactic and semantic distributional similarity networks for english through the technique of spectral analysis . we observe that while the syntactic network has a hierarchical structure with strong communities and their mixtures , the semantic network has several tightly knit communities along with a large core without any such welldefined community structure .

multilingual annotation and disambiguation of discourse connectives for
many discourse connectives can signal several types of relations between sentences . their automatic disambiguation , i.e . the labeling of the correct sense of each occurrence , is important for discourse parsing , but could also be helpful to machine translation . we describe new approaches for improving the accuracy of manual annotation of three discourse connectives ( two english , one french ) by using parallel corpora . an appropriate set of labels for each connective can be found using information from their translations . our results for automatic disambiguation are state-of-the-art , at up to 85 % accuracy using surface features . using feature analysis , contextual features are shown to be useful across languages and connectives .

By utilising parallel corpora, the authors offer innovative methods to increase the precision of manual annotation for three distinct discourse connectives (two in English and one in French). They show how a suitable set of labels for each connective can be found using information from translations. Using surface features, their automatic disambiguation results reach up to 85% accuracy, which is state-of-the-art. an analysis of bootstrapping for the recognition of temporal expressions
we present a semi-supervised ( bootstrapping ) approach to the extraction of time expression mentions in large unlabelled corpora . because the only supervision is in the form of seed examples , it becomes necessary to resort to heuristics to rank and filter out spurious patterns and candidate time expressions . the application of bootstrapping to time expression recognition is , to the best of our knowledge , novel . in this paper , we describe one such architecture for bootstrapping information extraction ( ie ) patterns suited to the extraction of entities , as opposed to events or relations and summarize our experimental findings . these point out to the fact that a pattern set with a good increase in recall with respect to the seeds is achievable within our framework while , on the other side , the decrease in precision in successive iterations is succesfully controlled through the use of ranking and selection heuristics . experiments are still underway to achieve the best use of these heuristics and other parameters of the bootstrapping algorithm .

These results show that by using ranking and selection heuristics, their framework can successfully control the decline in precision in subsequent iterations while achieving a notable increase in recall compared to the original seeds. learning dialogue strategies from older and younger simulated users
older adults are a challenging user group because their behaviour can be highly variable . to the best of our knowledge , this is the first study where dialogue strategies are learned and evaluated with both simulated younger users and simulated older users . the simulated users were derived from a corpus of interactions with a strict system-initiative spoken dialogue system ( sds ) . learning from simulated younger users leads to a policy which is close to one of the dialogue strategies of the underlying sds , while the simulated older users allow us to learn more flexible dialogue strategies that accommodate mixed initiative . we conclude that simulated users are a useful technique for modelling the behaviour of new user groups .

The results show that a policy that closely resembles one of the SDS's current dialogue strategies is produced by learning from simulated younger users. statistical machine translation improves question retrieval in community question answering via matrix factorization
community question answering ( cqa ) has become an increasingly popular research topic . in this paper , we focus on the problem of question retrieval . question retrieval in cqa can automatically find the most relevant and recent questions that have been solved by other users . however , the word ambiguity and word mismatch problems bring about new challenges for question retrieval in cqa . state-of-the-art approaches address these issues by implicitly expanding the queried questions with additional words or phrases using monolingual translation models . while useful , the effectiveness of these models is highly dependent on the availability of quality parallel monolingual corpora ( e.g. , question-answer pairs ) in the absence of which they are troubled by noise issue . in this work , we propose an alternative way to address the word ambiguity and word mismatch problems by taking advantage of potentially rich semantic information drawn from other languages . our proposed method employs statistical machine translation to improve question retrieval and enriches the question representation with the translated words from other languages via matrix factorization . experiments conducted on a real cqa data show that our proposed approach is promising .
human1: The problem of question retrieval in Community Question Answering (CQA), a developing field of study, is discussed in this paper. Although CQA seeks to automatically identify pertinent and recently resolved questions, word ambiguity and mismatch issues pose serious challenges. The state-of-the-art methods currently in use address these problems by employing monolingual translation models to implicitly expand the queried questions. Nevertheless, these models' efficacy is highly dependent on the availability of high-quality parallel monolingual corpora (such as question-answer pairs), and when such data is limited, noise considerably impairs their performance. The authors suggest a different approach that makes use of potentially rich semantic information from other languages in order to get around these restrictions. By enriching the question representation with translated terms from other languages through matrix factorisation, their innovative method uses statistical machine translation to improve question retrieval. Tests on actual CQA data show how promising their suggested approach is.

Human 2: Community Question Answering (CQA) has emerged as a popular area of research, with question retrieval being a key challenge. The goal of question retrieval is to automatically identify the most relevant and recently answered questions from other users. However, issues such as word ambiguity and word mismatch make this task difficult. Current state-of-the-art methods tackle these problems by implicitly expanding queries with additional words or phrases using monolingual translation models. While effective, their performance heavily depends on the availability of high-quality parallel monolingual corpora (e.g., question-answer pairs), and they are prone to noise when such corpora are lacking. In this work, we propose an alternative approach that leverages semantic information from other languages. Our method uses statistical machine translation to enhance question retrieval and enriches question representations with translated words via matrix factorization. Experiments on real CQA datasets indicate that this approach is promising.

learning to win by reading manuals in a monte-carlo framework
this paper presents a novel approach for leveraging automatically extracted textual knowledge to improve the performance of control applications such as games . our ultimate goal is to enrich a stochastic player with highlevel guidance expressed in text . our model jointly learns to identify text that is relevant to a given game state in addition to learning game strategies guided by the selected text . our method operates in the monte-carlo search framework , and learns both text analysis and game strategies based only on environment feedback . we apply our approach to the complex strategy game civilization ii using the official game manual as the text guide . our results show that a linguistically-informed game-playing agent significantly outperforms its language-unaware counterpart , yielding a 27 % absolute improvement and winning over 78 % of games when playing against the builtin ai of civilization ii .

The ultimate goal is to provide high-level text-based guidance to a stochastic player. The suggested model simultaneously learns game strategies based on the chosen text and recognises text pertinent to a specific game state. Crucially, the text analysis and game strategies are both learnt exclusively from environment feedback, and the method works within a Monte-Carlo search framework. Using the official game manual as a textual guide, the authors apply their methodology to the intricate strategy game Civilisation II. Their findings show that when compared to the built-in AI of Civilisation II, a linguistically-informed game-playing agent performs noticeably better than its language-unaware counterpart, winning over 78% of games and achieving a 27% absolute improvement.

Source: finding concepts in natural language utterances is a challenging task , especially given the scarcity of labeled data for learning semantic ambiguity . furthermore , data mismatch issues , which arise when the expected test ( target ) data does not exactly match the training data , aggravate this scarcity problem . to deal with these issues , we describe an efficient semisupervised learning ( ssl ) approach which has two components : ( i ) markov topic regression is a new probabilistic model to cluster words into semantic tags ( concepts ) . it can efficiently handle semantic ambiguity by extending standard topic models with two new features . first , it encodes word n-gram features from labeled source and unlabeled target data . second , by going beyond a bag-of-words approach , it takes into account the inherent sequential nature of utterances to learn semantic classes based on context . ( ii ) retrospective learner is a new learning technique that adapts to the unlabeled target data . our new ssl approach improves semantic tagging performance by 3 % absolute over the baseline models , and also compares favorably on semi-supervised syntactic tagging .

Markov Topic Regression represents the first one, working as a probabilistic model that partitions words into semantic labels successfully does so with ambiguity, by modeling sequential context and including word n-gram features. The second model is the model of the Retrospective Learner because it uses unlabeled target data on the model by grooming learned representations before training. sense and similarity : a study of sense-level similarity measures and torsten zesch
in this paper , we investigate the difference between word and sense similarity measures and present means to convert a state-of-the-art word similarity measure into a sense similarity measure . in order to evaluate the new measure , we create a special sense similarity dataset and re-rate an existing word similarity dataset using two different sense inventories from wordnet and wikipedia . we discover that word-level measures were not able to differentiate between different senses of one word , while sense-level measures actually increase correlation when shifting to sense similarities . sense-level similarity measures improve when evaluated with a re-rated sense-aware gold standard , while correlation with word-level similarity measures decreases .

a multi-teraflop constituency parser using gpus
constituency parsing with rich grammars remains a computational challenge . graphics processing units ( gpus ) have previously been used to accelerate cky chart evaluation , but gains over cpu parsers were modest . in this paper , we describe a collection of new techniques that enable chart evaluation at close to the gpus practical maximum speed ( a teraflop ) , or around a half-trillion rule evaluations per second . net parser performance on a 4-gpu system is over 1 thousand length30 sentences/second ( 1 trillion rules/sec ) , and 400 general sentences/second for the berkeley parser grammar . the techniques we introduce include grammar compilation , recursive symbol blocking , and cache-sharing .

On a 4-GPU system, the parser performs 400 general sentences per second using the Berkeley parser grammar and more than 1,000 sentences per second for sentences of length 30 (or 1 trillion rules/second). structured sparsity in structured prediction
linear models have enjoyed great success in structured prediction in nlp . while a lot of progress has been made on efficient training with several loss functions , the problem of endowing learners with a mechanism for feature selection is still unsolved . common approaches employ ad hoc filtering or l1regularization ; both ignore the structure of the feature space , preventing practicioners from encoding structural prior knowledge . we fill this gap by adopting regularizers that promote structured sparsity , along with efficient algorithms to handle them . experiments on three tasks ( chunking , entity recognition , and dependency parsing ) show gains in performance , compactness , and model interpretability .

Because of this, practitioners are unable to include structural prior knowledge in their models. The authors suggest employing regularizers that encourage structured sparsity and effective management algorithms to close this gap. Performance, model compactness, and interpretability are all improved in experiments on three tasks: dependency parsing, chunking, and entity recognition.

comparing and combining generative and posterior probability models : some advances in sentence boundary detection in speech
we compare and contrast two different models for detecting sentence-like units in continuous speech . the first approach uses hidden markov sequence models based on n-grams and maximum likelihood estimation , and employs model interpolation to combine different representations of the data . the second approach models the posterior probabilities of the target classes ; it is discriminative and integrates multiple knowledge sources in the maximum entropy ( maxent ) framework . both models combine lexical , syntactic , and prosodic information . we develop a technique for integrating pretrained probability models into the maxent framework , cand show that this approach can improve on an hmm-based state-of-the-art system for the sentence-boundary detection task . an even more substantial improvement is obtained by combining the posterior probabilities of the two systems .
human1: In this work, two different models for recognising sentence-like units in continuous speech are compared. The first model is a generative method that combines different data representations through model interpolation and uses hidden Markov sequence models based on n-grams and maximum likelihood estimation. The second model is a discriminative method that integrates various knowledge sources within the Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) framework to model the posterior probabilities of target classes. Lexical, syntactic, and prosodic information are all included in both models. The authors show how an HMM-based state-of-the-art system for sentence-boundary detection can be improved by incorporating pre-trained probability models into the MaxEnt framework. Combining the posterior probabilities produced by the two systems results in a more notable improvement.

Human 2: We compare and contrast two approaches for detecting sentence-like units in continuous speech. The first method employs Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) based on n-grams and maximum likelihood estimation, using model interpolation to merge different data representations. The second method models the posterior probabilities of the target classes, adopting a discriminative approach that integrates multiple knowledge sources within a Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) framework. Both approaches utilize lexical, syntactic, and prosodic cues. We also introduce a technique for incorporating pretrained probability models into the MaxEnt framework and demonstrate that this can outperform a state-of-the-art HMM-based system for sentence boundary detection. Further improvements are achieved by combining the posterior probabilities from both models.

Source: we present results of our experiments with the application of machine learning on binary blog classification , i.e . determining whether a given web page is a blog page . we have gathered a corpus in excess of half a million blog or blog-like pages and pre-classified them using a simple baseline . we investigate which algorithms attain the best results for our classification problem and experiment with resampling techniques , with the aim of utilising our large dataset to improve upon our baseline . we show that the application of off-the-shelf machine learning technology to perform binary blog classification offers substantial improvement over our baseline . further gains can sometimes be achieved using resampling techniques , but these improvements are relatively small compared to the initial gain .
human1: The present paper is an empirical report covering the application of machine learning to classify blogs such like defining in a blog or not using a web page. With a half million pages-plus, pre-identified, simple, baseline level of quality, blog or blog-like pages, we consider the results of several machine learning algorithms on this job. We also show how the resampling techniques can influence the large dataset so as to make it more useful. First, the combined results suggest that, standard machine learning modules should work significantly better than our initial baseline, and marginal gains can be achieved by resampling.

Human 2: We report the results of experiments applying machine learning to binary blog classification, specifically determining whether a given web page qualifies as a blog. We compiled a corpus of over half a million blog or blog-like pages, initially labeled using a simple baseline method. Our study explores which algorithms perform best for this classification task and examines the use of resampling techniques to leverage the large dataset for improved results. Source: semantic role labeling annotation task depends on the correct identification of predicates , before identifying arguments and assigning them role labels . however , most predicates are not constituted only by a verb : they constitute complex predicates ( cps ) not yet available in a computational lexicon . in order to create a dictionary of cps , this study employs a corpus-based methodology . searches are guided by pos tags instead of a limited list of verbs or nouns , in contrast to similar studies . results include ( but are not limited to ) light and support verb constructions . these cps are classified into idiomatic and less idiomatic . this paper presents an in-depth analysis of this phenomenon , as well as an original resource containing a set of 773 annotated expressions . both constitute an original and rich contribution for nlp tools in brazilian portuguese that perform tasks involving semantics .
human1: The present paper focuses on the problem of spotting the complex predicates (CPs) in the frame of the semantic role labeling (SRL), when valid predict ones should be caught and only after that any semantic role may be assigned to any argument. Since the study accepted that most predicates are not simple verbs and such CPs are poorly represented in computerized lexicons, the research has followed a corpus-driven method in development of a CPs dictionary in Brazilian Portuguese. Contrary to earlier attempts at using precompiled lists of nouns or verbs, part-of-speech (POS) tags are another means of more comprehensive search coverage as used in this methodology. The derived collection contains 773 annotated usages, which grasp both idiomatic and less idiomatic constructions in light verbs and support verbs. The article includes a linguistic analysis of these terms that is useful to the development of NLP tools based on semantic oriented tasks of Brazilian Portuguese.

predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight the contribution of the corpus-based methodology?
Human 2: The task of semantic role labeling relies on accurately identifying predicates before detecting their arguments and assigning role labels. However, many predicates are not simple verbs but form complex predicates (CPs) that are not yet represented in computational lexicons. To build a dictionary of CPs, this study adopts a corpus-based methodology, using POS tags to guide searches rather than relying on a restricted list of verbs or nouns, as done in previous studies. The resulting CPs include, among others, light and support verb constructions, which are further categorized as idiomatic or less idiomatic. This paper provides a detailed analysis of these phenomena and introduces a novel resource comprising 773 annotated expressions. Together, these contributions offer a valuable and original resource for NLP tools in Brazilian Portuguese that handle semantic tasks.

Source: sentiment analysis systems can benefit from the translation of sentiment information . we present a novel , graph-based approach using simrank , a well-established graph-theoretic algorithm , to transfer sentiment information from a source language to a target language . we evaluate this method in comparison with semantic orientation using pointwise mutual information ( so-pmi ) , an established unsupervised method for learning the sentiment of phrases .

In applying SimRank which is a proven simple graph-theoretic algorithm, the process in this method is to transfer the sentiment associations formed by the source language to the target language that is rich in resources. The efficiency of such strategy is compared with Semantic Orientation basing on Pointwise Mutual Information (SO-PMI) which is a baseline unsupervised method of learning phrase-level sentiment. The findings confirm that the graph-based approach has the potential to be a reliable option in replacing sentiment learning in languages that have few annotated data.

Source: in this paper , we present an integrated model of the two central tasks of dialog management : interpreting user actions and generating system actions . we model the interpretation task as a classication problem and the generation task as a prediction problem . these two tasks are interleaved in an incremental parsing-based dialog model . we compare three alternative parsing methods for this dialog model using a corpus of human-human spoken dialog from a catalog ordering domain that has been annotated for dialog acts and task/subtask information . we contrast the amount of context provided by each method and its impact on performance .
human1: The given paper introduces an integrated structure of dialog management that combines two of the most fundamental operations: user action interpretation and system response generation. Interpretation is windowed as a classification issue and response generation as a prediction task. These elements are mixed in a demand-driven, parsing-based conversational model. The paper investigates three possible parsing strategies upon a corpus of annotated spoken dialogs of the human-human dialog on a domain in a catalog ordering area, with the annotation of dialog acts and of task/subtasks structure. The comparison shows the impact of the quantity of the contextual information offered by a given method on the overall performance within the system.

Human 2: In this paper, we propose an integrated model addressing the two core tasks of dialogue management: interpreting user actions and generating system responses. Source: multi-word expressions ( mwes ) account for a large portion of the language used in dayto-day interactions . a formal system that is flexible enough to model these large and often syntactically-rich non-compositional chunks as single units in naturally occurring text could considerably simplify large-scale semantic annotation projects , in which it would be undesirable to have to develop internal compositional analyses of common technical expressions that have specific idiosyncratic meanings . this paper will first define a notion of functorargument decomposition on phrase structure trees analogous to graph coloring , in which the tree is cast as a graph , and the elementary structures of a grammar formalism are colors . the paper then presents a formal argument that tree-rewriting systems , a class of grammar formalism that includes tree adjoining grammars , are able to produce a proper superset of the functor-argument decompositions that string-rewriting systems can produce .
human1: Multi-word units (MWEs) are ubiquitous in colloquial speech and usually have elaborate syntax and non-combinatorial meaning. Formalizing these expressions as unit-heads of a system can drastically help alleviate the loads of semantic annotation overheads when it is more infeasible to subdivide technical or idiomatic expressions much used. In this paper we extend this idea to phrase structure trees, and in analogy with graph coloring, we think of trees and grammatical elementary structures as graphs, colors, respectively. It also gives a mathematical argument that tree-rewriting systems, like tree adjoining grammars, can produce a strictly larger number of such decompositions than string-rewriting systems hence providing more leeway to expressing MWEs in language analysis.
llm_questions:
1.        What exact problem are you trying to solve by treating MWEs as unit-heads? Who specifically benefits from this solution?
2.        Could you explain why current methods are inadequate at handling MWEs in phrase structure trees?
3.        What exactly does it mean to treat MWEs as “unit-heads of a system”? How is graph coloring being used as an analogy?
4.        Could this abstract be understandable to readers outside of formal grammar theory?
5.        Have you applied this method to any real-world corpus or NLP task ?
6.        Is your extension to phrase structure trees a new theoretical contribution or an application of existing results?
irrelevant_question: 5.        Have you applied this method to any real-world corpus or NLP task ?
predicted_question: Does the abstract define unit-heads?
Human 2: Multi-word expressions (MWEs) make up a significant part of everyday language. Developing a formal system capable of treating these large, often syntactically complex, non-compositional units as single entities in natural text could greatly streamline large-scale semantic annotation projects, avoiding the need to construct detailed compositional analyses for common technical expressions with unique meanings. This paper first introduces a concept of functor–argument decomposition on phrase structure trees, analogous to graph coloring, where a tree is represented as a graph and the elementary grammar structures are treated as colors. We then provide a formal argument showing that tree-rewriting systems a class of grammar formalisms including tree adjoining grammars can generate a proper superset of the functor argument decompositions achievable by string-rewriting systems.

Source: we study a novel shallow information extraction problem that involves extracting sentences of a given set of topic categories from medical forum data . given a corpus of medical forum documents , our goal is to extract two related types of sentences that describe a biomedical case ( i.e. , medical problem descriptions and medical treatment descriptions ) . such an extraction task directly generates medical case descriptions that can be useful in many applications . we solve the problem using two popular machine learning methods support vector machines ( svm ) and conditional random fields ( crf ) . we propose novel features to improve the accuracy of extraction . experiment results show that we can obtain an accuracy of up to 75 % .
human1: The given paper is devoted to a new shallow information extraction task that aims at the recognition of topic-specific sentences in data collected in medical forums. In particular, this is expected to extract two important categories of sentences namely those ones that narrate medical issues and those sentences that narrate medical treatment strategies to a corpus of medical related forums documents. The case descriptions obtained could be used in clinical decision support, biomedical research and patient education. We are using two machine learning approaches to this task: Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Conditional Random Fields (CRF), and suggest new sets of features contributing to their better performance. The results obtained by experimentation prove that the suggested method is applicable with an extraction accuracy of up to 75 percent, which indicates the potential of its application in automated generation of medical cases descriptions.

Human 2: We investigate a new shallow information extraction task that focuses on identifying sentences belonging to specific topic categories within medical forum data. From a corpus of forum documents, our aim is to extract two related types of sentences describing biomedical cases: medical problem descriptions and treatment descriptions. Successfully performing this task produces structured medical case summaries, which have practical applications in various domains. We approach the problem using two widely used machine learning techniques, support vector machines (SVM) and conditional random fields (CRF), and introduce novel feature sets to enhance extraction accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves up to 75% accuracy.

Source: the lack of positive results on supervised domain adaptation for wsd have cast some doubts on the utility of handtagging general corpora and thus developing generic supervised wsd systems . in this paper we show for the first time that our wsd system trained on a general source corpus ( bnc ) and the target corpus , obtains up to 22 % error reduction when compared to a system trained on the target corpus alone . in addition , we show that as little as 40 % of the target corpus ( when supplemented with the source corpus ) is sufficient to obtain the same results as training on the full target data . the key for success is the use of unlabeled data with svd , a combination of kernels and svm .
human1: The current paper deals with the issues of supervised domain adaptation in word sense disambiguation (WSD) where previous research efforts have not been very fruitful and there is a general belief that it is not worth the time and effort spent in pinpointing the generic corpora in terms of hand-tagging. It is the initial indicative evidence that a training corpus (the British National Corpus, BNC) of a general source of information as well as a training corpus of a target domain that was used to train a WSD system can diminish error by up to 22 percent with respect to a scenario in which only the target data is used to train the system. Also, we prove that in the setting where labeled data make up 40 percent of the labeled data at the target, along with the source corpus, the same amount of performance could be achieved should the whole labeled data at the target be trained on. The latter are made possible by the inclusion of unlabeled data via singular value decomposition (SVD), and by the use of a mixture of kernel techniques and support vector machines (SVMs), which suggests the viability of hybrid methods in regard to domain adaptation in WSD.

Human 2: The limited success of supervised domain adaptation for word sense disambiguation (WSD) has raised questions about the value of manually annotating general corpora and developing broadly applicable supervised WSD systems. In this paper, we demonstrate for the first time that a WSD system trained on both a general source corpus (BNC) and a target corpus achieves up to a 22% reduction in error compared to a system trained solely on the target corpus. Furthermore, we show that using only 40% of the target corpus, when combined with the source corpus, is sufficient to match the performance of training on the full target dataset. The key to this improvement lies in leveraging unlabeled data with singular value decomposition (SVD) along with a combination of kernel methods and SVMs.

Source: this paper describes a question answering system which retrieves answers from structured data regarding cinemas and movies . the system represents the first prototype of a multilingual and multimodal qa system for the domain of tourism . based on specially designed domain ontology and using textual entailment as a means for semantic inference , the system can be used in both monolingual and cross-language settings with slight adjustments for new input languages .

Source: the paper is concerned with automatic classification of new lexical items into synonymic sets on the basis of their cooccurrence data obtained from a corpus . our goal is to examine the impact that different types of linguistic preprocessing of the cooccurrence material have on the classification accuracy . the paper comparatively studies several preprocessing techniques frequently used for this and similar tasks and makes conclusions about their relative merits . we find that a carefully chosen preprocessing procedure achieves a relative effectiveness improvement of up to 88 % depending on the classification method in comparison to the window-based context delineation , along with using much smaller feature space .

The paper compares some of the widely applied forms of preprocessing and analyses their performative adequacy. Source: differences between large-scale historical population archives and small decentralized databases can be used to improve data quality and record connectedness in both types of databases . a parser is developed to account for differences in syntax and data representation models . a matching procedure is described to discover records from different databases referring to the same historical event . the problem of verification without reliable benchmark data is addressed by matching on a subset of record attributes and measuring support for the match using a different subset of attributes . an application of the matching procedure for comparison of family trees is discussed . a visualization tool is described to present an interactive overview of comparison results .

Human 2: Differences between large-scale historical population archives and smaller, decentralized databases can be leveraged to enhance data quality and improve record connectivity in both settings. We develop a parser to handle variations in syntax and data representation formats. A matching procedure is then proposed to identify records across different databases that correspond to the same historical event. To address the challenge of verification without reliable benchmarks, matches are based on a subset of record attributes, while support for these matches is assessed using a complementary set of attributes. We illustrate the procedure by comparing family trees and introduce a visualization tool that provides an interactive summary of the comparison results.

Source: predicting the success of literary works is a curious question among publishers and aspiring writers alike . we examine the quantitative connection , if any , between writing style and successful literature . based on novels over several different genres , we probe the predictive power of statistical stylometry in discriminating successful literary works , and identify characteristic stylistic elements that are more prominent in successful writings . our study reports for the first time that statistical stylometry can be surprisingly effective in discriminating highly successful literature from less successful counterpart , achieving accuracy up to 84 % . closer analyses lead to several new insights into characteristics of the writing style in successful literature , including findings that are contrary to the conventional wisdom with respect to good writing style and readability .
human1: The research considers the ability of the writing style of a book to influence its future success, which is of interest to both the publishers and the authors of novels. The study compares the results of statistical procedures commonly referred to as stylometry when applied to novels of various genres in order to understand how successful novels can be identified as opposed to less popular ones. The results indicate that some of these aspects of the writing style are more likely to occur in the popular books, and stylometry can be used to predict success of a literary work with a probability of 84 percent. Interestingly, one also finds in the study things against conventional notions of how a good piece of writing should be or how it can be read, and it presents a different view on what defines good literature.

Human 2: Predicting which literary works will achieve success is a topic of interest for both publishers and aspiring authors. In this study, we investigate whether there is a measurable link between writing style and literary success. Using novels from multiple genres, we assess the ability of statistical stylometry to distinguish between successful and less successful works, and we identify stylistic features that tend to appear more frequently in popular literature. Our results show, for the first time, that statistical stylometry can be surprisingly effective in predicting literary success, achieving accuracy rates of up to 84%. Further analysis provides new insights into the stylistic traits of successful writing, including findings that challenge traditional assumptions about what constitutes good style and readability.

Source: in this paper , we describe how field linguists can use the wordseye linguistics tool ( welt ) to study endangered languages . welt is a tool under development for eliciting endangered language data and formally documenting a language , based on wordseye ( coyne and sproat , 2001 ) , a text-to-scene generation tool that produces 3d scenes from text input . first , a linguist uses welt to create elicitation materials and collect language data . next , he or she uses welt to formally document the language . finally , the formal models are used to create a textto-scene system that takes input in the endangered language and generates a picture representing its meaning .
human1: Through the present paper, it will be explained by field linguists how the Wordseye Linguistics Tool (WELT) can be utilized in order to facilitate studying and preserving endangered languages. Among those that are currently under development is a WELT which assists in gathering of language data along with formulating it in a formal company. It is founded on Wordseye that translates the written language into 3-D imaging environments. In WELT, linguists produce materials in the first place in order to elicit language data on the part of native speakers. After that, they construct formal theories of the language using the tool. Such models are then applied to construct a system that translates text to text in the endangered language and visually depicts meaning in a form of an image representing its semantics, which in addition to serving as a means of documentation is also a very entertaining and easy to perception mean of exploring language use.

Human 2: This paper presents how field linguists can employ the WordSeer Linguistics Tool (WELT) to study endangered languages. WELT is being developed to facilitate the elicitation of language data and formal documentation, building on WordSeer (Coyne and Sproat, 2001), a text-to-scene system that generates 3D visualizations from textual input. Initially, linguists use WELT to design elicitation materials and gather linguistic data. Next, the tool supports the formal documentation of the language. Finally, the resulting formal models are applied to a text-to-scene system that interprets input in the endangered language and produces images representing the conveyed meaning.

Source: starting from first principles , we re-visit the statistical approach and study two forms of the bayes decision rule : the common rule for minimizing the number of string errors and a novel rule for minimizing the number of symbols errors . the bayes decision rule for minimizing the number of string errors is widely used , e.g . in speech recognition , pos tagging and machine translation , but its justification is rarely questioned . to minimize the number of symbol errors as is more suitable for a task like pos tagging , we show that another form of the bayes decision rule can be derived . the major purpose of this paper is to show that the form of the bayes decision rule should not be taken for granted ( as it is done in virtually all statistical nlp work ) , but should be adapted to the error measure being used . we present first experimental results for pos tagging tasks .
human1: This paper recapitulates the premises of the statistical approach of natural language processing by reviewing two forms of the Bayes decision rule. The currently popular version tries to minimize errors in the overall output sequences of those tasks, whereas we put forward a new version trying to minimize errors on individual units, e.g. words or tags. In spite of the fact that the standard rule is very popular in speech recognition, machine translation and POS tagging tasks, its appropriateness is hardly doubted. We claim that it will depend on the type of error that one would wish to minimize, whether to select either of the two decision rules. In other tasks such as POS tagging where symbol accuracy is of more importance a variant of the Bayes rule is suitable. We back this assertion with some preliminary experimental findings of POS tagging activity that indicates that a rule level adaptation to the measurement criterion can result in enhanced performance.

3.        Beyond POS tagging, what other NLP tasks could benefit from your rule variant? Are there applications in named entity recognition, parsing, or sentiment analysis?
4.        How were the POS tagging experiments conducted? Which corpus, which tagger, what baseline?

6.        How might this finding affect evaluation strategies in future NLP research or benchmarks?

Human 2: Starting from first principles, we revisit the statistical framework and examine two variants of the Bayes decision rule: the traditional version aimed at minimizing the number of string errors, and a novel variant designed to reduce the number of symbol errors. While the standard Bayes rule for string-error minimization is widely applied in areas such as speech recognition, POS tagging, and machine translation, its underlying assumptions are rarely scrutinized. For tasks like POS tagging, where minimizing symbol errors is more relevant, we demonstrate that an alternative form of the Bayes decision rule can be derived. The main goal of this study is to emphasize that the choice of Bayes decision rule should be guided by the error metric in use, rather than assumed by default, as is common in most statistical NLP research. We also provide initial experimental results on POS tagging tasks to illustrate this point.

Source: syntactic reordering approaches are an effective method for handling word-order differences between source and target languages in statistical machine translation ( smt ) systems . this paper introduces a reordering approach for translation from chinese to english . we describe a set of syntactic reordering rules that exploit systematic differences between chinese and english word order . the resulting system is used as a preprocessor for both training and test sentences , transforming chinese sentences to be much closer to english in terms of their word order . we evaluated the reordering approach within the moses phrase-based smt system ( koehn et al , 2007 ) . the reordering approach improved the bleu score for the moses system from 28.52 to 30.86 on the nist 2006 evaluation data . we also conducted a series of experiments to analyze the accuracy and impact of different types of reordering rules .
human1: This paper proposes a syntactic reordering technique to Chinese to English translation of statistical machine translation (SMT). This approach relies on a set of grammar-based rules that deal with typical word order variation between the Chinese and English. They are used as a preprocessing operation on training and test data with the aim of getting Chinese sentences closer to the English word order. The method was compared to the Moses phrase-based SMT system and we concluded that it did increase the quality of translation, and the BLEU score jumped to 30.86 to 28.52 on the NIST 2006 data. Other experiments that could check the precision and impact using alternative types of reordering rule were also carried out which could help in giving more details on the contribution of these rules to performance of translation.

3.        Is there any insight into error analysis—for instance, which types of sentence structures benefit most from the reordering?
4.        Is your approach language-pair specific, or could the methodology be adapted to other pairs with similar structural differences?
5.        Were human evaluations also conducted to validate translation fluency or adequacy, in addition to BLEU? Was the BLEU improvement consistent across multiple test sets, or only on NIST 2006?

Human 2: Syntactic reordering has proven to be an effective strategy for addressing word-order discrepancies between source and target languages in statistical machine translation (SMT) systems. In this paper, we present a reordering method specifically for Chinese-to-English translation. We define a set of syntactic rules that leverage consistent differences between Chinese and English word orders. These rules are applied as a preprocessing step to both training and test sentences, aligning Chinese sentence structures more closely with English. We evaluated this approach within the Moses phrase-based SMT framework (Koehn et al., 2007), observing an improvement in BLEU score from 28.52 to 30.86 on the NIST 2006 evaluation set. Additionally, we performed experiments to examine the accuracy and effects of various types of reordering rules.

Source: while large-scale corpora and various corpus query tools have long been recognized as essential language resources , the value of word association norms as language resources has been largely overlooked . this paper conducts some initial comparisons of the lexical relationships observed within japanese collocation data extracted from a large corpus using the japanese language version of the sketch engine ( ske ) tool ( srdanovi et al , 2008 ) and the relationships found within japanese word association sets taken from the large-scale japanese word association database ( jwad ) under ongoing construction by joyce ( 2005 , 2007 ) . the comparison results indicate that while some relationships are common to both linguistic resources , many lexical relationships are only observed in one resource . these findings suggest that both resources are necessary in order to more adequately cover the diverse range of lexical relationships . finally , the paper reflects briefly on the implementation of association-based word-search strategies into electronic dictionaries proposed by zock and bilac ( 2004 ) and zock ( 2006 ) .
human1: Whereas the availability of large corpora as well as corpus analysis tools is considered one of the most important language research resources, word association norms are a much less discussed subject. The current paper provides some preliminary cross-examination between the Japanese collocation data data -retrieved by a large corpus utilizing the Japanese version of the Sketch Engine- and the Japanese word association data which is collected through the running Japanese Word Association Database (JWAD). The findings indicate that although there are some of the connections between words that can be found in both sources, many of them can be identified only in collocation data or norms of associations. Such findings imply that the two different types of the resources will be necessary to represent the entire range of word relationships in the Japanese language. It is also mentioned in the paper how implementing word association strategies, such as in such past propositions by Zock and others, might possibly be implemented into electronic dictionaries.

Human 2: Although large-scale corpora and corpus query tools have long been regarded as crucial language resources, the potential of word association norms has received comparatively little attention. This paper presents an initial comparison between lexical relationships identified in Japanese collocation data extracted from a large corpus using the Japanese version of the Sketch Engine (SKE) tool (Srdanovi et al., 2008) and those found in Japanese word association sets from the ongoing Japanese Word Association Database (JWAD) compiled by Joyce (2005, 2007). Our results show that while some lexical relations appear in both resources, many are unique to one of the datasets. These findings suggest that combining both types of resources is necessary to capture the full diversity of lexical relationships. Finally, we briefly discuss how association-based word-search methods could be integrated into electronic dictionaries, as proposed by Zock and Bilac (2004) and Zock (2006).

Source: mother tongue interference is the phenomenon where linguistic systems of a mother tongue are transferred to another language . recently , nagata and whittaker ( 2013 ) have shown that language family relationship among mother tongues is preserved in english written by indoeuropean language speakers because of mother tongue interference . at the same time , their findings further introduce the following two research questions : ( 1 ) does the preservation universally hold in non-native english other than in english of indo-european language speakers ( 2 ) is the preservation independent of proficiency in english in this paper , we address these research questions . we first explore the two research questions empirically by reconstructing language family trees from english texts written by speakers of asian languages . we then discuss theoretical reasons for the empirical results . we finally introduce another hypothesis called the existence of a probabilistic module to explain why the preservation does or does not hold in particular situations .
human1: Interference caused by mother tongue is whereby features of the native language transfer to another language. And in a recent study Nagata and Whittaker (2013) showed that the genealogical structure of the Indo-European languages remains articulated within the process of translating texts into English in a language that is not native, probably due to the interference of their mother tongue. This pattern of preservation poses two critical research questions as far as their findings are concerned; (1) Is this pattern true of non-native English produced by speakers of non-Indo-European languages? Does this maintenance not rely on English language proficiency? (2) These questions are empirically explored in this paper, through reconstructions of language family trees based on text of English written by the speakers of different Asian languages. We proceed to theoretically discuss the visualized patterns, and hypothesize in the existence of a probabilistic cognitive module, which serves to give an understanding of the when and why, such preservation of genealogy may be facilitated or not.

predicted_question: Does the abstract explain interference?
Human 2: Mother-tongue interference refers to the transfer of linguistic properties from a speaker’s native language into another language they are learning. Nagata and Whittaker (2013) recently demonstrated that English written by speakers of Indo-European languages still reflects their native language family due to such interference. Their findings raise two further questions:(1) Does this pattern also appear in English produced by speakers of non–Indo-European languages?(2) Is the effect independent of a writer’s English proficiency?In this paper, we investigate these questions by empirically reconstructing language family trees from English texts authored by speakers of various Asian languages. We then provide theoretical explanations for the observed outcomes. Finally, we propose an additional hypothesis the presence of a probabilistic module to account for situations where the preservation of language family traits either emerges or fails to appear.

Source: to overcome the shortage of labeled data for implicit discourse relation recognition , previous works attempted to automatically generate training data by removing explicit discourse connectives from sentences and then built models on these synthetic implicit examples . however , a previous study ( sporleder and lascarides , 2008 ) showed that models trained on these synthetic data do not generalize very well to natural ( i.e . genuine ) implicit discourse data . in this work we revisit this issue and present a multi-task learning based system which can effectively use synthetic data for implicit discourse relation recognition . results on pdtb data show that under the multi-task learning framework our models with the use of the prediction of explicit discourse connectives as auxiliary learning tasks , can achieve an averaged f1 improvement of 5.86 % over baseline models .
human1: The absence of labeled data on implicit discourse relation recognition e.g., Walter et al. has been considered in previous research, which proposes the use of synthetic training data which involves eliminating explicitly coded discourse connectives e.g., Walter et al. Nevertheless, it is the findings of past study (Sporleder and Lascarides, 2008) giving demonstration that the models so trained with these types of constructed data could not effectively generalize to naturally given implicit discourse relations. In this work, we reconsider this difficulty and design a multi-task learning framework, which is capable of utilizing synthetic data to the greater extent when it comes to the implicit discourse relation recognition. The prediction of explicit discourse connectives is considered as one of the auxiliary tasks, which promotes the control of a primary learning goal. The testing of the Penn Discourse Treebank (PDTB) proves that our approach outperforms the baseline models by an average increase in F1 scores equal to 5.86.

irrelevant_question: 1.        What specific multi-task learning setup do you use? Do you share encoders, losses, or use any task-specific heads?
predicted_question: Does the abstract highlight the importance of labeling implicit discourse connectives?
Human 2: To address the lack of annotated data for recognizing implicit discourse relations, earlier research tried generating training material automatically by deleting explicit discourse markers from sentences and treating the resulting text as implicit examples. However, prior work (Sporleder & Lascarides, 2008) found that models trained solely on such synthetic data fail to transfer well to genuine implicit discourse instances. In this study, we re-examine this problem and introduce a multi-task learning approach that can make effective use of synthetic data. Using the prediction of explicit discourse connectives as auxiliary tasks within the multi-task framework, our models achieve an average F1 improvement of 5.86% on PDTB datasets compared to baseline systems.

Source: the tree-transducer grammars that arise in current syntactic machine translation systems are large , flat , and highly lexicalized . we address the problem of parsing efficiently with such grammars in three ways . first , we present a pair of grammar transformations that admit an efficient cubic-time cky-style parsing algorithm despite leaving most of the grammar in n-ary form . second , we show how the number of intermediate symbols generated by this transformation can be substantially reduced through binarization choices . finally , we describe a two-pass coarse-to-fine parsing approach that prunes the search space using predictions from a subset of the original grammar . in all , parsing time reduces by 81 % . we also describe a coarse-to-fine pruning scheme for forest-based language model reranking that allows a 100-fold increase in beam size while reducing decoding time . the resulting translations improve by 1.3 bleu .
human1: The tree-transducer grammars employed in contemporary syntactic machine translation systems are normally enormous, planar, and very populated with lexical entries hence a major problem of parsing the grammar items efficiently exists. In this paper, the contributing role in this issue is covered by three major contributions. The first in our plan is to introduce two grammar transformations that enable an efficient cubic-time CKY-style parser to be provided, whilst still leaving most of the grammar in its n-ary representation. Second, we demonstrate the fact that the amount of intermediate symbols may be specified by the choice of comparisons considered successfully using the choices of binarization considerably.Third, we suggest two-pass coarse-to-fine parsing solution that trims down the search space with predictions based on simplified sub-set of the initial grammar. In total, these methods cause a decrease in the parsing time by 81%. We also introduce a coarse-to-fine beam pruning strategy of forest-based language model reranking, and it can increase the beam by 100 times, and also shortens the decoding time. Through this method, better translation quality can be obtained with 1.3 BLEU score improvement.

predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key contributions?
Human 2: Tree-transducer grammars used in modern syntax-based machine translation tend to be large, shallow, and heavily lexicalized. We tackle the challenge of parsing these grammars efficiently through three contributions. First, we introduce two grammar transformations that allow a cubic-time CKY-style parser to be applied while still keeping most of the grammar in its original n-ary structure. Second, we demonstrate that careful choices in binarization can greatly reduce the number of intermediate symbols introduced by the transformation. Third, we present a two-stage coarse-to-fine parsing strategy that limits the search space by relying on predictions derived from a selected subset of the grammar. Overall, these techniques cut parsing time by 81%. We further propose a coarse-to-fine pruning method for forest-based language-model reranking, enabling a 100× increase in beam size together with faster decoding. These improvements yield translations that are 1.3 BLEU points better.

Source: with the proliferation of user-generated articles over the web , it becomes imperative to develop automated methods that are aware of the ideological-bias implicit in a document collection . while there exist methods that can classify the ideological bias of a given document , little has been done toward understanding the nature of this bias on a topical-level . in this paper we address the problem of modeling ideological perspective on a topical level using a factored topic model . we develop efficient inference algorithms using collapsed gibbs sampling for posterior inference , and give various evaluations and illustrations of the utility of our model on various document collections with promising results . finally we give a metropolis-hasting inference algorithm for a semi-supervised extension with decent results .
human1: As user-generated content has skyrocketed on the web, there has been growing importance to establish automated techniques that are able to identify ideological bias in document collections. Although previous methods might be used to determine the general ideological orientation of a text, there has been scant regard on analysis of bias at the topic level. In the present paper, we reduce this shortcoming by proposing a factored topic model that aims at modeling ideological aspect in topics. Effective algorithms in inference are built on collapsed Gibbs sampling and posterior using Gibbs sample, and the performance of our model is proved and shows promising performance on experiment carried out on various document collections. Besides, we propose a Metropolis-Hastings inference procedure of a semi-supervised variant of our model, which is also favorable.

5.        Could you explain in one sentence how ideology is embedded or represented in your model?
irrelevant_question: 4.        You mention collapsed Gibbs sampling and Metropolis-Hastings—is one used for the unsupervised model and one for the semi-supervised?
predicted_question: How can the abstract highlight key features of the model?
Human 2: As user-generated content continues to expand across the web, there is a growing need for automated techniques that can detect ideological bias within document collections. Although existing approaches can identify the political leaning of an individual document, far less work examines how such bias manifests at the topical level. In this paper, we propose a factored topic model designed to capture ideological perspectives within specific topics. We introduce efficient posterior inference procedures based on collapsed Gibbs sampling and demonstrate the model’s effectiveness through multiple experiments and visualizations on different text corpora. In addition, we present a Metropolis–Hastings–based inference method for a semi-supervised variant of the model, achieving reasonably strong performance.

Source: data archeology is a theoreticallyinformed approach to make sense of the digital artifacts left behind by a prior learning civilization . critical elements include use of theoretical learning models to construct analytic metrics , attention to temporality as a means to reconstruct individual and collective trajectories , and consideration of the pedagogical and technological structures framing activity . examples of the approach using discussion forum trace data will be presented .

Source: previous research has established several methods of online learning for latent dirichlet alocation ( lda ) . however , streaming learning for lda allowing only one pass over the data and constant storage complexityis not as well explored . we use reservoir sampling to reduce the storage complexity of a previously-studied online algorithm , namely the particle filter , to constant . we then show that a simpler particle filter implementation performs just as well , and that the quality of the initialization dominates other factors of performance .
human1: A number of ways to teach Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) online have been presented in the previous studies. Streaming learning environments (where just one access over the data is possible and complexity of the storage should be fixed) have however not been extensively dealt with. This is done in this work, where in applying reservoir sampling we make the storage complexity of a already studied online algorithm, the particle filter, constant bound. We demonstrate also that a simplified version of the particle filter exhibit equal performance, and that quality of model initialization is a determinant factor in the performance of the particle filter, in comparison to other variables.

Human 2: Earlier work has introduced a variety of online learning techniques for latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA). However, approaches that support true streaming learning—requiring only a single pass through the data while keeping storage fixed—have received far less attention. In this paper, we apply reservoir sampling to an existing online method, the particle filter, bringing its memory usage down to a constant level. We further demonstrate that a more streamlined version of the particle filter achieves comparable performance, and that the choice of initialization has a stronger impact on results than other implementation details.

Source: in this paper , we conducted a systematic comparative analysis of language in different contexts of bursty topics , including web search , news media , blogging , and social bookmarking . we analyze ( 1 ) the content similarity and predictability between contexts , ( 2 ) the coverage of search content by each context , and ( 3 ) the intrinsic coherence of information in each context . our experiments show that social bookmarking is a better predictor to the bursty search queries , but news media and social blogging media have a much more compelling coverage . this comparison provides insights on how the search behaviors and social information sharing behaviors of users are correlated to the professional news media in the context of bursty events .
human1: Here, we aim to give a systematic comparative analysis of language use to various platforms of bursty topic events such as web search, news media, blogs and social book marking. We check: (1) similarity of content and cross-context predictability, (2) how much each context is involved with search-related content, (3) internal consistency of information within a context. Experimental findings show that social bookmarking is a powerful forecast of bursty search queries and the news and blogging sites have more detailed content coverage. Such results provide useful information about how user practice in search and cluster information broadcasting is interrelated to professional reporting of news during bursts.
